This solution will show you how to analyze AWS Web Application Firewall (AWS WAF) logs and quickly build multiple dashboards, without booting up any servers. With the new AWS WAF full logs feature, you can log all traffic inspected by AWS WAF into Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) buckets by leveraging Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose. First, you need to enable AWS WAF logging for the given web ACL(s). You can then create an Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream into which the AWS WAF full logs are delivered. Then you need to set up an AWS Glue crawler job and an Amazon Athena table. Finally, you’ll set up Amazon QuickSight dashboards to read the logs data by querying the Athena tables and thereby help you visualize the web traffic traversing through the AWS WAF layer.
via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/enabling-serverless-security-analytics-using-aws-waf-full-logs/ Incorrect Options:
  • Configure AWS Web Application Firewall (AWS WAF) to send logs to CloudWatch Logs. Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to interactively search and analyze your log data. Publish logs data to Amazon QuickSight dashboards through direct CloudWatch integration with QuickSight — It is possible to send web ACL traffic logs to a CloudWatch Logs log group. However, CloudWatch and Amazon QuickSight cannot directly connect and will need Amazon Athena as part of the solution. Hence, this option is incorrect.
  • Configure AWS Web Application Firewall (AWS WAF) to feed logs to Amazon S3 bucket via Amazon Kinesis Data Streams. Set up an AWS Glue crawler job and an Amazon Athena table to query for required data and create visualizations using Amazon QuickSight dashboards — AWS WAF logs only support the following logging destinations - Amazon CloudWatch Logs, Amazon Simple Storage Service, and Amazon Kinesis Data Firehose. So this option is incorrect.
Configure AWS Web Application Firewall (AWS WAF) to send your web ACL traffic logs to the Amazon S3 bucket. Use Amazon Redshift Spectrum to query data directly from files on Amazon S3. Using the existing integration of RedShift Spectrum with Amazon QuickSight, generate visualizations to be used in manager dashboards - To use Redshift Spectrum, you need an Amazon Redshift cluster and a SQL client that's connected to your cluster, so that you can run SQL commands. Since the required solution should be serverless, this option is not the right fit. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/logging-destinations.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/quicksight/latest/user/supported-data-sources.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/dg/c-getting-started-using-spectrum.html

questions.question_text:
A media company stores all of its business data on Amazon S3 buckets. Since a massive growth in the number of customers has resulted in complicated bucket policies, the company has now hired you as an AWS Certified Security Specialist for simplifying the company's S3 buckets configuration to facilitate access for the company's customers as well as other connected applications.
What are the important configuration characteristics to consider while defining access points for the S3 buckets? (Select three)
options: The cross-account access points don’t grant access to data until you are granted permissions from the bucket owner
You can't configure Cross-Region Replication to operate through an access point

Aliases for S3 Access Points are interchangeable with S3 bucket names. Aliases can be used as a logging destination for AWS CloudTrail logs and S3 server access logs. However, an alias cannot be used in AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) policies
You can only use access points to perform operations on objects. You can't use access points to perform Amazon S3 operations, such as modifying or deleting buckets
After you create an access point, you can't change its virtual private cloud (VPC) configuration from the console anymore. An AWS CLI has to be used for modifying the configuration
Access points support access over HTTP and HTTPS alone. It does not offer support over TCP and UDP protocols
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • You can only use access points to perform operations on objects. You can't use access points to perform Amazon S3 operations, such as modifying or deleting buckets — Access points support access only over HTTPS.
  • The cross-account access points don’t grant access to data until you are granted permissions from the bucket owner — The cross-account access points don’t grant access to data until you are granted permissions from the bucket owner. The bucket owner always retains ultimate control of the data and must update the bucket policy to authorize requests from the cross-account access point.
  • You can't configure Cross-Region Replication to operate through an access point — You can't use an access point as a destination for S3 Replication.
Access points restrictions and limitations:
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/access-points-restrictions-limitations.html Access point alias limitations:
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/access-points-alias.html Incorrect Options:
  • Access points support access over HTTP and HTTPS alone. It does not offer support over TCP and UDP protocols — This statement is incorrect. Access points support access only over HTTPS.
  • Aliases for S3 Access Points are interchangeable with S3 bucket names. Aliases can be used as a logging destination for AWS CloudTrail logs and S3 server access logs. However, an alias cannot be used in AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) policies — Aliases for S3 Access Points are automatically generated and are interchangeable with S3 bucket names anywhere you use a bucket name for data access. Every time you create an access point for a bucket, S3 automatically generates a new Access Point Alias.
Aliases cannot be used as a logging destination for S3 server access logs. Aliases cannot be used as a logging destination for AWS CloudTrail logs.
  • After you create an access point, you can't change its virtual private cloud (VPC) configuration from the console anymore. An AWS CLI has to be used for modifying the configuration — After you create an access point, you can't change its virtual private cloud (VPC) configuration.
    • https://aws.amazon.com/s3/features/access-points/ https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/access-points-alias.html

questions.question_text:
A company uses AWS Organizations and has separated responsibilities across multiple AWS accounts. In the workload-launch AWS account, Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling launches instances by using a service-linked role. In the security-keyring AWS account, Amazon EBS volumes are encrypted by using a customer managed AWS KMS key. A security engineer must ensure that the Auto Scaling service-linked role in the workload-launch account can successfully launch Amazon EC2 instances that use the encrypted Amazon EBS volumes defined in the security-keyring account.
Which combination of actions should the security engineer take across the two accounts? (Select two)
options: Attach an IAM policy with KMS permissions to the IAM role that is attached to the Amazon EC2 instances after launch, and then allow the workload-launch account in the KMS key policy
Create a KMS grant for the Auto Scaling service-linked role so it can use the customer managed key to launch instances with encrypted volumes
Share the encrypted Amazon EBS snapshots with the workload-launch account, and allow the Auto Scaling service-linked role to use only ec2:RunInstances without any KMS-related access
Update the KMS key policy in the security-keyring account to allow the workload-launch account to use the customer managed KMS key

Attach an IAM policy to the Auto Scaling service-linked role in the workload-launch account that allows kms:CreateGrant, kms:DescribeKey, kms:Encrypt, kms:GenerateDataKey, kms:Decrypt, and kms:ReEncrypt* on the customer managed key
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Update the KMS key policy in the security-keyring account to allow the workload-launch account to use the customer managed KMS key
For a customer managed AWS KMS key in one AWS account to be used by a principal in another AWS account, the KMS key policy in the key-owning account must allow that external account to use the key. AWS documents that cross-account use of a KMS key requires the key policy to grant access to the external account or principal. Without that key policy entry, the Auto Scaling service-linked role in the workload-launch account cannot use the key, regardless of any permissions elsewhere. This is a foundational requirement for cross-account KMS access. Because the encrypted EBS volumes rely on a customer managed key that lives in the security-keyring account, the key policy must first establish trust for the external workload-launch account.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/key-policy-requirements-EBS-encryption.html
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/key-policy-requirements-EBS-encryption.html
  • Create a KMS grant for the Auto Scaling service-linked role so it can use the customer managed key to launch instances with encrypted volumes
    • Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling uses a service-linked role to launch instances, and AWS documents that when you use encrypted EBS volumes with a customer managed KMS key, you can authorize the Auto Scaling service-linked role by creating a KMS grant. This grant allows the service-linked role to use the key for the required encryption operations during instance launch. This is especially important in the cross-account case because the workload-launch account uses the service-linked role while the key exists in the security-keyring account. AWS specifically documents the pattern of allowing the external account in the key policy and then creating a grant for the Auto Scaling service-linked role. Together, these two options provide the correct cross-account KMS authorization model. The key policy enables cross-account trust, and the KMS grant authorizes the Auto Scaling service-linked role to use the key during instance launches. Incorrect Options:
    • Attach an IAM policy to the Auto Scaling service-linked role in the workload-launch account that allows kms:CreateGrant, kms:DescribeKey, kms:Encrypt, kms:GenerateDataKey, kms:Decrypt, and kms:ReEncrypt* on the customer managed key — This option sounds plausible because it lists the KMS actions commonly associated with encrypted volume workflows. However, in the AWS-documented Auto Scaling cross-account pattern, the direct required steps are to allow the external account in the key policy and create a KMS grant for the service-linked role. The service-linked role authorization is handled through the KMS grant rather than by relying only on an IAM policy attached to the role. Without the grant, the role still does not have the correct documented cross-account key usage path for this Auto Scaling scenario.
    • Attach an IAM policy with KMS permissions to the IAM role that is attached to the Amazon EC2 instances after launch, and then allow the workload-launch account in the KMS key policy — The IAM role attached to the EC2 instances after they launch is not the role that performs the encrypted launch operation. The actual launch is performed by Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling through its service-linked role. Because the requirement is to enable the service-linked role to launch instances with encrypted volumes, granting KMS permissions to the instance profile role does not solve the problem. It targets the wrong principal.
    • Share the encrypted Amazon EBS snapshots with the workload-launch account, and allow the Auto Scaling service-linked role to use only ec2:RunInstances without any KMS-related access — Sharing encrypted snapshots can be part of some cross-account image workflows, but it does not remove the need for KMS authorization when the underlying customer managed key is in another account. The Auto Scaling service-linked role still needs permission to use the key during instance launch. Granting only ec2:RunInstances is insufficient because encrypted EBS operations require access to the KMS key as well. This option ignores the key usage path that is central to the problem.
    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/key-policy-requirements-EBS-encryption.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policy-modifying-external-accounts.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/grants.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/autoscaling-service-linked-role.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ebs/latest/userguide/ebs-encryption.html

questions.question_text:
A junior developer has been asked to configure access to an Amazon EC2 instance hosting a web application. The developer has configured a new security group to permit incoming HTTP traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 and retained any default outbound rules. A custom Network Access Control List (NACL) connected with the instance's subnet is configured to permit incoming HTTP traffic from 0.0.0.0/0 and retained any default outbound rules.
As a Security Engineer, which of the following solutions would you suggest if the EC2 instance needs to accept and respond to requests from the internet?
options: Outbound rules need to be configured both on the security group and on the NACL for sending responses to the Internet Gateway
An outbound rule must be added to the Network ACL (NACL) to allow the response to be sent to the client on the ephemeral port range
The configuration is complete on the EC2 instance for accepting and responding to requests
An outbound rule on the security group has to be configured, to allow the response to be sent to the client on the HTTP port
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • An outbound rule must be added to the Network ACL (NACL) to allow the response to be sent to the client on the ephemeral port range — Security groups are stateful, so allowing inbound traffic to the necessary ports enables the connection. Network ACLs are stateless, so you must allow both inbound and outbound traffic. By default, each custom Network ACL denies all inbound and outbound traffic until you add rules.
To enable the connection to a service running on an instance, the associated network ACL must allow both: 1. Inbound traffic on the port that the service is listening on 2. Outbound traffic to ephemeral ports When a client connects to a server, a random port from the ephemeral port range (1024-65535) becomes the client's source port. The designated ephemeral port becomes the destination port for return traffic from the service. Outbound traffic to the ephemeral port must be allowed in the network ACL. Network ACL basics:
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network-acls.html Incorrect Options:
  • The configuration is complete on the EC2 instance for accepting and responding to requests — As explained above, this is an incorrect statement.
  • An outbound rule on the security group has to be configured, to allow the response to be sent to the client on the HTTP port — Security groups are stateful. Therefore you don't need a rule that allows responses to inbound traffic.
  • Outbound rules need to be configured both on the security group and on the NACL for sending responses to the Internet Gateway — * - Security Groups are stateful. Hence, return traffic is automatically allowed, so there is no need to configure an outbound rule on the security group.
https://aws.amazon.com/premiumsupport/knowledge-center/resolve-connection-sg-acl-inbound/ https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network-acls.html#nacl-ephemeral-ports

questions.question_text:
A company deploys two EC2 instances in a public subnet that must be able to initiate outbound TLS connections to external HTTPS services on port 443. Network security policy requires that inbound access on port 3306 (MySQL) be explicitly denied to prevent database connections from untrusted sources.
Which network ACL configuration correctly implements these requirements?
options: Inbound rule 100 denies port 3306, inbound rule 200 allows port 443, outbound rule 100 allows port 443 for encrypted traffic
Inbound rule 100 allows port 443 and port 3306, inbound rule 200 denies all traffic, outbound rule 100 allows port 443
Inbound rule 100 denies all traffic, inbound rule 200 allows port 443, outbound rule 100 allows port 443 and port 3306
Inbound rule 100 denies port 3306, inbound rule 200 allows ephemeral ports 1024-65535 for return traffic, outbound rule 100 allows port 443
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Inbound rule 100 denies port 3306, inbound rule 200 allows ephemeral ports 1024-65535 for return traffic, outbound rule 100 allows port 443
This configuration correctly implements stateless filtering using network ACL rules with appropriate rule numbers and port ranges. Inbound rule 100 with DENY on port 3306 prevents incoming database connections as required, taking effect before any ALLOW rules with higher numbers. Inbound rule 200 ALLOW on ephemeral ports (1024-65535) permits return traffic from outbound TLS connections, which is essential for stateless firewalls that must explicitly allow responses to initiated connections. Outbound rule 100 allows port 443 traffic, enabling the EC2 instances to initiate HTTPS connections to external services. The stateless nature of network ACLs means that responses to these outbound requests must be explicitly allowed in the inbound rules through the ephemeral port range, which this configuration correctly addresses. Rule ordering is critical because network ACLs process rules sequentially from lowest to highest rule number, and the first matching rule determines the action. This configuration ensures that port 3306 is denied first, avoiding any potential confusion with broader allow rules. Incorrect Options:
  • Inbound rule 100 allows port 443 and port 3306, inbound rule 200 denies all traffic, outbound rule 100 allows port 443 — This configuration unnecessarily allows inbound traffic on both port 443 and port 3306, which directly violates the security requirement to deny port 3306 access. The logic of allowing a service port (3306) that should be explicitly denied contradicts the stated security policy. The second rule denying all traffic would block return traffic on ephemeral ports for outbound connections.
  • Inbound rule 100 denies all traffic, inbound rule 200 allows port 443, outbound rule 100 allows port 443 and port 3306 — Rule 100 denying all traffic would block all inbound packets, preventing ephemeral port return traffic from reaching the instances and failing the requirement to support outbound HTTPS connections. This overly restrictive initial rule would break all inbound network communication. Subsequent rules would be unable to override the deny-all policy effectively, making this configuration operationally non-functional for the stated requirements.
  • Inbound rule 100 denies port 3306, inbound rule 200 allows port 443, outbound rule 100 allows port 443 for encrypted traffic — Inbound rule 200 allowing port 443 is unnecessary and incorrect because the instances need to initiate outbound connections to port 443, not receive inbound connections on that port. This rule misunderstands the traffic flow requirements. Additionally, without explicit inbound ephemeral port allowances, responses to outbound HTTPS connections would be blocked, causing connection failures despite the misleading port 443 allow rule.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/security.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network-acls.html

questions.question_text:
A large enterprise uses AWS Organizations to manage dozens of AWS accounts that are grouped into multiple organizational units. The company stores business data in Amazon S3 buckets across these accounts. Teams in different OUs already access the buckets through existing permissions, and that access must continue to work without modification.
The security team needs to ensure that IAM principals that do not belong to the same AWS organization cannot access any of the company’s Amazon S3 buckets. The team wants to enforce this requirement at the organization level without changing the current access model for internal accounts and OUs.
Which solution should the security team implement?
options: Deploy a service control policy that uses the aws:ResourceOrgID condition with ${aws:PrincipalOrgID} so that only principals from the same AWS organization can access the buckets
Configure an Amazon S3 bucket policy on each bucket that denies requests unless the aws:userid value matches an approved list of internal IAM principals
Create an AWS Config rule that detects access from principals outside the organization and automatically removes the associated bucket permissions
Deploy a service control policy that compares aws:ResourceOrgPaths to ${aws:PrincipalOrgPaths} so that only principals from matching organizational paths can access the given buckets
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Deploy a service control policy that uses the aws:ResourceOrgID condition with ${aws:PrincipalOrgID} so that only principals from the same AWS organization can access the buckets
Using a service control policy with the aws:ResourceOrgID and ${aws:PrincipalOrgID} condition enforces an organization-wide boundary based on AWS Organizations membership. This approach allows access only when the principal and the target resource belong to the same organization. Because the condition evaluates organization identity instead of OU-specific paths, internal access across multiple OUs can continue without requiring the company to redesign existing bucket permissions. This solution works well when the requirement is to block principals from outside the organization while preserving current access for accounts that are already part of the organization. The policy acts as a guardrail at the AWS Organizations layer and prevents external principals from accessing S3 resources, even if a bucket policy or identity-based policy might otherwise allow the request. Internal principals remain unaffected as long as they belong to the same organization and have the necessary IAM and bucket permissions. The key advantage is that this model enforces a broad organization-level restriction without introducing OU-to-OU segmentation. A condition based on organization paths would be too restrictive because it would require the principal and resource to align on specific paths. By using organization ID instead, the company satisfies the requirement to block external principals while preserving access patterns among its existing organizational units.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/tutorial_cross-account-with-roles.html Incorrect Options:
  • Configure an Amazon S3 bucket policy on each bucket that denies requests unless the aws:userid value matches an approved list of internal IAM principals — Using individual bucket policies with explicit aws:userid allowlists would require the company to maintain principal-specific mappings for every bucket. That approach does not scale well across many accounts and OUs, especially in a large organization with evolving IAM roles and principals.
This design also shifts enforcement to each bucket instead of applying a centralized organization-level guardrail. The requirement asks for a solution that prevents external principals while preserving internal access. A principal allowlist is operationally complex and brittle, and it does not take advantage of AWS Organizations context keys that are designed for this exact use case.
  • Create an AWS Config rule that detects access from principals outside the organization and automatically removes the associated bucket permissions — AWS Config can detect configuration drift and noncompliant resource states, and remediation actions can help correct issues after detection. However, Config is not a preventative access-control mechanism for real-time authorization decisions against Amazon S3.
    • An external principal could potentially access a bucket before the Config rule evaluates the configuration and triggers remediation. The requirement is to prevent access from outside the organization, not to detect it after the fact and attempt cleanup. Because this option does not provide immediate policy enforcement during authorization, it does not satisfy the requirement.
  • Deploy a service control policy that compares aws:ResourceOrgPaths to ${aws:PrincipalOrgPaths} so that only principals from matching organizational paths can access the given buckets — A service control policy that compares aws:ResourceOrgPaths with ${aws:PrincipalOrgPaths} evaluates whether the principal and the resource belong to matching organizational paths. That condition is more restrictive than the requirement because it can limit access between different OUs inside the same organization.
    • The scenario states that the solution must not affect existing access that the OUs already have to the S3 buckets. If internal access currently occurs across multiple OUs, a path-based comparison can block that access when the paths do not match exactly. As a result, this option introduces unnecessary OU-level restrictions and does not preserve the current internal access model. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_condition-keys.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scps.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/evaluate-config.html

questions.question_text:
A large enterprise runs workloads across dozens of AWS accounts managed through AWS Organizations. Company governance policy mandates that all AWS resources carry an Environment tag with one of four approved values: Development, Testing, Staging, or Production. The company must enforce this tagging requirement at resource creation time and prevent any user from updating the Environment tag to a non-approved value after creation.
Which solution will meet these requirements?
options: Deploy an AWS Service Catalog portfolio and populate it with pre-approved AWS CloudFormation templates. Require all teams to deploy resources only through Service Catalog and embed the Environment tag enforcement in the CloudFormation stack parameter constraints for each product

Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations. Create a tag policy that specifies the Environment tag key with the four approved values and configure the policy to enforce noncompliant operations. Create an SCP that denies resource creation when the aws:RequestTag/Environment condition key is null, ensuring every new resource carries the required tag at creation time

Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations. Create a tag policy that specifies the Environment tag key with the four approved values. Configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to invoke an AWS Lambda function when a noncompliant tag value is detected and program the Lambda function to overwrite the tag with the nearest approved value

Create an AWS Config Custom Policy rule by using AWS CloudFormation Guard. Define the rule to check that all newly created resources include an Environment tag key with one of the four approved values. Deploy the Config rule to every member account using a CloudFormation StackSet. Create an SCP that denies resource creation when the value of the aws:RequestTag/Environment condition key does not match any of the four approved values in the policy
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations. Create a tag policy that specifies the Environment tag key with the four approved values and configure the policy to enforce noncompliant operations. Create an SCP that denies resource creation when the aws:RequestTag/Environment condition key is null, ensuring every new resource carries the required tag at creation time
AWS Organizations tag policies let you standardize tags across the resources in your organization's accounts. A tag policy defines the tag keys that resources must carry, specifies the allowed values for those keys, and optionally enforces compliance by preventing noncompliant operations. When you configure a tag policy to enforce noncompliant operations, any attempt to create or update a resource with a tag value not listed in the policy fails automatically. This ensures the Environment tag can only be set to one of the four approved values. Tag policies alone do not prevent the creation of resources that lack the Environment tag entirely. A resource could be created without any tags and remain non-compliant. The SCP component closes this gap by denying resource creation requests when the aws:RequestTag/Environment condition key is null. This condition key evaluates to null when the requester does not include the Environment tag in the create API call. Combining the tag policy with the SCP creates a complete preventive control: every new resource must carry the Environment tag (enforced by the SCP) and that tag must have an approved value (enforced by the tag policy). This solution is entirely preventive and operates at the policy evaluation layer without requiring event-driven Lambda functions, periodic detection scans, or manual remediation workflows. Both controls apply across all member accounts through the Organizations management hierarchy, making the solution inherently scalable to any number of accounts. The combination of tag policies and SCPs is the AWS-recommended pattern for enforcing mandatory tagging standards at organization scale.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies.html Incorrect Options:
  • Create an AWS Config Custom Policy rule by using AWS CloudFormation Guard. Define the rule to check that all newly created resources include an Environment tag key with one of the four approved values. Deploy the Config rule to every member account using a CloudFormation StackSet. Create an SCP that denies resource creation when the value of the aws:RequestTag/Environment condition key does not match any of the four approved values in the policy — AWS Config rules are detective controls. They evaluate resources after creation and report non-compliance, but they cannot prevent the creation of a non-compliant resource in real time. A Config rule detects that a resource was created with an incorrect tag value and flags it as non-compliant, but the resource already exists at that point. Even with the SCP component, this option has a gap: the SCP only denies creation when the tag value is non-compliant but does not deny creation when the tag is entirely absent. A user could create a resource without any Environment tag and bypass the SCP entirely. Deploying Config rules to dozens of accounts via StackSets also adds operational overhead that a centralized tag policy avoids.
  • Deploy an AWS Service Catalog portfolio and populate it with pre-approved AWS CloudFormation templates. Require all teams to deploy resources only through Service Catalog and embed the Environment tag enforcement in the CloudFormation stack parameter constraints for each product — AWS Service Catalog with pre-approved templates can enforce tagging standards for resources deployed through the catalog. However, this approach only controls resources launched through Service Catalog. Engineers with direct AWS API access, AWS CLI access, or the ability to deploy CloudFormation stacks outside Service Catalog can create resources without any tag enforcement. Unless all direct API access is blocked by an SCP, this solution has significant gaps.
Maintaining a catalog of CloudFormation templates for every resource type the organization uses is also operationally intensive and requires continuous updates as new services and resource types are adopted. A centralized tag policy is far less maintenance-intensive.
  • Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations. Create a tag policy that specifies the Environment tag key with the four approved values. Configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to invoke an AWS Lambda function when a noncompliant tag value is detected and program the Lambda function to overwrite the tag with the nearest approved value — Using tag policies to enforce approved values is correct, but replacing the SCP preventive control with a reactive EventBridge-Lambda remediation mechanism introduces a window of non-compliance between resource creation and remediation. Overwriting a tag with the nearest approved value after creation is ambiguous and error-prone because "nearest" is not a well-defined concept for arbitrary tag values. This option also does not prevent the creation of resources without any Environment tag.
    • The requirements state that the company must prevent non-approved values, not detect and correct them after the fact. A preventive policy control is required. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scps.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_condition-keys.html#condition-keys-requesttag https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/operational-best-practices-for-tagging.html

questions.question_text:
A multinational corporation uses AWS Organizations and AWS IAM Identity Center (AWS Single Sign-On) to manage access across 22 member accounts. A security architect is authoring a permission set in IAM Identity Center that will be deployed to users across all 22 accounts. The permission set includes both an AWS managed policy and a customer-created policy. The architect holds full administrative privileges and is operating within the management account. When attempting to assign the permission set to an IAM Identity Center user who holds user access to multiple member accounts, the assignment operation fails.
What action should the architect take to resolve this failure?
options: Create the customer-managed policy in every member account that will receive the permission set, while ensuring that the policy name and permissions are identical in each account
Abandon the permission set modification and instead edit the user's existing permission set by adding the AWS managed policy and the customer-managed policy directly to the existing role

Create a service role in the management account that contains both policies, link the permission set to the service role through a cross-account trust relationship configured across all 22 member accounts, and delegate credential assumption to IAM Identity Center
Verify that the AWS managed policy and the customer-managed policy do not contain conflicting permissions, and reconcile any overlapping statements before redeploying the permission set
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Create the customer-managed policy in every member account that will receive the permission set, while ensuring that the policy name and permissions are identical in each account
IAM Identity Center permission sets that include customer-managed policies require that the customer-managed policy be present in every account where the permission set is assigned. Customer-managed policies are account-scoped resources; they do not replicate automatically across accounts like AWS managed policies do. When IAM Identity Center attempts to assign the permission set to a user in a member account, it references the customer-managed policy by name, but if that policy does not exist in the target account, the assignment fails. The resolution is to create the customer-managed policy in all 22 member accounts with the identical name and identical permissions. Once the policy exists in each account, IAM Identity Center can successfully reference it from any member account, and the permission set assignment completes. The AWS managed policy, in contrast, is already available in all accounts and requires no additional provisioning. This approach ensures that the permission set behaves consistently across all member accounts and that users receive the intended permissions regardless of which account they access through IAM Identity Center. Incorrect Options:
  • Verify that the AWS managed policy and the customer-managed policy do not contain conflicting permissions, and reconcile any overlapping statements before redeploying the permission set — Verifying for conflicting policies is a good general practice, but conflicting policy logic is not the cause of assignment failure in this scenario. IAM Identity Center fails the assignment because it cannot locate the customer-managed policy in the member account, not because of policy conflicts. Reconciling permissions does not resolve the missing policy problem.
  • Abandon the permission set modification and instead edit the user's existing permission set by adding the AWS managed policy and the customer-managed policy directly to the existing role — Editing the user's existing permission set does not address the organizational requirement to deploy the permission set across 22 accounts. Additionally, this approach is not scalable because it requires modifying each user individually rather than leveraging a centralized permission set that can be reused across multiple users.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/what-is.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/permissionsets.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies_create-console.html

questions.question_text:
A biotechnology company deploys a data pipeline application on Amazon Elastic Container Service (ECS) that processes patient medical records. During a recent security assessment, the team discovered that PostgreSQL database credentials were embedded directly in the application's Docker image and stored in the company's private container registry. A security engineer must establish a solution that secures database credentials, enables automatic credential rotation, restricts credential access to only the ECS task containers that require it, and prevents database administrators from sharing plaintext passwords with other team members. The solution should introduce minimal operational complexity.
Which option best meets these requirements?
options: Embed encrypted credentials in the Docker image using KMS encryption at build time, store the KMS key in AWS Secrets Manager with access restricted to ECS task roles, decrypt credentials at container runtime using a sidecar container that fetches the KMS key from Secrets Manager, and rotate the KMS key quarterly through AWS Systems Manager Automation

Place database credentials in AWS Secrets Manager, use inline IAM policies on ECS task roles to limit credential retrieval to specific containers, and establish manual credential rotation by extracting credentials through AWS CLI commands

Store database credentials in AWS Secrets Manager, configure IAM roles attached to ECS task definitions to grant permission to retrieve credentials from Secrets Manager, and enable automatic rotation of database credentials through Secrets Manager rotation Lambda functions

Store database credentials in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store, associate IAM roles with ECS task definitions to grant permission to retrieve stored credentials, and manage rotation through manual AWS CLI exports on a quarterly schedule
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Store database credentials in AWS Secrets Manager, configure IAM roles attached to ECS task definitions to grant permission to retrieve credentials from Secrets Manager, and enable automatic rotation of database credentials through Secrets Manager rotation Lambda functions
AWS Secrets Manager is purpose-built for storing, managing, and rotating secrets like database credentials. Unlike the parameter store, Secrets Manager integrates with AWS Lambda to automate credential rotation without requiring manual operator intervention. When a credential is stored in Secrets Manager, the owner provides a Lambda function that knows how to rotate the credential against the target database; Secrets Manager invokes that function automatically on a schedule the administrator sets, such as every 30 days. ECS task definitions can include an IAM role that grants the necessary permissions to retrieve a specific secret from Secrets Manager. This permission model ensures that only the ECS tasks explicitly granted this role can decrypt and retrieve the database credential. Other containers or processes without the role are denied access, preventing inadvertent exposure. Database administrators never handle plaintext credentials in email or Slack because the rotation Lambda function updates the secret directly in Secrets Manager and in the database engine simultaneously. The task role approach eliminates the need for instance profiles or other workarounds because ECS natively supports IAM roles at the task level. This gives fine-grained access control and follows AWS best practices for least privilege. The solution introduces minimal operational overhead because once the rotation Lambda is configured, credential management happens automatically without human intervention. Incorrect Options:
  • Embed encrypted credentials in the Docker image using KMS encryption at build time, store the KMS key in AWS Secrets Manager with access restricted to ECS task roles, decrypt credentials at container runtime using a sidecar container that fetches the KMS key from Secrets Manager, and rotate the KMS key quarterly through AWS Systems Manager Automation — Running a dedicated ECS service as a credential distribution layer introduces unnecessary architectural complexity and additional operational overhead. The custom credential store on EBS volumes must be backed up, encrypted, and monitored. A single point of failure in the credential distribution service could disrupt the entire data pipeline. This approach also requires custom Lambda logic for rotation, increasing development effort compared to using Secrets Manager's built-in rotation framework.
  • Place database credentials in AWS Secrets Manager, use inline IAM policies on ECS task roles to limit credential retrieval to specific containers, and establish manual credential rotation by extracting credentials through AWS CLI commands — AWS Secrets Manager is the correct service choice, but inline IAM policies are less maintainable than task roles. Inline policies are embedded directly in the role and cannot be reused or updated across multiple task definitions efficiently. More importantly, this option specifies manual credential rotation via AWS CLI commands, which defeats the purpose of using Secrets Manager's automated rotation feature and introduces operational complexity and human error risk.
  • Store database credentials in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store, associate IAM roles with ECS task definitions to grant permission to retrieve stored credentials, and manage rotation through manual AWS CLI exports on a quarterly schedule — AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store does not support automated credential rotation natively. The quarterly manual rotation schedule leaves a 90-day window during which an old credential remains active, violating the principle of short credential lifetime. Manual rotation also introduces human error and requires operators to handle plaintext credentials, increasing the risk of accidental exposure or sharing.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager/latest/userguide/intro.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager/latest/userguide/rotating-secrets.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task-iam-roles.html

questions.question_text:
A company hosts a public website behind an Application Load Balancer that routes requests to a fleet of stateless Amazon EC2 instances. The application reads data from an Amazon DynamoDB table. The company wants to reduce the impact of malicious scanning activity and application-layer denial-of-service attempts. The company needs to ensure that each client IP address can retrieve data no more than 15 times during any 10-minute period. The solution must require the least implementation effort.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
options: Configure an Amazon CloudFront distribution in front of the Application Load Balancer, enable access logging, and use an AWS Lambda@Edge function to reject requests after a client IP address exceeds 15 requests in 10 minutes
Configure AWS WAF for the Application Load Balancer and create a rate-based rule that blocks requests from any source IP address that exceeds 15 requests in a 10-minute evaluation window
Enable AWS Shield Advanced on the Application Load Balancer and configure proactive engagement so the service automatically enforces a limit of 15 requests per IP address every 10 minutes
Store source IP addresses and request timestamps in the Amazon DynamoDB table, apply a 10-minute TTL to each entry, and limit the table throughput to 15 read operations
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Configure AWS WAF for the Application Load Balancer and create a rate-based rule that blocks requests from any source IP address that exceeds 15 requests in a 10-minute evaluation window
AWS WAF is designed to protect web applications that are fronted by resources such as an Application Load Balancer. AWS documents that AWS WAF supports rate-based rules, which count requests from each source and apply a block or other action when the configured request rate is exceeded during the evaluation window. This directly meets the requirement because the company wants to restrict each client IP address to only 15 reads in any 10-minute period. Since the website is already behind an ALB, attaching AWS WAF to the ALB requires little architectural change and avoids modifying the application code or the data model. This is also the least-effort solution because it uses a native managed control at the application edge. The rate limit can be enforced before requests reach the EC2 instances or DynamoDB-backed application logic, which reduces both operational complexity and application load.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-rule-statement-type-rate-based-high-level-settings.html Incorrect Options:
  • Configure an Amazon CloudFront distribution in front of the Application Load Balancer, enable access logging, and use an AWS Lambda@Edge function to reject requests after a client IP address exceeds 15 requests in 10 minutes — This design could be made to work, but it adds more components and more operational complexity than necessary. The company would need to introduce CloudFront and Lambda@Edge even though the ALB can already be protected directly by AWS WAF. Because AWS WAF can attach to an Application Load Balancer and enforce rate-based rules natively, this CloudFront-based approach is not the least-effort solution. It is a plausible architecture, but it is more elaborate than required.
  • Enable AWS Shield Advanced on the Application Load Balancer and configure proactive engagement so the service automatically enforces a limit of 15 requests per IP address every 10 minutes — AWS Shield Advanced provides additional protection against distributed denial-of-service events, but it is not the service used to implement a precise per-IP application-layer request limit like 15 requests per 10 minutes. The requirement is specifically about enforcing a client IP request threshold. AWS WAF rate-based rules are the native mechanism for that use case, whereas Shield Advanced focuses on broader DDoS protection.
  • Store source IP addresses and request timestamps in the Amazon DynamoDB table, apply a 10-minute TTL to each entry, and limit the table throughput to 15 read operations — Using DynamoDB to store request timestamps and source IP addresses introduces unnecessary complexity and does not correctly enforce a per-IP request limit by itself. Reducing DynamoDB read capacity to 15 would affect the application globally rather than restricting each client IP address independently. This option also pushes rate-limiting logic into the data layer, which is not the right enforcement point for a public web application. It increases operational overhead and risks disrupting legitimate application behavior.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/how-aws-waf-works.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-rule-statement-type-rate-based-high-level-settings.html

questions.question_text:
A security operations team maintains all AWS KMS customer managed keys for an organization. Only security team members can administer these keys directly. A data engineering team has a batch processing workflow that requires occasional short-term cryptographic access to specific keys. The security team must provide this access while retaining full control over the access lifecycle.
Which solution meets these requirements with the least operational overhead?
options: Generate new KMS key material outside of AWS, import it into a new customer managed key whenever the data engineering team requests access, then delete the key when the access window closes
Create a KMS key grant that allows the data engineering team's batch process to perform the required cryptographic actions, then revoke the grant when the team no longer needs access

Export the KMS key material to an on-premises hardware security module and provide the data engineering team with credentials to access the HSM so the team can perform the required cryptographic operations independently

Store the KMS key ARN and alias in an AWS Secrets Manager secret, grant the data engineering team IAM permission to retrieve the secret, and delete the secret from Secrets Manager when the team no longer needs to reference the key during batch operations
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Create a KMS key grant that allows the data engineering team's batch process to perform the required cryptographic actions, then revoke the grant when the team no longer needs access
AWS KMS key grants provide a built-in delegation mechanism that is specifically designed for temporary or programmatic access without requiring key policy modifications. A grant specifies the grantee principal, the permitted cryptographic actions such as kms:Decrypt and kms:GenerateDataKey, and optional constraints such as an encryption context. Once created, the grant takes effect immediately and allows the grantee to perform the specified operations without changes to the key policy or the grantee's IAM policies. Grants are operationally efficient because both creation and revocation require a single API call each. Revoking a grant removes the grantee's access immediately and completely. Because grants are separate from the key policy, the security team can manage temporary access through the grant API without touching the key policy document that governs permanent administrative permissions. The entire temporary access lifecycle is managed independently from the key policy. The grant-based approach also provides a cleaner audit trail through CloudTrail, where kms:CreateGrant and kms:RevokeGrant events record who created and revoked each grant. This separation of temporary delegation from permanent policy management is the principal reason AWS KMS grants exist.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/grants.html Incorrect Options:
  • Export the KMS key material to an on-premises hardware security module and provide the data engineering team with credentials to access the HSM so the team can perform the required cryptographic operations independently — Exporting KMS key material to an on-premises HSM removes the security boundary that AWS KMS provides. Key material exported from a standard AWS KMS customer managed key cannot be used in a compliant manner outside the KMS service because AWS KMS does not allow key material export from keys where the material was generated internally. Exporting key material changes the trust model and requires managing a separate HSM with its own access controls, logging, and operational procedures. This approach significantly increases operational complexity while reducing the security controls available through AWS KMS.
  • Store the KMS key ARN and alias in an AWS Secrets Manager secret, grant the data engineering team IAM permission to retrieve the secret, and delete the secret from Secrets Manager when the team no longer needs to reference the key during batch operations — Storing the KMS key ARN or alias in Secrets Manager provides no cryptographic access to the key. The key ARN is a non-secret identifier visible in IAM policies, the AWS console, and CloudTrail logs. Placing it in Secrets Manager does not grant or restrict KMS permissions in any way. The data engineering team would still need explicit IAM permissions allowing the required KMS actions, so Secrets Manager adds operational overhead without addressing the access control requirement. Deleting the Secrets Manager secret removes access only to the ARN string, not the underlying KMS permissions.
  • Generate new KMS key material outside of AWS, import it into a new customer managed key whenever the data engineering team requests access, then delete the key when the access window closes — Importing key material into a new KMS key for each access cycle requires generating high-quality key material outside of AWS in a FIPS-compliant environment, wrapping the material correctly for the KMS import API, and securely managing the raw key material storage between cycles. Deleting a KMS key initiates a mandatory minimum 7-day waiting period before permanent deletion, making this approach unsuitable for scenarios requiring frequent or short-duration access windows. Additionally, data encrypted with one imported key instance cannot be decrypted after the key is deleted, creating long-term data accessibility risks.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/APIReference/API_CreateGrant.html https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/managing-permissions-with-grants-in-aws-key-management-service/

questions.question_text:
A company runs a removable fleet of Amazon EC2 instances for an application. Each instance in the fleet can be taken out of service without affecting application availability. All instances currently share the same security group named ProdFleet. Amazon GuardDuty and AWS Config are enabled in the AWS account. A security engineer must automatically stop any EC2 instance from sending outbound traffic when Amazon GuardDuty produces a cryptocurrency-related finding for that instance. The engineer creates a new security group named isolate that has no outbound rules. The engineer also creates an AWS Lambda function that removes an affected EC2 instance from the ProdFleet security group and adds the instance to the isolate security group.
What additional step should the security engineer implement to complete this solution?
options: Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes the AWS Lambda function whenever Amazon GuardDuty generates a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* finding
Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes the AWS Lambda function whenever AWS Config detects a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* configuration change event for an EC2 instance
Create an AWS Systems Manager Automation runbook that checks AWS Config snapshots for CryptoCurrency:EC2/* findings and then updates the EC2 instance security groups when a matching snapshot is found
Configure Amazon GuardDuty to invoke the AWS Lambda function directly whenever GuardDuty generates a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* finding
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes the AWS Lambda function whenever Amazon GuardDuty generates a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* finding
Amazon GuardDuty findings are emitted to Amazon EventBridge, and AWS documents using EventBridge rules to route GuardDuty findings to downstream targets such as AWS Lambda for automated response. That makes EventBridge the native trigger mechanism for invoking the Lambda function when a specific finding type such as CryptoCurrency:EC2/* occurs. This fits the requirement because the security engineer already built the remediation function and the isolate security group. What is still needed is the event-driven linkage between the GuardDuty finding and the Lambda function. An EventBridge rule can filter on the GuardDuty finding type and invoke the function immediately when the cryptocurrency finding is generated. This solution also has low operational overhead and uses the intended AWS integration path. GuardDuty produces the detection, EventBridge routes the matching event, and Lambda performs the containment by changing the instance security group association. Incorrect Options:
  • Configure Amazon GuardDuty to invoke the AWS Lambda function directly whenever GuardDuty generates a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* finding — Amazon GuardDuty does not directly invoke AWS Lambda functions as a target integration for findings. The standard event-driven pattern is for GuardDuty to emit findings to Amazon EventBridge, and then for EventBridge to invoke Lambda or another target. This option is close in spirit, but it skips the required routing service. Because GuardDuty is not configured to call Lambda directly for finding events in this workflow, the option is incorrect.
  • Create an AWS Systems Manager Automation runbook that checks AWS Config snapshots for CryptoCurrency:EC2/* findings and then updates the EC2 instance security groups when a matching snapshot is found — AWS Systems Manager Automation can be useful for running remediation workflows, but the trigger logic described here is incorrect. AWS Config snapshots do not contain GuardDuty finding events as the signal to drive remediation. This design is also heavier than necessary. The engineer already has a Lambda function that performs the required isolation action, so the missing component is the proper event source and routing path from GuardDuty, which is EventBridge.
  • Create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes the AWS Lambda function whenever AWS Config detects a CryptoCurrency:EC2/* configuration change event for an EC2 instance — This option correctly uses EventBridge as an event-routing mechanism, which makes it look plausible. However, it still uses the wrong event source because AWS Config does not detect GuardDuty cryptocurrency finding types as configuration change events. The finding the engineer cares about is generated by GuardDuty. Therefore, the EventBridge rule must match GuardDuty findings, not AWS Config events.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_findings_eventbridge.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eventbridge/latest/ref/events-ref-guardduty.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_finding-types-ec2.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/findings_management.html

questions.question_text:
A retail e-commerce company runs a customer-facing application on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. The application processes orders using Amazon Simple Queue Service (Amazon SQS) queues and stores product assets in Amazon S3. The application scales with AWS Auto Scaling. The company's PCI compliance policy mandates least privilege access and requires that all communication between the application and AWS services use private network connectivity. A security engineer must implement private connectivity to the AWS services the application uses.
Which combination of steps should the security engineer take to meet this requirement? (Select three)
options: Update the IAM instance profile role attached to the EC2 instances in the Auto Scaling group to add permissions that allow outbound network connections to the VPC interface endpoint IP addresses
Route Amazon S3 API traffic through AWS Firewall Manager with a managed security policy that centralizes private connectivity enforcement for all S3 endpoint traffic
Update the resource policies on all created VPC endpoints to restrict access to only the specific SQS queues and S3 buckets that the application requires

Configure AWS PrivateLink for Amazon S3 by creating a VPC endpoint service in the application VPC, register the resulting endpoint network interfaces with Amazon Route 53 Resolver forwarding rules so that S3 DNS queries resolve to private IP addresses within the VPC CIDR range, and update security group inbound rules on all EC2 instances to allow responses from the endpoint network interface IP addresses
Create a gateway VPC endpoint for Amazon S3 to allow the EC2 instances to reach Amazon S3 without internet connectivity
Create an interface VPC endpoint for Amazon SQS to route SQS API traffic from the EC2 instances through the AWS private network without traversing the internet
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Create an interface VPC endpoint for Amazon SQS to route SQS API traffic from the EC2 instances through the AWS private network without traversing the internet
Creating an interface VPC endpoint for SQS deploys elastic network interfaces with private IP addresses in the designated subnets. EC2 instances in the VPC send SQS API requests to these private IP addresses, and the traffic remains within the AWS network without traversing the internet. This directly satisfies the private connectivity requirement for SQS.
  • Create a gateway VPC endpoint for Amazon S3 to allow the EC2 instances to reach Amazon S3 without internet connectivity
    • Amazon S3 supports a gateway VPC endpoint, which is a route table entry rather than a network interface. Creating a gateway VPC endpoint for S3 adds prefix list entries to the route tables associated with the EC2 instance subnets so that S3 API traffic is routed directly to the S3 endpoint within the AWS network. No NAT gateway or internet gateway is needed for S3 access. Gateway endpoints for S3 are available at no additional cost and are the recommended approach for private S3 connectivity from a VPC.
  • Update the resource policies on all created VPC endpoints to restrict access to only the specific SQS queues and S3 buckets that the application requires
    • VPC endpoint policies control which principals can access which resources through the endpoint. By default, endpoint policies allow all traffic. Updating the endpoint policies to specify only the SQS queues and S3 buckets that the application uses restricts the endpoint to least privilege access and satisfies the PCI compliance requirement that all existing AWS resources use least privilege access. This prevents the endpoints from being used to access other AWS resources outside the application's defined scope. Incorrect Options:
    • Configure AWS PrivateLink for Amazon S3 by creating a VPC endpoint service in the application VPC, register the resulting endpoint network interfaces with Amazon Route 53 Resolver forwarding rules so that S3 DNS queries resolve to private IP addresses within the VPC CIDR range, and update security group inbound rules on all EC2 instances to allow responses from the endpoint network interface IP addresses — AWS PrivateLink for Amazon S3 through a custom endpoint service and Route 53 Resolver forwarding rules describes a complex custom architecture that does not reflect how VPC endpoints for S3 work. Amazon S3 gateway VPC endpoints are a built-in AWS feature that does not require creating a custom endpoint service, configuring PrivateLink, or setting up DNS resolver forwarding rules. The standard approach of creating a gateway endpoint and updating route tables achieves the same private connectivity outcome with far less configuration complexity.
    The architecture described would be relevant for exposing a custom service hosted in one VPC to consumers in another VPC via PrivateLink, which is a different use case from providing private access to Amazon S3. Applying this pattern to S3 introduces unnecessary complexity and potential points of failure.
  • Update the IAM instance profile role attached to the EC2 instances in the Auto Scaling group to add permissions that allow outbound network connections to the VPC interface endpoint IP addresses — IAM role permissions govern what API actions a principal is authorized to perform, not which network paths or endpoints are permitted for outbound traffic. Adding permissions to an IAM role does not affect network routing or connectivity to VPC endpoint IP addresses. Network connectivity to interface endpoints is controlled by subnet route tables, security groups, and endpoint policies, not by IAM instance profile permissions.
    • The IAM instance profile role already holds the appropriate permissions to call SQS and S3 APIs per the requirement that least privilege has been applied to all existing resources. Creating the VPC endpoints and ensuring the security groups allow HTTPS traffic to the endpoint IP addresses establishes the network connectivity without any changes to the IAM role.
  • Route Amazon S3 API traffic through AWS Firewall Manager with a managed security policy that centralizes private connectivity enforcement for all S3 endpoint traffic — AWS Firewall Manager is a security management service that centrally configures and enforces AWS WAF rules, Shield Advanced protections, and security groups across accounts and Regions. It does not manage or create VPC endpoints for S3 or enforce private connectivity routing. Firewall Manager operates at the layer of web application firewall rules and network security policies, not at the layer of VPC routing for private service access.
    • Private connectivity to Amazon S3 from a VPC requires a gateway VPC endpoint, which is a VPC routing configuration change. Firewall Manager cannot create or manage gateway endpoints and therefore cannot satisfy the private S3 connectivity requirement. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/gateway-endpoints.html https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/how-to-use-interface-vpc-endpoints-to-meet-your-security-objectives/

questions.question_text:
A company manages multiple AWS accounts by using AWS Organizations, including separate development and production accounts. An external auditor has asked for evidence that the workloads running in the production account can withstand disruptive events. The company needs a solution that not only provides evidence for the auditor but also improves the resilience of each production workload through identified remediation actions.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
options: Create a new AWS Audit Manager assessment by using the AWS Well-Architected framework, rely on the automated evidence collection to prove resilience for the production workloads, and provide the assessment report to the auditor without changing the workload architecture

Review each production workload in the AWS Well-Architected Tool, evaluate the workloads against the Operational Excellence, Security, and Reliability pillars, implement the recommended mitigations, and provide the resulting documentation to the auditor

Use AWS Fault Injection Service to generate resilience experiment reports for the development account only, and use those reports as direct evidence that the production account workloads are resilient because the workloads share the same application code

Use AWS Audit Manager to create an assessment in the production account by using the AWS Operational Best Practices framework, wait for the assessment to finish, and then give the auditor access to the generated reports
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Review each production workload in the AWS Well-Architected Tool, evaluate the workloads against the Operational Excellence, Security, and Reliability pillars, implement the recommended mitigations, and provide the resulting documentation to the auditor
The AWS Well-Architected Tool is the best fit because it is specifically designed to review workloads against AWS architectural best practices and identify risks and improvement opportunities. AWS states that the review process is intended to identify critical issues and areas that can be improved, and the Reliability pillar focuses on a workload’s ability to recover from disruptions and continue operating correctly. This option also directly satisfies the requirement to improve the resilience of each production workload. The engineer reviews the production workloads, identifies risks across the Operational Excellence, Security, and Reliability pillars, and then implements the necessary mitigations. That produces both actual resilience improvements and documented evidence for the auditor. Among the available choices, this is the only option that explicitly combines a resilience-focused architectural review with implementation of corrective actions on the production workloads themselves. That makes it the strongest answer for both improving resilience and producing audit-ready documentation.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/userguide/intro.html Incorrect Options:
  • Use AWS Audit Manager to create an assessment in the production account by using the AWS Operational Best Practices framework, wait for the assessment to finish, and then give the auditor access to the generated reports — AWS Audit Manager helps automate evidence collection for compliance and control assessments, but it does not itself improve workload resilience. AWS also notes that the AWS Operational Best Practices framework is intended to help prepare for audits and does not guarantee that systems are resilient or that an audit will be passed. This option can help gather audit evidence, but it does not meet the requirement to improve the resilience of each production workload. It is therefore incomplete for the stated objective.
  • Create a new AWS Audit Manager assessment by using the AWS Well-Architected framework, rely on the automated evidence collection to prove resilience for the production workloads, and provide the assessment report to the auditor without changing the workload architecture — AWS Audit Manager has a prebuilt AWS Well-Architected framework, but AWS describes it as a way to collect evidence that is relevant for an audit rather than a mechanism to review and improve workload design directly. This option also explicitly avoids changing the workload architecture. Since the requirement says the solution must improve the resilience of each production workload, relying only on assessment evidence without implementing mitigations is insufficient.
  • Use AWS Fault Injection Service to generate resilience experiment reports for the development account only, and use those reports as direct evidence that the production account workloads are resilient because the workloads share the same application code — AWS Fault Injection Service reports can help demonstrate that experiments were run, but using development-only experiment reports as proof of production resilience is too indirect. Shared application code does not guarantee that the production account has the same architecture, dependencies, scaling behavior, or operational characteristics. This option therefore fails on both evidence quality and scope. The auditor asked for evidence about the production account workloads, and this approach does not provide that direct assurance.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/userguide/intro.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/pdfs/wellarchitected/latest/framework/wellarchitected-framework.pdf https://docs.aws.amazon.com/audit-manager/latest/userguide/what-is.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/audit-manager/latest/userguide/OBP.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/fis/latest/userguide/what-is.html

questions.question_text:
A company runs a frontend application on Amazon EC2 instances behind an Application Load Balancer. The EC2 instances use Amazon EBS volumes for storage, and the application stores large media files in Amazon S3. The company has implemented preventive and detective controls against ransomware attacks. The security team now needs to design a disaster recovery solution that can restore the environment to a known good state if ransomware bypasses existing controls. The solution must support an RPO of 1 hour and must allow rapid reconstruction of the application environment.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
options: Configure AWS Backup to take hourly backups of EBS volumes and S3 data, and store infrastructure definitions as AWS CloudFormation templates to enable rapid environment reconstruction

Enable AWS Config rules to monitor the configuration compliance of EC2 instances and S3 buckets against a backup frequency policy. Create an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule to invoke an AWS Lambda function every 30 minutes that validates the backup compliance status of all protected resources and sends an Amazon SNS alert to the security team if any resource has not received a compliant backup within the required RPO window
Use Amazon Security Lake and AWS Security Hub to centralize findings and define recovery procedures, and store AWS CloudFormation templates to rebuild infrastructure when needed
Configure AWS Backup to take daily backups of EBS volumes and S3 data, and use Amazon Security Lake to analyze logs and trigger automated remediation workflows during incidents
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Configure AWS Backup to take hourly backups of EBS volumes and S3 data, and store infrastructure definitions as AWS CloudFormation templates to enable rapid environment reconstruction
Using AWS Backup with an hourly backup schedule ensures that both EBS volumes and S3 data are protected with a recovery point objective of 1 hour. AWS Backup provides centralized and automated backup management, which is critical for maintaining consistent recovery points across multiple services. Storing infrastructure definitions as AWS CloudFormation templates allows the company to quickly recreate the entire environment, including load balancers, EC2 instances, and networking components. This ensures that recovery is not limited to data restoration but also includes rebuilding the application stack in a controlled and repeatable way. This combination directly addresses ransomware recovery requirements by enabling both data restoration and infrastructure redeployment. It meets the RPO requirement, minimizes recovery time, and aligns with best practices for disaster recovery and infrastructure as code.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/plan-options-and-configuration.html Incorrect Options:
  • Configure AWS Backup to take daily backups of EBS volumes and S3 data, and use Amazon Security Lake to analyze logs and trigger automated remediation workflows during incidents — Daily backups do not meet the required RPO of 1 hour. Any data changes within the last 24 hours would be lost in the event of a ransomware attack. Although Amazon Security Lake provides valuable centralized logging and analytics capabilities, it does not contribute to meeting backup and recovery objectives. This option focuses more on detection and analysis than on recovery.
  • Use Amazon Security Lake and AWS Security Hub to centralize findings and define recovery procedures, and store AWS CloudFormation templates to rebuild infrastructure when needed — Amazon Security Lake and AWS Security Hub are useful for detection, visibility, and incident response coordination, but they do not provide data backup or recovery capabilities. While storing CloudFormation templates supports infrastructure reconstruction, the absence of frequent backups means that the solution cannot meet the RPO requirement. This option lacks a critical data protection component.
  • Enable AWS Config rules to monitor the configuration compliance of EC2 instances and S3 buckets against a backup frequency policy. Create an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule to invoke an AWS Lambda function every 30 minutes that validates the backup compliance status of all protected resources and sends an Amazon SNS alert to the security team if any resource has not received a compliant backup within the required RPO window — Configuring AWS Config rules and Lambda functions to monitor backup compliance and send alerts does not create backups or recover data. This approach provides compliance monitoring and alerting, which are useful operational controls, but the Lambda function only notifies the security team of missing backups rather than executing the recovery itself. Receiving an SNS alert does not constitute a recovery solution and does not address the DR requirement to return to normal operations after a ransomware event bypasses preventive and detective controls.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/whatisbackup.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/aws-backup/latest/devguide/plan-options-and-configuration.html

options: Direct engineers to run the aws sts get-session-token command with the --serial-number and --token-code flags to obtain temporary session credentials, then configure the AWS CLI profile to use the returned AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, and SessionToken values for subsequent API calls

Define an IAM role with a trust policy condition that requires MFA authentication, instruct engineers to call the aws sts assume-role command with --serial-number and --token-code parameters, store the returned temporary credentials as the AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, and AWS_SESSION_TOKEN environment variables, and add sts:AssumeRole to the policy's NotAction list to prevent session context bypass

Deploy AWS IAM Identity Center with MFA-enabled permission sets assigned to each DevOps engineer, then instruct engineers to authenticate through the IAM Identity Center portal to receive temporary AWS CLI credentials that already carry the correct MFA session context required by the BoolIfExists condition evaluation
Instruct users to regenerate their long-term access keys after enabling MFA so that subsequent CLI calls automatically include MFA context in the request
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Direct engineers to run the aws sts get-session-token command with the --serial-number and --token-code flags to obtain temporary session credentials, then configure the AWS CLI profile to use the returned AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, and SessionToken values for subsequent API calls
The BoolIfExists condition key evaluates aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent against the current session context. When engineers use long-term IAM user access keys directly with the AWS CLI, those credentials are not associated with an MFA session, so aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent is either absent or false. The Deny statement with BoolIfExists fires in both cases, blocking all ec2:* actions. The engineers have registered MFA devices, but they have not obtained session tokens that carry MFA authentication context, which is why the CLI calls still fail.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/STS/latest/APIReference/API_GetSessionToken.html Running aws sts get-session-token with --serial-number set to the MFA device ARN and --token-code set to the current one-time password generates temporary credentials that include an MFA authentication context. When these temporary credentials are used in the AWS CLI, aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent evaluates to true, the Deny condition is not satisfied, and the Allow statement from the first policy statement takes effect.
