Engineers configure their CLI profile with the three returned credential values to enable the MFA-authenticated session. This approach requires no changes to the existing IAM policy and enforces MFA exactly as intended. The policy continues to deny access to engineers who have not authenticated with MFA, and engineers who have obtained session tokens through sts get-session-token operate normally.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/STS/latest/APIReference/API_GetSessionToken.html Incorrect Options:
  • Instruct users to regenerate their long-term access keys after enabling MFA so that subsequent CLI calls automatically include MFA context in the request — Regenerating access keys does not include MFA context in API requests. Long-term credentials remain static and cannot satisfy the MFA condition. Temporary credentials from STS are required.
  • Deploy AWS IAM Identity Center with MFA-enabled permission sets assigned to each DevOps engineer, then instruct engineers to authenticate through the IAM Identity Center portal to receive temporary AWS CLI credentials that already carry the correct MFA session context required by the BoolIfExists condition evaluation — AWS IAM Identity Center is a valid solution for enforcing MFA across AWS CLI access, and it does produce temporary credentials with appropriate session context. However, deploying IAM Identity Center requires configuring an identity source, assigning permission sets, and migrating the DevOps engineers from IAM user credentials to Identity Center credentials. This is a broader infrastructure change than required to resolve the described problem.
The existing IAM users, MFA devices, and policy are already in place. The engineers only need to use the sts get-session-token command to obtain session credentials that carry MFA context, which resolves the issue immediately without any additional infrastructure deployment or credential migration.
  • Define an IAM role with a trust policy condition that requires MFA authentication, instruct engineers to call the aws sts assume-role command with --serial-number and --token-code parameters, store the returned temporary credentials as the AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, and AWS_SESSION_TOKEN environment variables, and add sts:AssumeRole to the policy's NotAction list to prevent session context bypass — Using sts:AssumeRole with MFA in the trust policy is an alternative MFA enforcement pattern, but it is more complex than the get-session-token approach for this use case. The engineers are IAM users in the same account who need to run EC2 commands; assuming a role adds an unnecessary layer of credential exchange and role management. Adding sts:AssumeRole to the policy's NotAction list would also require restructuring the existing policy significantly. The get-session-token approach in the correct option directly produces temporary credentials with MFA context for the existing IAM identity without requiring role assumption, role trust policy configuration, or policy restructuring.
    • https://docs.aws.amazon.com/STS/latest/APIReference/API_GetSessionToken.html https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/enhance-programmatic-access-for-iam-users-using-yubikey-for-multi-factor-authentication/

questions.question_text:
A company is deploying a new workload across multiple AWS accounts in an AWS Organizations environment. The finance team requires every supported resource to include a tag named CostCenter. The tag value must be one of three approved values that map to internal billing codes. The company must enforce the allowed CostCenter values during create and tag operations, and it also must prevent users from creating resources without the CostCenter tag.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
options: Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations, define a tag policy for the CostCenter tag with the approved values, and create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes an AWS Lambda function whenever a noncompliant CostCenter tag is created so the function can block further tag changes

Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations, define a tag policy for the CostCenter tag with the three approved values, turn on enforcement for supported resource types, and create a service control policy that denies create operations when the aws:RequestTag/CostCenter condition key is null

Create an AWS Organizations backup policy that requires a CostCenter tag on all supported resources and configure the policy to reject any resource operation when the CostCenter tag is missing or invalid

Create an organization-wide AWS Config rule that checks all resources for the CostCenter tag and automatically remediates missing or invalid values by applying one of the approved values based on the resource type
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations, define a tag policy for the CostCenter tag with the three approved values, turn on enforcement for supported resource types, and create a service control policy that denies create operations when the aws:RequestTag/CostCenter condition key is null
AWS Organizations tag policies are the native mechanism for standardizing tag keys and allowed tag values across accounts in an organization. AWS documents that tag policies can define acceptable values for a tag such as CostCenter and can enforce noncompliant operations on supported resource types. This directly meets the requirement to allow only one of three approved CostCenter values and to prevent the tag from being changed to an unapproved value on supported services.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies.html However, AWS also documents that tag policies alone do not evaluate untagged resources unless you use the required tag key capability or another control. To prevent resource creation when the CostCenter tag is missing, an SCP can deny create operations when aws:RequestTag/CostCenter is null. AWS IAM documentation shows that aws:RequestTag/tag-key is the correct condition key pattern for controlling which tag values can be passed in a request. This combination gives the company both controls it needs. The tag policy governs the approved values and blocks noncompliant tag operations for supported resource types, while the SCP forces users to supply the CostCenter tag during creation. It is the most direct and centralized solution.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_example-tag-policies.html Incorrect Options:
  • Create an AWS Organizations backup policy that requires a CostCenter tag on all supported resources and configure the policy to reject any resource operation when the CostCenter tag is missing or invalid — AWS Organizations backup policies are used to manage backup plans and backup-related governance. They are not the control mechanism for required resource tags or enforcement of approved tag values. This option therefore uses the wrong type of organizational policy. The requirement is specifically about tagging governance, which is handled by tag policies and IAM or SCP tag condition keys, not backup policies.
  • Create an organization-wide AWS Config rule that checks all resources for the CostCenter tag and automatically remediates missing or invalid values by applying one of the approved values based on the resource type — An organization-wide Config rule can help identify noncompliant resources, and remediation can help correct drift after the fact. That makes this design useful for reporting and cleanup. It still does not meet the requirement to enforce the policy at create time. It also introduces a risky remediation pattern by automatically assigning approved values based on resource type, which may not reflect the correct financial ownership of the resource.
  • Enable tag policies in AWS Organizations, define a tag policy for the CostCenter tag with the approved values, and create an Amazon EventBridge rule that invokes an AWS Lambda function whenever a noncompliant CostCenter tag is created so the function can block further tag changes — The tag policy portion is directionally correct because tag policies are the right service for standardizing allowed tag values across the organization. On supported resource types, they can prevent noncompliant tagging operations. The EventBridge and Lambda part is unnecessary and still reactive. A Lambda function triggered after a noncompliant event is not the right mechanism to block the original tag mutation in real time, and it does not solve the requirement to require the CostCenter tag during create operations.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_tag-policies-enforcement.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_example-tag-policies.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/WhatIsConfig.html

questions.question_text:
An organization recently rotated EC2 instance host keys in CloudHSM to comply with security policies, but after the rotation, users attempting to connect via EC2 Instance Connect receive host key validation failures and cannot establish connections. The security team confirms that the new host keys are correctly installed on the EC2 instances and SSH connectivity tests from alternative sources succeed, indicating the issue is specific to EC2 Instance Connect functionality.
What is the most likely cause of the connection failures?
options: The new host keys must be uploaded to the AWS trusted host keys database to enable EC2 Instance Connect verification
The EC2 instance profile lacks the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore IAM policy required for EC2 Instance Connect to function
Host key validation failures indicate that Systems Manager Session Manager must be enabled as a prerequisite to EC2 Instance Connect
The EC2 Instance Connect service requires host key entries to be stored in AWS Secrets Manager before authentication succeeds
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • The EC2 instance profile lacks the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore IAM policy required for EC2 Instance Connect to function
EC2 Instance Connect requires specific IAM permissions on the EC2 instance profile to function correctly, including the ability to retrieve temporary SSH public keys from the EC2 Instance Connect API and verify them with the running EC2 instance. The AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore IAM policy grants these necessary permissions and allows the EC2 instance to communicate with AWS Systems Manager services. Without this policy attached to the instance profile, the EC2 instance cannot authenticate the temporary SSH public key that EC2 Instance Connect generates during the connection process. This explains why standard SSH key pairs and direct SSH connections work (they do not require EC2 Instance Connect API communication), but EC2 Instance Connect connections fail. The host key rotation itself is not the issue, as host keys are used for server identity verification, not client authentication. The problem manifests as a host key validation failure because the connection attempt fails before host key negotiation completes successfully. Incorrect Options:
  • The EC2 Instance Connect service requires host key entries to be stored in AWS Secrets Manager before authentication succeeds — AWS Secrets Manager stores application secrets, database credentials, and API keys, not SSH host keys or EC2 Instance Connect credentials. EC2 Instance Connect does not interact with Secrets Manager or require host key entries to be stored there. Host key validation occurs through SSH protocol mechanisms, not through AWS secrets management services, making this option technically infeasible.
  • The new host keys must be uploaded to the AWS trusted host keys database to enable EC2 Instance Connect verification — AWS does not maintain a centralized "trusted host keys database" where administrators upload EC2 instance host keys. EC2 Instance Connect does not validate instance identity through any AWS-managed database of host keys; it validates the SSH connection through IAM policies and temporary credentials. This option misrepresents how EC2 Instance Connect functions and does not match any actual AWS service capability.
  • Host key validation failures indicate that Systems Manager Session Manager must be enabled as a prerequisite to EC2 Instance Connect — AWS Systems Manager Session Manager is a separate session management feature that provides shell access to EC2 instances without requiring SSH key management. While Session Manager also uses the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy, it is not a prerequisite for EC2 Instance Connect to function. EC2 Instance Connect and Session Manager are independent features, and enabling Session Manager does not resolve EC2 Instance Connect host key validation failures.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/connect-linux-inst-eic.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/ec2-instance-connect-set-up.html

questions.question_text:
A company has enabled AWS Config across an AWS Organizations environment and has deployed hundreds of Amazon S3 buckets across multiple accounts. A security engineer must identify any buckets that are not configured to use AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) for default encryption. The company also must ensure that new objects cannot be uploaded unless the uploads use AWS KMS encryption. The solution must work across the organization with centralized governance and low operational overhead.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
options: Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-bucket-ssl-requests-only to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create a service control policy that allows s3:PutObject only when the upload request uses AWS KMS encryption

Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create an AWS Config remediation action that automatically rewrites every PutObject request that does not specify AWS KMS encryption

Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create bucket policies on each bucket to deny s3:PutObject only when the request specifies server-side encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys

Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create a service control policy that allows s3:PutObject only when the upload request uses AWS KMS encryption
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create a service control policy that allows s3:PutObject only when the upload request uses AWS KMS encryption
The AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms is the correct managed rule for identifying buckets that are not configured to use AWS KMS for default encryption. AWS documents that this rule evaluates whether S3 buckets use AWS KMS default encryption and marks buckets as noncompliant if they are not configured accordingly. To prevent uploads that are not encrypted with AWS KMS, the company needs a preventative control on s3:PutObject. A service control policy can act as an organization-wide guardrail so that member accounts cannot upload objects unless the request satisfies the required encryption conditions. This provides centralized governance and avoids the operational burden of managing individual bucket policies across hundreds of buckets. This combination meets both parts of the requirement. AWS Config provides detection of buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and the SCP provides centralized prevention for future object uploads that do not use AWS KMS. Incorrect Options:
  • Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create bucket policies on each bucket to deny s3:PutObject only when the request specifies server-side encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys — The AWS Config rule in this option is correct, but the bucket policy approach is weaker operationally because the company has hundreds of buckets across an organization. Writing and maintaining a bucket policy on every bucket introduces much more administrative overhead than an organization-level SCP.
This option is also too narrow in the way it is described. Denying only requests that specify SSE-S3 does not fully guarantee that all uploads use AWS KMS, because requests could omit the header or use another encryption mode. The preventative control should require AWS KMS, not simply deny one undesired encryption method.
  • Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-bucket-ssl-requests-only to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create a service control policy that allows s3:PutObject only when the upload request uses AWS KMS encryption — The s3-bucket-ssl-requests-only AWS Config managed rule checks whether bucket policies require requests to use SSL or TLS. It is a transport security rule, not a rule that checks whether a bucket is configured to use AWS KMS default encryption. Although the SCP portion is directionally plausible, the detection half of the solution is wrong. Because the rule does not identify buckets that are not KMS-encrypted, this option does not meet the requirement.
  • Use the AWS Config managed rule s3-default-encryption-kms to identify S3 buckets that are not configured for AWS KMS default encryption, and create an AWS Config remediation action that automatically rewrites every PutObject request that does not specify AWS KMS encryption — AWS Config remediation actions are useful for correcting noncompliant resource configuration after AWS Config detects drift. They do not sit inline on every PutObject request and cannot rewrite each upload request in real time before the object is stored. Because the requirement is to prevent unencrypted uploads, the control must be preventative rather than post-detection remediation. This option therefore uses the wrong enforcement model.
    • https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingKMSEncryption.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/specifying-kms-encryption.html

questions.question_text:
During a post-breach forensic investigation, a security team discovers that an attacker gained temporary access to an AWS account with ID 987654321098. The attacker created an AWS KMS key during the compromise. The key policy on this KMS key grants a specific IAM principal kms:* permissions. Although the security team scheduled the key for deletion the previous day, the key remains active and usable today. The key has the ARN arn:aws:kms:us-west-2:987654321098:key/mrk-1a2b3c4d5e6f7891011121314151617.
The forensics team must delete this key as quickly as possible to stop any potential continued data access.
Which solution will meet this requirement?
options: Call the kms:CancelKeyDeletion API on the KMS key to reset its pending deletion status. Then immediately call kms:DeleteKey on the key ARN to remove the key from the account without any waiting period

Identify all AWS Regions where the multi-Region KMS key or any of its replicas currently exist. Schedule the key for deletion in each Region where it is present, using the minimum waiting period of 7 days in every Region

Update the key policy on the KMS key to add an explicit Allow statement for kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion and all other kms:* actions, specifying the security team's IAM role ARN as an additional key administrator. Then reissue the kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion API call for the key ARN and specify a waiting period of 7 days to satisfy the mandatory KMS deletion waiting period

Call kms:RotateKeyOnDemand on the KMS key to generate new key material. Then schedule the original key material version for immediate deletion because the original version has been superseded by the newly rotated key material and is no longer needed
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Identify all AWS Regions where the multi-Region KMS key or any of its replicas currently exist. Schedule the key for deletion in each Region where it is present, using the minimum waiting period of 7 days in every Region
The key ARN contains the prefix mrk-, which identifies this as a multi-Region key (MRK). Multi-Region keys consist of a primary key in one Region and replica keys in one or more additional Regions. All replicas share the same key material and key ID. Because each replica can independently encrypt and decrypt data, a multi-Region key remains usable as long as any replica exists in any Region. To completely eliminate the key, the security team must schedule deletion for the primary key and every replica in every Region where they exist. AWS requires that all replica keys be scheduled for deletion before the primary key can be deleted. Attempting to delete the primary key while replicas still exist produces an error. The minimum waiting period for KMS key deletion is 7 days, and this minimum applies to both primary and replica keys in every Region. There is no API or mechanism that bypasses this waiting period, even in incident response scenarios. The 7-day window protects against accidental deletion while still allowing prompt action. The team should use the minimum 7-day window since the requirement is to delete the key as quickly as possible. The reason the key remains active despite a previous deletion schedule is most likely that deletion was scheduled only for the primary key in one Region, while one or more replica keys continue to exist in other Regions. The security team must enumerate all Regions containing the key using the KMS console or the kms:ListKeys and kms:DescribeKey APIs across Regions, then schedule deletion with the minimum 7-day waiting period in each.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/multi-region-keys-overview.html
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/multi-region-keys-overview.html Incorrect Options:
  • Call kms:RotateKeyOnDemand on the KMS key to generate new key material. Then schedule the original key material version for immediate deletion because the original version has been superseded by the newly rotated key material and is no longer needed — AWS KMS on-demand key rotation generates new key material for the key, but all previous key material versions are retained and remain available for decrypting data that was encrypted with them. AWS does not allow selective deletion of individual key material versions by version ID. Deleting a KMS key deletes all versions of its key material simultaneously after the mandatory waiting period expires. There is no API to delete only the original key material version while keeping the key active.
Additionally, rotating the key material does not prevent the attacker from using the key if they have a valid session or API credentials that allow them to call kms:GenerateDataKey or kms:Encrypt. The key remains enabled and usable until scheduled for deletion in all Regions and the waiting period expires.
  • Update the key policy on the KMS key to add an explicit Allow statement for kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion and all other kms:* actions, specifying the security team's IAM role ARN as an additional key administrator. Then reissue the kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion API call for the key ARN and specify a waiting period of 7 days to satisfy the mandatory KMS deletion waiting period — The key policy already grants the IAM principal kms:* permissions, which already includes kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion. Adding another explicit Allow statement and a key administrator to the key policy is redundant and does not resolve any permission problem. The problem is not a missing permission; it is that deletion was not scheduled across all Regions where the multi-Region key and its replicas exist.
    • Modifying a key policy during an active security incident also introduces unnecessary changes that complicate the forensic record and audit trail. The policy modification adds no value and does not accelerate the deletion of the key across all Regions.
  • Call the kms:CancelKeyDeletion API on the KMS key to reset its pending deletion status. Then immediately call kms:DeleteKey on the key ARN to remove the key from the account without any waiting period — The kms:CancelKeyDeletion API cancels a pending deletion schedule and returns the key to an enabled or disabled state. Calling this API moves the key further away from deletion, not closer to it, making this the opposite of what is needed. Furthermore, there is no kms:DeleteKey API in AWS KMS that immediately destroys a key without a waiting period. The only deletion mechanism in AWS KMS is kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion, which enforces a mandatory waiting period of 7 to 30 days. Immediate key deletion is intentionally prevented in AWS KMS to protect against accidental loss.
    • https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/multi-region-keys-overview.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/deleting-keys.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/rotate-keys.html

questions.question_text:
A compliance team at a technology company has deployed an AWS Lambda function to generate daily security reports. The function gathers Amazon Inspector findings from AWS Security Hub in the us-east-1 Region and uses an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule to trigger report generation each day. A security engineer discovers that the Lambda function is failing to produce the reports. The engineer must resolve the failure and ensure the function's execution role follows the principle of least privilege.
Which solution will meet these requirements?
options: Attach the AWS managed AWSSecurityHubReadOnlyAccess policy to the Lambda function's execution role to provide complete read access to all Security Hub data across all products and standards

Write an inline IAM policy that explicitly allows Get, List, Batch, and Describe actions scoped to the arn:aws:securityhub:us-east-1::product/aws/inspector/* resource ARN and attach this policy to the Lambda function's execution role to restrict access to Inspector-sourced findings only
Create a custom IAM policy that grants read-only permissions scoped to Amazon Inspector findings and AWS Security Hub resources in us-east-1, and attach it to the Lambda function's execution role

Create a separate IAM role with both the SecurityAudit and ReadOnlyAccess AWS managed policies attached, then update the Lambda function code to call the sts:AssumeRole API at runtime to assume this broader-privilege role before executing Security Hub and Inspector API queries
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Create a custom IAM policy that grants read-only permissions scoped to Amazon Inspector findings and AWS Security Hub resources in us-east-1, and attach it to the Lambda function's execution role
The Lambda function requires permission to read Amazon Inspector findings that are aggregated in AWS Security Hub. The correct least privilege approach is to create a custom IAM policy that grants only the specific read-only actions needed to query Security Hub findings and, if necessary, Inspector data in the us-east-1 Region. Attaching this custom policy directly to the function's execution role ensures the role holds exactly the permissions required and nothing more. A custom policy scoped to the relevant services and Region satisfies both the functional requirement to access the findings data and the security requirement to apply least privilege. The engineer can identify the precise API actions the function uses, such as securityhub:GetFindings or securityhub:BatchGetFindings, and include only those actions in the policy. This targeted approach avoids granting access to Security Hub features and data sources the function does not need. The failure is caused by the execution role lacking the necessary permissions to call Security Hub APIs. Attaching a properly scoped custom read-only policy to the execution role resolves the failure without introducing excess permissions. Incorrect Options:
  • Attach the AWS managed AWSSecurityHubReadOnlyAccess policy to the Lambda function's execution role to provide complete read access to all Security Hub data across all products and standards — The AWSSecurityHubReadOnlyAccess AWS managed policy grants read access to all Security Hub findings, standards, insights, and product integrations across the entire account. This provides far broader access than the function requires since the function only needs to read Inspector findings in a single Region. Attaching this managed policy violates the principle of least privilege by granting access to Security Hub data from all integrated products and all Regions. Least privilege requires granting only the specific permissions needed for the function's defined task. A custom policy scoped to the relevant actions and Region is more appropriate than a broad managed policy that covers all Security Hub capabilities.
  • Create a separate IAM role with both the SecurityAudit and ReadOnlyAccess AWS managed policies attached, then update the Lambda function code to call the sts:AssumeRole API at runtime to assume this broader-privilege role before executing Security Hub and Inspector API queries — Configuring the function to assume a secondary role with SecurityAudit and ReadOnlyAccess managed policies adds unnecessary complexity and grants far more permissions than the function requires. SecurityAudit grants read access to a wide range of AWS services and configuration data, while ReadOnlyAccess provides read access to virtually all AWS services. Both policies violate the principle of least privilege for a function that only needs to read Inspector findings from Security Hub. Using sts:AssumeRole at runtime also requires the execution role to hold the sts:AssumeRole permission, adds latency to each function invocation for the assume role API call, and introduces an additional role to manage. The simpler and more secure approach is to attach a minimal custom policy directly to the execution role.
  • Write an inline IAM policy that explicitly allows Get, List, Batch, and Describe actions scoped to the arn:aws:securityhub:us-east-1::product/aws/inspector/* resource ARN and attach this policy to the Lambda function's execution role to restrict access to Inspector-sourced findings only — The resource ARN arn:aws:securityhub:us-east-1::product/aws/inspector/* is used with some Security Hub resource-level permission restrictions, but many Security Hub API actions such as securityhub:GetFindings do not support resource-level restrictions and require a wildcard resource. Scoping all Security Hub actions to this ARN may prevent certain required API calls from succeeding because the specified resource pattern does not match the resource type expected by those actions. Additionally, this approach grants wildcard action patterns such as Get, List, Batch, and Describe on Security Hub without verifying which specific actions are required. A correct least privilege policy identifies the exact actions the function invokes rather than using wildcards. Option Create a custom IAM policy that grants read-only permissions scoped to Amazon Inspector findings and AWS Security Hub resources in us-east-1, and attach it to the Lambda function's execution role is more precise and operationally correct.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/security-iam.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/nine-aws-security-hub-best-practices/

questions.question_text:
A company uses AWS Config rules to detect Amazon S3 buckets that violate internal data protection requirements. The S3 buckets exist across multiple AWS Regions and multiple AWS accounts that are part of an AWS Organizations structure. The security team needs a centralized solution that can address buckets that are already noncompliant and can also remediate buckets that become noncompliant in the future. The solution must work across the organization and across Regions with minimal ongoing administrative effort.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
options: Deploy an AWS Config aggregator with organization-wide aggregation, and attach a service control policy to all organizational units that denies API actions that could create noncompliant S3 buckets

Deploy an AWS Config aggregator with organization-wide aggregation across accounts and Regions, and invoke an AWS Lambda remediation function whenever AWS Config identifies a noncompliant S3 bucket so the function can correct or remove the bucket configuration

Deploy AWS Security Hub across the organization, aggregate findings from all accounts and Regions, and use Security Hub automation rules to delete or reconfigure noncompliant S3 buckets whenever related findings appear
Deploy an AWS Config aggregator that includes only the accounts and Regions the company currently uses, and invoke an AWS Lambda remediation function when AWS Config reports a noncompliant S3 bucket
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Deploy an AWS Config aggregator with organization-wide aggregation across accounts and Regions, and invoke an AWS Lambda remediation function whenever AWS Config identifies a noncompliant S3 bucket so the function can correct or remove the bucket configuration
An AWS Config aggregator with organization-wide aggregation provides centralized visibility into compliance results across multiple AWS accounts and multiple AWS Regions. This is the right foundation for the requirement because the company already uses AWS Config rules to identify noncompliant S3 buckets, and the aggregator can collect those results across the entire organization.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/aggregate-data.html Using an AWS Lambda function for remediation addresses the second part of the requirement, which is to remediate both existing noncompliant buckets and future noncompliant buckets. When AWS Config detects a violation, the remediation workflow can invoke Lambda to reconfigure the bucket or take another corrective action. This makes the solution both centralized and automated. This design meets all stated requirements. It covers current and future noncompliant resources, works across accounts and Regions, and uses AWS Config in the way the service is intended to support organizational compliance visibility and remediation. Incorrect Options:
  • Deploy an AWS Config aggregator with organization-wide aggregation, and attach a service control policy to all organizational units that denies API actions that could create noncompliant S3 buckets — An organization-wide AWS Config aggregator is useful for visibility, but a service control policy does not remediate resources that already exist. SCPs act as permission guardrails and can restrict future API actions, but they do not inspect and repair noncompliant S3 buckets that are already present. This option therefore addresses prevention only partially and does not satisfy the requirement to remediate existing noncompliant buckets. It also assumes that an SCP can clean up existing drift, which is not how SCPs operate.
  • Deploy an AWS Config aggregator that includes only the accounts and Regions the company currently uses, and invoke an AWS Lambda remediation function when AWS Config reports a noncompliant S3 bucket — A remediation Lambda function tied to AWS Config findings is a valid way to correct noncompliant S3 buckets. However, limiting the aggregator to only the accounts and Regions the company currently uses creates a future coverage gap. The scenario explicitly mentions a multi-account, multi-Region organization and requires a solution for future noncompliant buckets as well. If new accounts or Regions come into use, the limited scope would miss them unless administrators manually updated the aggregator. That makes this option less suitable than organization-wide aggregation.
  • Deploy AWS Security Hub across the organization, aggregate findings from all accounts and Regions, and use Security Hub automation rules to delete or reconfigure noncompliant S3 buckets whenever related findings appear — AWS Security Hub is useful for aggregating and prioritizing security findings, but it is not the primary service for evaluating organization-wide AWS Config rule compliance and remediating S3 bucket configuration drift. Security Hub can centralize findings, but the underlying remediation requirement here is directly tied to AWS Config noncompliance. This option also overstates what Security Hub automation rules do in this context. Security Hub can support workflow actions and integration patterns, but the direct and native fit for this requirement is AWS Config plus remediation logic. Therefore, this option introduces an unnecessary extra layer and does not align as cleanly with the problem.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/aggregate-data.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/aggregated-create.html https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/mt/manage-custom-aws-config-rules-with-remediation-using-conformance-packs/ https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/what-is-securityhub.html

questions.question_text:
A company operates hundreds of AWS accounts within an AWS Organizations structure in a single AWS Region. The company has designated a centralized security tooling account as the delegated administrator for both Amazon GuardDuty and AWS Security Hub. GuardDuty and Security Hub are automatically enabled for all existing and new accounts. During a security validation exercise, the security team launches an Amazon EC2 instance in one of the member accounts and attempts to generate a DNS-based GuardDuty finding by making repeated DNS queries to a test domain. However, no corresponding finding appears in the centralized Security Hub account.
What is the most likely reason that the finding did not appear in the delegated administrator account?
options: The GuardDuty service-linked role was not created in the member account, which prevents GuardDuty from collecting DNS query data for analysis
The integration between GuardDuty and Security Hub was not enabled in the member account where the activity was generated
The EC2 instance does not have an IAM role that allows publishing DNS activity logs to GuardDuty, which prevents the detection from being generated
The VPC uses a custom DNS resolver instead of the default AmazonProvidedDNS, which prevents GuardDuty from analyzing DNS query logs
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • The VPC uses a custom DNS resolver instead of the default AmazonProvidedDNS, which prevents GuardDuty from analyzing DNS query logs
Amazon GuardDuty relies on DNS logs that are generated by the AmazonProvidedDNS resolver within the VPC. When a VPC is configured to use a custom DNS resolver, such as an external or third-party DNS service, those DNS queries are no longer visible to GuardDuty. Because GuardDuty cannot inspect DNS traffic that bypasses AmazonProvidedDNS, it cannot generate DNS-based findings. As a result, even though the activity occurred, no finding is created and therefore nothing is forwarded to Security Hub. This is a well-known limitation and a common exam trap. GuardDuty DNS findings require the use of the default AWS DNS resolver to function correctly.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_data-sources.html
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_data-sources.html Incorrect Options:
  • The GuardDuty service-linked role was not created in the member account, which prevents GuardDuty from collecting DNS query data for analysis — GuardDuty automatically creates and manages its service-linked role when the service is enabled. If GuardDuty is already enabled across the organization, the service-linked role exists. The absence of a finding is not related to role creation but to the inability to observe DNS traffic.
  • The integration between GuardDuty and Security Hub was not enabled in the member account where the activity was generated — In an organization where GuardDuty and Security Hub are automatically enabled, integration between the two services is typically configured centrally. If GuardDuty is properly enabled and integrated, findings are automatically forwarded to Security Hub. The absence of a finding indicates that the detection did not occur, not that the integration failed.
  • The EC2 instance does not have an IAM role that allows publishing DNS activity logs to GuardDuty, which prevents the detection from being generated — GuardDuty does not require an IAM role on EC2 instances to collect DNS activity. It analyzes traffic at the AWS infrastructure level rather than relying on instance-level permissions. This option incorrectly assumes that instance roles are required for threat detection, which is not the case for GuardDuty.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_data-sources.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_finding-types-active.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/securityhub-accounts.html

questions.question_text:
A company operates several AWS accounts in a multi-account environment. Developers sign in to a dedicated development account for their day-to-day work. A subset of those developers now needs controlled access to selected resources in a separate production account. The security team wants a solution that follows least privilege principles, avoids long-term credential sharing, and provides the most secure cross-account access model for production resources.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
options: Create an IAM user in the production account for cross-account access, grant the required permissions to that user, and allow developers in the development account to use those user credentials when they need production access
Create an IAM role in the production account, grant the required permissions to that role, and allow developers in the development account to assume the role to access the production resources

Create a resource-based policy on each required production resource that allows all principals in the development account to access the resources directly, and rely on developer-side IAM policies in the development account to control who actually uses that access
Create an IAM role in the development account, grant the required permissions to that role, and allow developers in the development account to assume the role to access production resources
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Create an IAM role in the production account, grant the required permissions to that role, and allow developers in the development account to assume the role to access the production resources
The most secure way to provide cross-account access is to create an IAM role in the production account and allow trusted principals from the development account to assume that role. This design uses AWS Security Token Service to issue temporary credentials instead of relying on long-term shared credentials. This approach follows AWS best practices for multi-account access. Permissions are defined in the production account where the protected resources exist, and the trust policy on the role explicitly identifies which principals in the development account can assume the role. That keeps administrative control close to the resources being protected. This model also supports least privilege and strong auditability. Developers continue to authenticate in their own account, then assume a narrowly scoped role only when they need access to production resources. The use of temporary credentials reduces credential exposure and avoids password or access key sharing. Incorrect Options:
  • Create a resource-based policy on each required production resource that allows all principals in the development account to access the resources directly, and rely on developer-side IAM policies in the development account to control who actually uses that access — Resource-based policies can support cross-account access for some services, but allowing all principals in the development account is overly broad and weakens the security boundary. It shifts too much reliance to the source account to prevent misuse. This option is also operationally heavier because each production resource would need to be managed individually. A role in the production account provides a cleaner and more secure central access point with explicit trust and least privilege permissions.
  • Create an IAM role in the development account, grant the required permissions to that role, and allow developers in the development account to assume the role to access production resources — Creating the IAM role in the development account does not properly solve cross-account access to production resources. The role that grants access to production resources should exist in the production account because that is where the resources and trust boundary reside. If the role exists only in the development account, it cannot by itself serve as the correct cross-account authorization point for production. The trust relationship and permissions need to be anchored in the target account.
  • Create an IAM user in the production account for cross-account access, grant the required permissions to that user, and allow developers in the development account to use those user credentials when they need production access — Creating an IAM user in the production account for developers to use introduces long-term credentials and credential distribution risks. Even if each developer had a separate user, this would still be less secure than using temporary role-based access. This option also increases administrative overhead because the production account would need to manage user lifecycle tasks directly. AWS recommends using IAM roles for cross-account access rather than cross-account IAM users.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_common-scenarios_aws-accounts.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles.html#id_roles_terms-and-concepts https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/best-practices.html

questions.question_text:
An organization is implementing AWS Identity Center as the centralized identity management platform for the entire AWS organization spanning multiple accounts. The organization's AWS administrator needs to establish the prerequisites for delegating Identity Center administration to a designated person in the management account.
Which of the following statements correctly describe the required steps? (Select three)
options: Create user and group assignments within the Identity Center directory structure that establishes the initial organization membership
Create permission sets in the management account that define the roles and access levels that users will be assigned across member accounts
Set up and configure a second AWS Region in the management account to provide geographic redundancy for Identity Center operations
Create IAM users in the management account and assign them to Identity Center as the source of identity authentication
Create or link an AWS Identity Center directory in the management account to serve as the identity source for the organization
Deploy an on-premises Active Directory Connector in the management account to facilitate hybrid identity integration with existing corporate directories
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Create or link an AWS Identity Center directory in the management account to serve as the identity source for the organization
AWS Identity Center requires an identity source (directory) in the management account. Organizations can either create a new Identity Center directory managed by AWS or link an existing AWS Directory Service (Managed Microsoft Active Directory or Simple AD). This directory serves as the authoritative source of identity and is prerequisite to all other Identity Center configurations. Without a directory, the platform has no users or groups to manage, making this step logically first.
  • Create permission sets in the management account that define the roles and access levels that users will be assigned across member accounts
    • Permission sets are Identity Center's mechanism for defining IAM roles and their permissions. Permission sets serve as templates that are applied to users and groups across member accounts. Organizations define permission sets in the management account that specify job functions and their associated IAM permissions (PowerUser, Billing Admin, Developer, etc.). These permission sets are then assigned to users and groups, automatically creating corresponding IAM roles in member accounts. Permission sets must be created before user assignments can be made effective.
  • Create user and group assignments within the Identity Center directory structure that establishes the initial organization membership
    • User and group assignments establish which individuals and teams have access to which accounts and permission sets. Assignments are created within the Identity Center directory, linking directory users or groups to specific AWS accounts and permission sets. These assignments generate the actual temporary credentials and session tokens that users receive when they authenticate. Creating assignments is a prerequisite to users being able to access AWS accounts through Identity Center. Incorrect Options:
    • Deploy an on-premises Active Directory Connector in the management account to facilitate hybrid identity integration with existing corporate directories — While Active Directory Connectors can be used for hybrid identity scenarios, they are optional and are used to synchronize corporate directory users into Identity Center after initial setup. The connector does not need to be deployed in the management account (it typically runs in an on-premises data center or through an EC2 instance). Active Directory integration is not a prerequisite for basic Identity Center setup; it is an optional enhancement for organizations with existing corporate directories.
    • Set up and configure a second AWS Region in the management account to provide geographic redundancy for Identity Center operations — AWS Identity Center operates across AWS Regions automatically without requiring redundancy setup in a second Region. The directory created in the management account is replicated and available across all Regions. Multi-Region configuration for Identity Center is not a prerequisite and is handled transparently by AWS infrastructure. This is not part of the standard prerequisite setup.
    • Create IAM users in the management account and assign them to Identity Center as the source of identity authentication — Traditional IAM users are not the source of identity for Identity Center. Identity Center is specifically designed to eliminate the need for individual IAM users in AWS accounts. Users authenticate to Identity Center (which uses its managed directory or an external identity provider), and Identity Center provisions temporary credentials. Creating IAM users in the management account for Identity Center authentication is the opposite of the intended architecture.
    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/getting-started.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/manage-your-identity-source.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/permissionsets.html

questions.question_text:
An organization uses AWS Identity Center for workforce identity management across multiple AWS accounts and has delegated AWS Security Hub administration to a security team member's account. A CloudTrail organization trail logs API calls to an S3 bucket via AWS Organizations, and a CloudTrail Lake event data store has been configured to retain 7 years of organization-wide API events. An IAM user's credentials have been compromised and the incident response team must immediately disable the user's access across all organization accounts and collect 7 days of API activity for forensic analysis.
Which solution will meet these requirements?
options: Remove all IAM policies attached to the compromised user in the management account and query CloudTrail findings through Security Hub to collect 7 days of actions
Disable the compromised IAM user account in the management account and query the CloudTrail S3 logs using Athena to retrieve 7 days of API activity
Revoke all temporary credentials for the user using STS GetCallerIdentity and query Security Hub for historical activity across all accounts over a duration of 7 days
Disable the user's access in AWS Identity Center by removing user assignments and query the CloudTrail Lake organization event data store to retrieve 7 days of API activity
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Disable the user's access in AWS Identity Center by removing user assignments and query the CloudTrail Lake organization event data store to retrieve 7 days of API activity
AWS Identity Center is the central identity provider for workforce identities across the organization. When a user is assigned to an AWS account through Identity Center, their access spans all linked accounts automatically. Removing user assignments in Identity Center immediately revokes access across all organization accounts with a single action, providing organization-wide access denial without requiring account-by-account IAM user modifications. This approach ensures the compromised user cannot access any resources in any organization account. CloudTrail Lake event data stores are specifically designed for long-term retention and queryable analysis of organization-wide CloudTrail events. Unlike S3-based CloudTrail logs which require complex Athena queries or third-party tools, CloudTrail Lake provides native SQL-like query capabilities through its console and API. The organization event data store configured for 7-year retention is pre-populated with all member account activity and can be queried to retrieve exactly 7 days of activity for the specific compromised user without additional setup or data migration. This combination provides both immediate and comprehensive remediation: Identity Center disables access at the source (identity provider) ensuring organization-wide protection, and CloudTrail Lake provides efficient forensic analysis of the 7-day activity window required for incident investigation. Incorrect Options:
  • Disable the compromised IAM user account in the management account and query the CloudTrail S3 logs using Athena to retrieve 7 days of API activity — Disabling an IAM user in the management account only affects access within that single account. In a multi-account organization using Identity Center, users do not have IAM user accounts in member accounts; their access is derived from Identity Center assignments. This approach fails to disable access in any member accounts where the user may have been granted permissions. Additionally, querying S3-based CloudTrail logs with Athena requires manual table creation and SQL expertise, and S3 logs may not be configured for all organization accounts, resulting in incomplete forensic data.
  • Remove all IAM policies attached to the compromised user in the management account and query CloudTrail findings through Security Hub to collect 7 days of actions — Removing IAM policies from a management account IAM user still does not address member account access in an Identity Center environment. IAM policy removal is not the appropriate remediation for an Identity Center user; permission sets control access for Identity Center users across accounts. Security Hub provides security findings and compliance status but is not designed for CloudTrail event query or forensic analysis. This approach uses tools designed for security monitoring rather than incident response and does not provide the 7-day activity data needed for forensic investigation.
  • Revoke all temporary credentials for the user using STS GetCallerIdentity and query Security Hub for historical activity across all accounts over a duration of 7 days — STS GetCallerIdentity is a diagnostic API call that returns information about the currently authenticated principal; it does not revoke credentials or disable access. Even if this were possible, it would only affect temporary credentials issued after the command executes, leaving previously issued credentials still valid. Security Hub aggregates findings from security services but is not a forensic analysis tool and cannot provide detailed 7-day activity logs for the compromised user in the format needed for incident investigation.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/ https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-lake.html https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/optimize-security-operations-with-aws-security-incident-response/

questions.question_text:
A financial institution operates a large fleet of Amazon Linux 2 EC2 instances that process confidential transaction data. The company's security policy enforces three strict compliance requirements:
1. No SSH or RDP management ports may be exposed on any EC2 instance, either internally or externally.
2. All remote session activity must be captured in a tamper-proof, centrally managed audit log.
3. All remote access must be authenticated and authorized through the company's approved centralized cloud identity management system.
The infrastructure team periodically connects to these EC2 instances to investigate performance issues.
Which solution provides remote access to the EC2 instances while satisfying all three compliance requirements in the most operationally efficient manner?
options: Use AWS Systems Manager Automation runbooks to temporarily open SSH access on the EC2 instances whenever the infrastructure team requires access. Configure the runbooks to close the ports automatically after each session ends

Attach an IAM instance role to each EC2 instance that grants the AWS Systems Manager service the permissions it needs to manage the instance. Create an IAM policy that allows Systems Manager Session Manager actions. Assign this policy to the infrastructure team's permission set in the company's centralized identity management system so that all remote sessions route through Session Manager without any open network ports

Install a certified third-party privileged access management solution on each EC2 instance in the fleet. Configure the agent to enforce multi-factor authentication using the company's SAML-based identity provider before granting any interactive shell session. Forward all session keystroke recordings to a centralized audit vault maintained by the third-party vendor, configure automated alerts for sessions exceeding a 30-minute duration threshold, and produce weekly compliance summary reports for all privileged access events across the entire fleet
Enable the EC2 serial console at the AWS account level. Create an IAM policy that allows the infrastructure team's IAM role to use the EC2 serial console on all instances in the fleet
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Attach an IAM instance role to each EC2 instance that grants the AWS Systems Manager service the permissions it needs to manage the instance. Create an IAM policy that allows Systems Manager Session Manager actions. Assign this policy to the infrastructure team's permission set in the company's centralized identity management system so that all remote sessions route through Session Manager without any open network ports
AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides browser-based and CLI-based shell access to EC2 instances without requiring open inbound ports, bastion hosts, or SSH key management. Session Manager communicates with the SSM Agent installed on each instance through an outbound HTTPS connection that the agent initiates to the Systems Manager service endpoint. Because all connectivity is outbound from the instance, no inbound ports need to be open on the instance or its security group, satisfying the first compliance requirement. Session Manager automatically logs all session activity to Amazon S3 or Amazon CloudWatch Logs. These logs are written by the Systems Manager service, not by the session user, making them tamper-proof from the perspective of anyone conducting the session. This satisfies the second compliance requirement for a centrally managed, immutable audit trail. Session Manager also integrates natively with AWS IAM Identity Center, allowing the infrastructure team's permission sets to control who can initiate sessions, which satisfies the third compliance requirement for centralized identity-based authorization. The solution addresses all three compliance requirements using fully managed AWS services with no third-party dependencies, no open network ports, and no additional software to install beyond the SSM Agent (which is pre-installed on Amazon Linux 2 AMIs). Any engineer who authenticates through the company's centralized identity system and holds the appropriate Session Manager permission receives controlled, audited access without any exposure of management ports. Incorrect Options:
  • Enable the EC2 serial console at the AWS account level. Create an IAM policy that allows the infrastructure team's IAM role to use the EC2 serial console on all instances in the fleet — The EC2 serial console provides out-of-band access through the instance's serial port, typically used for troubleshooting boot or network configuration failures. However, the EC2 serial console does not integrate with the company's centralized cloud identity management system for per-session authentication and authorization. Access through the serial console uses instance-level operating system credentials rather than centrally managed identities, failing the third compliance requirement.
The EC2 serial console also does not record session activity in a tamper-proof centrally managed audit log. Session Manager provides far more comprehensive session recording and centralized identity integration than the serial console.
  • Install a certified third-party privileged access management solution on each EC2 instance in the fleet. Configure the agent to enforce multi-factor authentication using the company's SAML-based identity provider before granting any interactive shell session. Forward all session keystroke recordings to a centralized audit vault maintained by the third-party vendor, configure automated alerts for sessions exceeding a 30-minute duration threshold, and produce weekly compliance summary reports for all privileged access events across the entire fleet — Third-party privileged access management solutions typically require inbound network connectivity on a proprietary port for client-to-agent communication. This likely exposes a management port on the instance, violating the first compliance requirement. Third-party agents also introduce additional software to deploy, patch, and maintain on every EC2 instance in a large fleet, increasing operational complexity and the attack surface.
    • Using a third-party solution is operationally more expensive than AWS Systems Manager Session Manager, which is available as a fully managed AWS service with native integration into the company's AWS identity management environment. AWS recommends native services over third-party tools when a native solution fully satisfies all stated requirements.
  • Use AWS Systems Manager Automation runbooks to temporarily open SSH access on the EC2 instances whenever the infrastructure team requires access. Configure the runbooks to close the ports automatically after each session ends — Dynamically opening and closing SSH ports using Automation runbooks is a just-in-time access pattern. However, this approach still violates the first compliance requirement because it exposes SSH port 22 on the security group during each access window. The compliance requirement states that no remote access management ports can be exposed at any time. Even a brief exposure window is non-compliant.
    • This pattern also introduces operational risk because if the runbook that closes the port fails or times out, the security group is left in a non-compliant state with an open SSH port. The approach relies on automation working correctly every time rather than having no open ports as a baseline configuration. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager-logging.html

questions.question_text:
A multinational consulting firm maintains a distributed IT infrastructure across 12 AWS accounts organized within an AWS Organizations structure. Each account serves a different business unit and operates independently, but there is a centralized security and compliance team that manages a dedicated organizational account responsible for security oversight. The firm must catalog all instances of sensitive data including financial records, client intellectual property, and personal information stored across all Amazon S3 buckets in the entire organization. The findings must be visible through a single centralized platform without requiring security analysts to log into individual accounts.
Which solution best meets these requirements?
options: Set the security account as the delegated administrator for AWS Security Hub and Amazon Macie. Configure the AWS Glue Data Catalog to index all S3 buckets across member accounts as a centralized metadata repository, and use AWS Lake Formation to manage access permissions and sensitive data classifications

Designate the security account as the delegated administrator for AWS Security Hub only. Configure Amazon Inspector in each of the 12 member accounts to scan S3 bucket contents for sensitive data patterns and route all findings to the central Security Hub instance in the security account

Enable and configure Amazon Macie independently in each of the 12 member accounts to identify sensitive data. Integrate each account's Macie instance with AWS Trusted Advisor and consolidate all sensitive data findings in the Trusted Advisor console visible to the security account

Configure the security account as the delegated administrator for both Amazon Macie and AWS Security Hub. Enable and configure Macie to scan all S3 buckets across the organization's member accounts and publish all sensitive data findings directly to the central Security Hub instance
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Configure the security account as the delegated administrator for both Amazon Macie and AWS Security Hub. Enable and configure Macie to scan all S3 buckets across the organization's member accounts and publish all sensitive data findings directly to the central Security Hub instance
Amazon Macie as a delegated administrator service in AWS Organizations allows a single designated account, in this case the security account, to enable and manage Macie scans across all member accounts without requiring separate Macie deployments in each account. Delegation is automatic through the Organizations API, so Macie in the security account has the authorization to scan resources in all member accounts. When Macie discovers sensitive data in S3 buckets across any member account, it publishes findings to AWS Security Hub, also configured with the security account as delegated administrator. Security Hub serves as the centralized dashboard where all Macie findings appear in a unified interface. The security team logs into the security account once and reviews sensitive data discoveries from all 12 member accounts in a single pane of glass. This architecture eliminates the need for analysts to log into each member account separately and ensures no discoveries are missed because a single Macie configuration scans all accounts under delegated administration. The centralized findings in Security Hub provide a complete inventory of sensitive data across the organization. Use Security Hub custom actions to remediate S3 resources based on Macie discovery results:
via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/use-security-hub-custom-actions-to-remediate-s3-resources-based-on-macie-discovery-results/
via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/use-security-hub-custom-actions-to-remediate-s3-resources-based-on-macie-discovery-results/ Incorrect Options:
  • Designate the security account as the delegated administrator for AWS Security Hub only. Configure Amazon Inspector in each of the 12 member accounts to scan S3 bucket contents for sensitive data patterns and route all findings to the central Security Hub instance in the security account — Amazon Inspector is designed to identify software vulnerabilities and exposures in EC2 instances, container images, and Lambda functions. It does not scan S3 bucket contents for sensitive data patterns and is not capable of discovering financial records, client IP, or personal information stored in objects. Configuring Inspector to analyze S3 for sensitive data misrepresents Inspector's capabilities, which are limited to vulnerability assessment on compute resources.
  • Set the security account as the delegated administrator for AWS Security Hub and Amazon Macie. Configure the AWS Glue Data Catalog to index all S3 buckets across member accounts as a centralized metadata repository, and use AWS Lake Formation to manage access permissions and sensitive data classifications — AWS Glue Data Catalog and AWS Lake Formation are data governance and metadata services designed for cataloging data assets and managing access permissions, not for discovering sensitive data patterns within existing S3 objects. These services require the data to be registered and indexed beforehand, not for automated sensitive data discovery. Lake Formation is an access control and governance layer, not a sensitive data detection tool, and it would not discover the sensitive data that already resides untagged in S3 buckets.
  • Enable and configure Amazon Macie independently in each of the 12 member accounts to identify sensitive data. Integrate each account's Macie instance with AWS Trusted Advisor and consolidate all sensitive data findings in the Trusted Advisor console visible to the security account — While Macie can be deployed in individual member accounts to identify sensitive data, requiring independent Macie instances in each of the 12 accounts creates significant operational overhead. Each account must have Macie independently enabled, configured, and monitored. Macie does not have a native integration with AWS Trusted Advisor for findings consolidation. Attempting to route Macie findings to Trusted Advisor requires custom development and does not produce a unified sensitive data inventory view.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/what-is-macie.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/securityhub-v2-concepts.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/findings.html https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/use-security-hub-custom-actions-to-remediate-s3-resources-based-on-macie-discovery-results/

questions.question_text: A global financial services firm creates automated snapshots of its Amazon RDS databases as part of a multi-region disaster recovery strategy. The firm maintains database copies in three AWS accounts: the primary production account, a secondary account in a different Region, and a tertiary account for long-term archival. All snapshots must be encrypted, and each receiving account must be able to decrypt and restore snapshots during a disaster recovery event without requiring decryption keys from external sources. Which solution best satisfies this requirement?
options: Generate snapshots using an AWS KMS customer managed key, then create an AWS Lambda function that imports the key into each account and configures the accounts to use the imported key for snapshot restoration

Create snapshots with the AWS default managed key, then grant the secondary and tertiary accounts IAM permissions on the default key within the primary account so they can decrypt snapshots from any account

Use an AWS KMS customer managed key to encrypt the snapshots, then grant an IAM principal in each account explicit permissions to use the key for decrypt operations through the customer managed key's key policy

Encrypt snapshots using an AWS KMS customer managed key in the primary account, establish AWS KMS key replication across the three accounts using the multi-Region key feature, and add IAM trust relationships between the accounts to enable cross-account key access
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use an AWS KMS customer managed key to encrypt the snapshots, then grant an IAM principal in each account explicit permissions to use the key for decrypt operations through the customer managed key's key policy
A customer managed key is a KMS key that the firm creates and controls, unlike the default AWS managed key which is managed by AWS. The key policy of a customer managed key can grant permissions to principals in other accounts, allowing those principals to use the key for specific operations such as decrypt. The process is straightforward: the primary account creates the customer managed key and uses it to encrypt RDS snapshots. The key policy includes statements that grant the decrypt permission to IAM principals (users or roles) in the secondary and tertiary accounts. When a disaster recovery event occurs, an administrator in the secondary or tertiary account can assume an IAM role that has permission to call the decrypt operation on the key. The role can restore the snapshot into its own account's RDS infrastructure because the key policy authorizes that account's principal to decrypt with the key. This approach ensures that each receiving account has independent ability to decrypt snapshots without requiring the primary account to import, export, or copy key material. The key material never leaves the KMS service, and the policy grants usage permissions instead. This is more secure than copying key material and simpler than setting up key replication. Incorrect Options:
  • Generate snapshots using an AWS KMS customer managed key, then create an AWS Lambda function that imports the key into each account and configures the accounts to use the imported key for snapshot restoration — AWS KMS does not provide an import key feature that accepts key materials from another AWS account's KMS service. The KMS import key feature is designed to import externally generated keys from outside AWS, not to import KMS-generated keys from other AWS accounts. Attempting to import a key from another account is not a supported KMS workflow.
  • Encrypt snapshots using an AWS KMS customer managed key in the primary account, establish AWS KMS key replication across the three accounts using the multi-Region key feature, and add IAM trust relationships between the accounts to enable cross-account key access — AWS KMS multi-Region keys are replicas of a primary customer managed key created in other Regions, not other accounts. A multi-Region key in a secondary Region is still associated with the same account as the primary key. Multi-Region keys are designed for cross-Region disaster recovery within a single account, not for cross-account access. Even with multi-Region key replication set up, the secondary and tertiary accounts would need to be in the same AWS account as the primary for this approach to work.
  • Create snapshots with the AWS default managed key, then grant the secondary and tertiary accounts IAM permissions on the default key within the primary account so they can decrypt snapshots from any account — Granting IAM permissions on an AWS managed key to principals in other accounts is not possible. AWS managed keys enforce account ownership and cannot be shared across accounts via IAM policy. IAM policy is account-specific and cannot grant cross-account access to AWS managed keys. This configuration is not supported by the AWS IAM and KMS integration model.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policy-default.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policy-modifying-external-accounts.html

questions.question_text:
A company publishes REST APIs by using Amazon API Gateway. The application team wants to understand how clients are accessing the APIs, including traffic patterns and request behavior. The team wants to analyze this information without building a custom log-parsing workflow. A security engineer must recommend a solution that provides visibility into API access patterns with the least operational effort.
Which combination of steps should the security engineer implement to meet these requirements? (Select two)
options: Enable Detailed CloudWatch Metrics for the required API stage to capture per-method API metrics
Enable access logging for the required API stage so that API Gateway records detailed request and response information
Use Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights to query and analyze API access information from the API Gateway access logs
Stream API Gateway logs into Amazon OpenSearch Service and build dashboards to analyze request distribution and caller behavior over time
Configure AWS X-Ray tracing for the API stage and use trace maps to analyze long-term API access patterns across different client segments
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Enable access logging for the required API stage so that API Gateway records detailed request and response information
Enabling access logging on an API Gateway stage is required to capture detailed request-level information such as source IP, user agent, request path, and response status. This data forms the foundation for analyzing API access patterns. Without access logs, there is no granular visibility into how clients interact with the API. Therefore, enabling access logging is a necessary first step to meet the requirement of analyzing API usage.
  • Use Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights to query and analyze API access information from the API Gateway access logs
    • Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights provides a serverless and efficient way to query and analyze API Gateway access logs without building a custom parsing solution. It allows the team to run structured queries directly on log data and extract meaningful insights. This satisfies the requirement of analyzing access patterns without manual log parsing. Combined with access logging, it delivers a low-effort and native AWS solution for operational visibility. Incorrect Options:
    • Enable Detailed CloudWatch Metrics for the required API stage to capture per-method API metrics — Detailed CloudWatch metrics provide aggregated statistics such as request count and latency. However, they do not provide request-level details such as source IP or user agent. Because the requirement focuses on analyzing access patterns, including client behavior, metrics alone are insufficient.
