    • Configure AWS X-Ray tracing for the API stage and use trace maps to analyze long-term API access patterns across different client segments — AWS X-Ray focuses on tracing request paths and diagnosing performance issues across distributed systems. It is not designed for analyzing API access patterns at scale. It also requires additional configuration and does not replace the need for access logs.
    • Stream API Gateway logs into Amazon OpenSearch Service and build dashboards to analyze request distribution and caller behavior over time — Amazon OpenSearch Service can provide advanced analytics capabilities, but it requires provisioning, ingestion pipelines, and ongoing management. This adds unnecessary complexity compared to native CloudWatch tools and does not align with the requirement for minimal effort.
    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/set-up-logging.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/AnalyzingLogData.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/xray/latest/devguide/aws-xray.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/opensearch-service/latest/developerguide/what-is.html

questions.question_text:
A security engineer must design a key management solution for a company that performs cryptographic operations on sensitive application data. The solution must create and manage symmetric AWS KMS keys whose key material is generated and used inside a custom key store backed by an AWS CloudHSM cluster. The company also needs to generate symmetric and asymmetric data key pairs for local use within applications. In addition, the company must maintain an audit trail of key usage and key management activity.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
options: Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster, and use Amazon GuardDuty to audit key management and cryptographic operations
Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster to create the keys, and use AWS CloudTrail to audit key usage and key management activity
Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster, and use Amazon Audit Manager to audit key management and cryptographic operations

Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster to create the symmetric KMS keys, use data keys and data key pairs for local cryptographic operations inside the applications, and use AWS Config to capture and audit every cryptographic operation that uses the keys
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster to create the keys, and use AWS CloudTrail to audit key usage and key management activity
AWS Key Management Service supports custom key stores that are backed by AWS CloudHSM clusters. This allows the company to create symmetric KMS keys whose key material is generated and used in the custom key store while still using the AWS KMS control plane and APIs. AWS KMS also supports generating data keys and data key pairs for local cryptographic use in applications, which satisfies the requirement to use symmetric and asymmetric key material outside of AWS KMS.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/keystore-cloudhsm.html
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/manage-cmk-keystore.html AWS CloudTrail is the correct auditing service for AWS KMS activity. AWS documents that CloudTrail logs all AWS KMS API calls, including key management operations such as CreateKey and cryptographic operations such as GenerateDataKey and Decrypt. This provides the required audit trail for both administrative activity and key usage. This combination best meets all requirements with native AWS services. It uses AWS KMS with a custom key store for the required key control model, supports data keys and data key pairs for local application use, and uses CloudTrail for a complete audit record of AWS KMS operations. Incorrect Options:
  • Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster, and use Amazon Audit Manager to audit key management and cryptographic operations — AWS Audit Manager is a compliance evidence collection service. It can collect evidence from services like CloudTrail and Config for assessments and audit reports, but it does not itself serve as the direct operational audit trail for KMS activity. So, this option is incorrect.
  • Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster, and use Amazon GuardDuty to audit key management and cryptographic operations — AWS KMS with a custom key store is again the correct key management service, but Amazon GuardDuty is not an audit service for AWS KMS usage. GuardDuty is a threat detection service that analyzes telemetry sources for suspicious activity. GuardDuty does not record every KMS API call or provide the required audit trail of key management and cryptographic operations. For this requirement, CloudTrail is the correct service.
  • Use AWS Key Management Service with a custom key store that is backed by the AWS CloudHSM cluster to create the symmetric KMS keys, use data keys and data key pairs for local cryptographic operations inside the applications, and use AWS Config to capture and audit every cryptographic operation that uses the keys — AWS KMS with a custom key store and the use of data keys and data key pairs is directionally correct and makes this option sound very plausible. However, AWS Config does not capture every cryptographic operation such as GenerateDataKey or Decrypt as an audit log of API activity. AWS Config records resource configuration state and compliance changes, not full operational audit trails for cryptographic API usage. The correct audit mechanism for AWS KMS API calls is AWS CloudTrail, not AWS Config.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/keystore-cloudhsm.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/manage-cmk-keystore.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/logging-using-cloudtrail.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/WhatIsConfig.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/what-is-guardduty.html

questions.question_text:
A development team publishes an open-source toolset for a SaaS application in a public repository. During a security review, the company discovers that the repository contains an IAM access key and secret key that provide access to internal AWS resources. A security engineer must determine any unauthorized usage of the exposed credentials and must immediately prevent any further misuse.
Which combination of actions should the security engineer take to meet these requirements? (Select two)
options: Use AWS Identity and Access Management Access Analyzer to identify which AWS resources were accessed and determine whether the exposed credentials were used
Enable AWS CloudTrail Insights and use anomaly detection to identify unusual API activity patterns associated with the exposed credentials going forward
Create an AWS Config rule to detect exposed access keys in source code repositories and automatically revoke the keys when detected
Generate an IAM credential report and review the last-used information for the access key to determine recent usage activity
Deactivate the compromised IAM access key in the IAM user account to immediately prevent any further use of the credentials
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use AWS Identity and Access Management Access Analyzer to identify which AWS resources were accessed and determine whether the exposed credentials were used
IAM Access Analyzer simplifies the forensic process through its Policy Generation and Unused Access features. Identify Usage: You can use Access Analyzer to review the specific services and actions an IAM entity (the compromised user) has used over a specific period. By analyzing the CloudTrail logs, it can generate a report of all "Last Accessed" information for that user. Forensic Scope: This allows a security engineer to quickly see if the credentials were used to touch sensitive services (like S3 or RDS) that the developer doesn't normally use, helping define the "blast radius" of the breach without manually writing complex SQL queries in Athena.
  • Deactivate the compromised IAM access key in the IAM user account to immediately prevent any further use of the credentials
    • Deactivating the exposed IAM access key is the most immediate and effective way to prevent further misuse. Once credentials are exposed in a public repository, they must be considered compromised, and continued use poses a significant security risk. By disabling the key, the security engineer ensures that any malicious actor who has obtained the credentials can no longer use them to access AWS resources. This aligns with AWS security best practices for credential compromise response. Incorrect Options:
    • Create an AWS Config rule to detect exposed access keys in source code repositories and automatically revoke the keys when detected — AWS Config can evaluate resource configurations but does not natively detect exposed credentials in external code repositories or automatically revoke access keys. This option introduces a control that is not applicable to the described scenario and does not address immediate remediation or investigation.
    • Enable AWS CloudTrail Insights and use anomaly detection to identify unusual API activity patterns associated with the exposed credentials going forward — CloudTrail Insights can detect unusual API activity patterns, but it is designed for ongoing anomaly detection rather than immediate investigation of a known compromised key. It does not provide direct evidence of past usage of the specific access key, nor does it prevent further misuse.
    • Generate an IAM credential report and review the last-used information for the access key to determine recent usage activity — The IAM credential report only shows the time the key was last used; it does not provide the detail needed to see what resources were accessed or who (which IP/location) used them.
    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access-analyzer.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access-analyzer-concepts.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_getting-report.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-insights.html

questions.question_text:
A company operates a web application deployed behind an Application Load Balancer in AWS. Security logs indicate a credential stuffing attack where attackers are submitting thousands of login requests from different IP addresses, all using a user agent string that identifies the request as coming from a mobile device emulator. The security team needs to block these attack requests while allowing legitimate user traffic to reach the application.
Which solution will meet these requirements?
options: Deploy an AWS Lambda function behind the ALB that inspects incoming requests and rejects those with suspicious user agent strings before they reach the application
Modify the ALB security group rules to deny all inbound traffic from the IP addresses identified in the attack logs
Create an AWS WAF web ACL associated with the ALB and define a custom rule that allows requests only when the user agent string matches legitimate mobile device types
Create an AWS WAF web ACL associated with the ALB and define a custom rule that blocks requests when the user agent string matches the mobile device emulator signature
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Create an AWS WAF web ACL associated with the ALB and define a custom rule that blocks requests when the user agent string matches the mobile device emulator signature
AWS WAF is the AWS-native web application firewall service designed specifically to protect web applications from common web exploits and application-layer attacks including credential stuffing. By creating a WAF web ACL and associating it directly with the ALB, the security team can evaluate and filter HTTP requests before they reach the application. A custom WAF rule that examines the user agent HTTP header can identify and block requests originating from mobile device emulators, effectively filtering out the attack traffic while allowing legitimate requests through. WAF rules operate at Layer 7 (application layer) where HTTP headers and request content are fully inspectable, making it the ideal service for header-based filtering. Custom rules can use string matching, regex patterns, and size constraints to evaluate user agent strings with high precision. WAF also provides real-time metrics and logging capabilities that help the security team monitor attack patterns and refine detection rules over time. This approach provides immediate protection without requiring manual IP-based blocking, eliminates operational overhead of managing multiple security group rules, and adapts as attackers change their attack vectors. WAF is purpose-built for application-layer attacks and provides the most efficient and effective solution for this credential stuffing scenario. Incorrect Options:
  • Deploy an AWS Lambda function behind the ALB that inspects incoming requests and rejects those with suspicious user agent strings before they reach the application — Placing a Lambda function behind the ALB adds processing overhead for every request, including legitimate traffic, which increases latency and costs. Lambda-based request filtering is not as efficient as WAF for high-volume attack scenarios and introduces complexity in deployment and scaling. WAF is purpose-built for this type of request filtering and operates more efficiently at the AWS infrastructure level without impacting application latency.
  • Modify the ALB security group rules to deny all inbound traffic from the IP addresses identified in the attack logs — While security groups can filter traffic based on source IP addresses, this approach requires the security team to identify and manually maintain a list of attack source IPs. In a distributed credential stuffing attack with sources from many different IP addresses, maintaining this list becomes operationally burdensome and is ineffective because attackers can rotate through different IP addresses faster than the team can block them. Security groups are also better suited for Layer 3 (network) filtering rather than application-layer filtering based on HTTP headers.
  • Create an AWS WAF web ACL associated with the ALB and define a custom rule that allows requests only when the user agent string matches legitimate mobile device types — Creating a rule that allows only specific user agent strings would be overly restrictive and break legitimate traffic from mobile browsers or applications with different user agent identifications. This approach assumes all legitimate traffic comes from predefined user agent strings, which is unrealistic in modern applications where thousands of different user agents may be valid. Maintenance of an allowlist would be cumbersome and would likely block legitimate users whose applications use unexpected user agent strings.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-rules.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-rule-statement-type-string-match.html

questions.question_text:
A company is deploying a new application into a newly created AWS account. The application VPC and subnets already exist. The application VPC is peered with an existing VPC in another AWS account in the same AWS Region so that selected Amazon EC2 instances can connect to database servers over TCP port 1521. Amazon EC2 instances in the application VPC are launched and terminated frequently, but only a subset of those instances should be able to reach the databases in the peered VPC. A security engineer must implement a network control that ensures only the required application instances can connect to the databases.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
options: Create a new security group in the database VPC, add an inbound rule that allows all traffic from the CIDR block of the application VPC, add a network ACL rule on the database subnets to allow TCP port 1521 from the application VPC CIDR block, and attach the new security group to the required database instances

Create a new security group in the database VPC that allows TCP port 1521 from the CIDR block of the application VPC, and use an Auto Scaling lifecycle hook to dynamically add and remove temporary host routes for only the approved application instances whenever they launch or terminate

Create a new security group in the application VPC with no inbound rules, create a new security group in the database VPC with an inbound rule that allows TCP port 1521 from the new application security group, attach the application security group to only the application instances that require database access, and attach the database security group to the database instances

Create a new network ACL for the application subnets that allows outbound TCP port 1521 only to the private IP addresses of the database servers in the peered VPC, and create a matching network ACL in the database subnets that allows inbound TCP port 1521 only from the entire application subnet range
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Create a new security group in the application VPC with no inbound rules, create a new security group in the database VPC with an inbound rule that allows TCP port 1521 from the new application security group, attach the application security group to only the application instances that require database access, and attach the database security group to the database instances
AWS allows security groups to reference security groups in a peered VPC when the VPCs are in the same Region, even if the VPCs are in different AWS accounts. This lets the security engineer create a database-side security group rule that permits TCP port 1521 only from the specific security group attached to approved application instances. This is the most precise solution because only the EC2 instances associated with the designated application security group can connect to the database instances. The application fleet can continue to scale dynamically, and only the subset of instances that need database access must be launched with that security group attached. This approach also minimizes operational effort and follows least privilege. It avoids broad CIDR-based access and avoids subnet-wide controls that would affect instances that should not have database connectivity. AWS specifically documents peer security group references as a way to control traffic between peered VPCs. Incorrect Options:
  • Create a new security group in the database VPC, add an inbound rule that allows all traffic from the CIDR block of the application VPC, add a network ACL rule on the database subnets to allow TCP port 1521 from the application VPC CIDR block, and attach the new security group to the required database instances — Allowing all traffic from the application VPC CIDR block is much broader than required. The requirement is that only some application instances should be able to access the databases, but this design would allow any instance in the application VPC to reach the database instances as long as routing exists. The added network ACL rule also increases complexity without solving the least-privilege problem. CIDR-based controls at the subnet or VPC level do not distinguish between approved and unapproved application instances the way a security group reference does.
  • Create a new network ACL for the application subnets that allows outbound TCP port 1521 only to the private IP addresses of the database servers in the peered VPC, and create a matching network ACL in the database subnets that allows inbound TCP port 1521 only from the entire application subnet range — Network ACLs apply at the subnet level, not at the individual instance level. Even though the rules can be narrowed to certain IP ranges or ports, they still do not provide the same workload-level granularity as security groups for a frequently changing EC2 fleet. This option is also operationally heavier because the engineer would need to keep the ACL logic aligned with the database IP layout and the application subnet ranges. It does not scale as cleanly as a peer security group reference for dynamic instances.
  • Create a new security group in the database VPC that allows TCP port 1521 from the CIDR block of the application VPC, and use an Auto Scaling lifecycle hook to dynamically add and remove temporary host routes for only the approved application instances whenever they launch or terminate — The database security group in this option still grants access from the entire application VPC CIDR, which is broader than the requirement. The added lifecycle-hook and route-management logic makes the design much more complex than necessary and does not actually solve the least-privilege issue at the packet-filtering layer. Routes are also not the right control for selecting which individual instances may talk to the database when VPC peering is already in place. Security groups are the intended mechanism for this type of selective access control between EC2 instances and database servers.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/security-group-rules.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/peering/vpc-peering-security-groups.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/peering/working-with-vpc-peering.html

questions.question_text:
A company discovers that several EC2 instances have been compromised and used to send phishing emails, resulting in an unexpected spike in AWS charges related to data transfer and bandwidth costs. The security team must detect future cost anomalies as early as possible to identify similar compromises before significant financial damage occurs.
Which approach enables the earliest detection of abnormal costs?
options: Review AWS Cost Explorer reports daily to identify spending patterns that deviate from historical averages
Create CloudWatch custom metrics for bandwidth usage and set static threshold-based alarms to notify on exceeding limits
Enable Cost Anomaly Detection with machine learning algorithms and configure individual SNS alerts for each detected anomaly

Create an AWS Budgets cost budget for the account, configure threshold alerts to send notifications to an Amazon SNS topic, and leverage budget threshold breaches to identify malicious usage as quickly as possible
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Enable Cost Anomaly Detection with machine learning algorithms and configure individual SNS alerts for each detected anomaly
AWS Cost Anomaly Detection uses machine learning algorithms trained on your historical cost patterns to identify spending that deviates significantly from normal behavior. The system monitors costs in real time and detects anomalies with minimal false positives by understanding day-of-week patterns, seasonal trends, and growth trajectories specific to your account. When an anomaly is detected, Cost Anomaly Detection sends immediate SNS alerts that provide context about the deviation, including the magnitude of the spike and services affected. This delivers notifications as soon as anomalies are detected, typically within hours of abnormal spending occurring, enabling rapid incident response before costs escalate further. Machine learning-based detection is superior to static thresholds because it adapts to legitimate cost changes and organization growth, reducing alert fatigue while maintaining sensitivity to genuine security incidents like compromised instances running unauthorized services.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/latest/userguide/manage-ad.html
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/latest/userguide/ad-SNS.html Incorrect Options:
  • Create CloudWatch custom metrics for bandwidth usage and set static threshold-based alarms to notify on exceeding limits — Static threshold-based alarms on bandwidth usage require administrators to predict appropriate limits in advance and cannot adapt to changing network patterns or legitimate usage growth. Thresholds set too low generate excessive false positives, while thresholds set too high might miss actual anomalies. Static alarms lack the intelligence to distinguish between authorized changes and security incidents, and this approach would not effectively detect cost spikes from novel attack vectors using previously unused services.
  • Review AWS Cost Explorer reports daily to identify spending patterns that deviate from historical averages — Manual daily review of Cost Explorer reports means detection is delayed by a full day at minimum, as anomalies that occur overnight would not be noticed until the following business day. By the time a cost spike is manually identified, significant charges could have accumulated. This reactive approach prioritizes convenience over security and fails to meet the requirement for earliest possible detection of abnormal costs.
  • Create an AWS Budgets cost budget for the account, configure threshold alerts to send notifications to an Amazon SNS topic, and leverage budget threshold breaches to identify malicious usage as quickly as possible — AWS Budgets can send alerts when actual or forecasted cost crosses thresholds, and the billing data Budgets uses is updated at least once per day. That makes it useful for governance, but not the earliest service for anomaly detection. This option is also less adaptive than anomaly detection because it depends on fixed thresholds rather than learned expected spending patterns. As a result, it is not as effective as Cost Anomaly Detection for catching unexpected malicious cost spikes early.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/latest/userguide/manage-ad.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/latest/userguide/ad-SNS.html https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws-cloud-financial-management/faster-anomaly-resolution-with-enhanced-root-cause-analysis-in-aws-cost-anomaly-detection/

questions.question_text:
A company wants to integrate AWS IAM Identity Center with an external identity provider (IdP) to centralize authentication and enable automated user provisioning.
Select and arrange the necessary steps from the list below. You may use each step one time or not at all:
1. Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source
2. Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center
3. Create an IAM role with a trust policy that references the external IdP endpoint
4. Enable automated user provisioning in IAM Identity Center
5. Enable automated provisioning capabilities in the external IdP
6. Retrieve the SAML metadata document from the external IdP
Which sequence of steps should a security engineer implement to meet these requirements?
options: Step 1: Create an IAM role with a trust policy that references the external IdP endpoint -> Step 2: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 3: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source

Step 1: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source -> Step 2: Enable automated user provisioning in IAM Identity Center -> Step 3: Enable automated provisioning capabilities in the external IdP

Step 1: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 2: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from the external IdP -> Step 3: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source

Step 1: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 2: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source -> Step 3: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from the external IdP
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Step 1: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 2: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from the external IdP -> Step 3: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source
The correct sequence begins with retrieving the SAML metadata from IAM Identity Center. This metadata is required by the external IdP to configure AWS as a trusted service provider and establish the federation endpoints. Next, retrieving the SAML metadata from the external IdP allows IAM Identity Center to obtain the necessary identity provider details, including certificates and assertion endpoints. This bidirectional metadata exchange is required to establish mutual trust. Finally, configuring IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source completes the federation setup. At this stage, both sides trust each other, and authentication requests can be securely processed through the external IdP. Incorrect Options:
  • Step 1: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source -> Step 2: Enable automated user provisioning in IAM Identity Center -> Step 3: Enable automated provisioning capabilities in the external IdP — This sequence attempts to enable provisioning before establishing SAML federation. Provisioning depends on a valid identity source configuration. Without completing metadata exchange and trust establishment, IAM Identity Center cannot synchronize users or authenticate requests.
  • Step 1: Create an IAM role with a trust policy that references the external IdP endpoint -> Step 2: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 3: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source — Creating an IAM role with a trust policy is not part of integrating IAM Identity Center with an external IdP. IAM Identity Center handles federation independently and does not require manually created IAM roles for SAML-based authentication. This step introduces unnecessary complexity and does not contribute to the solution.
  • Step 1: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from IAM Identity Center -> Step 2: Configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP as the identity source -> Step 3: Retrieve the SAML metadata document from the external IdP — This sequence is incorrect because it tries to configure IAM Identity Center to use the external IdP before IAM Identity Center has the IdP’s metadata. For SAML federation to work, both sides must exchange metadata first so they can trust each other. Without the IdP metadata, IAM Identity Center does not have the necessary information such as certificates and endpoints to validate authentication responses, so the setup remains incomplete and will not function correctly.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/manage-your-identity-source.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/gs-okta.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/provision-automatically.html

questions.question_text:
A company provides a mobile application that uses the MQTT protocol to connect to AWS IoT Core. The application subscribes to client-specific topics. Recently, the company identified an attack pattern where malicious applications impersonate legitimate clients by manipulating the client ID with injected characters to access unauthorized topics. A security engineer must implement controls to ensure that devices can connect only by using valid identities and cannot escalate privileges by altering the client ID.
Which combination of actions should the security engineer implement to mitigate this threat in the most operationally efficient manner? (Select two)
options: Attach an AWS IoT Core policy that permits the iot:Connect action on the resource client/${iot:ClientId}
Modify the application to use the AWS IoT thing name as the MQTT client ID when establishing a connection to AWS IoT Core

Implement AWS IoT Core custom authorizers backed by an AWS Lambda function that validates incoming client ID values against a regular expression pattern at connection time, rejecting any MQTT connection whose client ID contains special characters or does not correspond to a registered IoT thing name in the device registry
Configure an AWS IoT Core policy that allows iot:Subscribe on topic filters by using wildcards such as topicfilter/* to simplify topic access management across all devices
Attach an AWS IoT Core policy that permits the iot:Connect action on the resource client/${iot:Connection.Thing.ThingName}
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Modify the application to use the AWS IoT thing name as the MQTT client ID when establishing a connection to AWS IoT Core
Using the AWS IoT thing name as the MQTT client ID creates a strong binding between the device identity and the connection identifier. This ensures that each device connects with a predictable and controlled identifier that is registered in AWS IoT Core. By enforcing the use of thing names, the company prevents attackers from arbitrarily crafting client IDs with injected characters. This aligns the client identity with the AWS IoT registry and reduces the risk of unauthorized topic access due to manipulated identifiers.
  • Attach an AWS IoT Core policy that permits the iot:Connect action on the resource client/${iot:Connection.Thing.ThingName}
    • Using an IoT policy that allows the iot:Connect action only when the client ID matches the thing name enforces identity-based access control. The policy variable ${iot:Connection.Thing.ThingName} ensures that the connection request is evaluated against the registered thing name. This prevents a device from connecting with a spoofed or modified client ID. Even if an attacker attempts to inject special characters, the policy evaluation fails because the client ID does not match the associated thing name, effectively mitigating the threat. Incorrect Options:
    • Configure an AWS IoT Core policy that allows iot:Subscribe on topic filters by using wildcards such as topicfilter/* to simplify topic access management across all devices — Allowing broad topic access using wildcard filters increases the attack surface and weakens authorization controls. This approach does not restrict client identity or prevent misuse of client IDs. Instead, it makes it easier for attackers to access unintended topics.
    • Implement AWS IoT Core custom authorizers backed by an AWS Lambda function that validates incoming client ID values against a regular expression pattern at connection time, rejecting any MQTT connection whose client ID contains special characters or does not correspond to a registered IoT thing name in the device registry — AWS IoT Core custom authorizers can perform token-based or header-based authentication for devices that cannot use X.509 certificates. However, implementing a custom authorizer that validates client ID format with a Lambda function introduces additional latency on every connection request and adds an operational dependency on Lambda function availability and cold start behavior. The purpose-built iot:Connection.Thing.ThingName policy variable achieves the same client ID binding enforcement natively within the standard IoT Core policy engine without any custom code.
    Custom authorizers are intended for scenarios where standard AWS IoT authentication mechanisms are insufficient, such as when connecting devices that use bearer tokens instead of X.509 certificates. For devices that already use X.509 certificate-based authentication, policy variables provide a simpler and more reliable enforcement mechanism.
  • Attach an AWS IoT Core policy that permits the iot:Connect action on the resource client/${iot:ClientId} — Allowing iot:Connect on client/${iot:ClientId} permits any client ID that the device presents during connection.
    • This does not enforce a binding between the client ID and the registered thing. As a result, attackers can still manipulate the client ID to access unauthorized topics, making this option insufficient. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/iot-policies.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/connect-policy.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/thing-policy-variables.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/security-best-practices.html

questions.question_text:
A logistics company deploys a containerized application on Amazon ECS. The application retrieves configuration files and reference data from a specific Amazon S3 bucket. A security engineer attached an IAM policy to the ECS task execution role that should grant the application read access to the bucket. However, whenever the application attempts to fetch objects from S3, it receives AccessDenied errors.
A security engineer must resolve this issue without reducing the security of the S3 bucket or the application.
Which solution will meet these requirements?
options: Replace the IAM policy attached to the task execution role with an inline policy that grants s3:* on all resources. Then use CloudTrail logs to identify the actions the application actually uses and progressively restrict the inline policy over time

Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to inspect and validate the IAM policy attached to the task execution role. Verify that the policy grants the required S3 permissions. Confirm that the containerized application successfully assumes the task execution role during the container startup sequence

Create an Amazon S3 VPC endpoint in the VPC where the ECS tasks run. Configure the endpoint policy to explicitly allow s3:GetObject, s3:GetObjectVersion, and s3:ListBucket actions for the task execution role on the target bucket ARN. Enable VPC Flow Logs and CloudTrail data event logging to verify that all S3 requests from the ECS tasks route successfully through the VPC endpoint
Disable the S3 Block Public Access feature on the target S3 bucket. Review AWS CloudTrail logs to determine whether the task execution role is being assumed correctly by the application
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to inspect and validate the IAM policy attached to the task execution role. Verify that the policy grants the required S3 permissions. Confirm that the containerized application successfully assumes the task execution role during the container startup sequence
AWS IAM Access Analyzer provides policy validation that checks IAM policies against AWS best practices and policy grammar rules. When an application receives AccessDenied errors, the most common root causes are that the IAM policy does not grant the required actions or resources, the application is not successfully assuming the intended role, or a resource-based policy on the target resource explicitly denies access. Access Analyzer can identify policy misconfigurations such as missing actions, incorrect resource ARNs, or overly restrictive condition keys. Validating the IAM policy through Access Analyzer and confirming role assumption addresses the two most probable root causes without making any changes to the S3 bucket configuration. If the policy is incorrectly scoped, Access Analyzer highlights the exact issue with actionable findings. If the application is not assuming the task execution role at all, that becomes visible during investigation through CloudTrail or ECS task metadata analysis. This approach is methodical and preserves the existing security posture of both the bucket and the application. Correct diagnostic workflows start with understanding the problem before making changes. Rather than immediately adding bucket-level permissions or modifying the bucket's access configuration, using Access Analyzer to validate the policy and verifying role assumption respects the least-privilege principle. After confirming the root cause, the engineer applies a targeted fix to the IAM policy without weakening the bucket's security configuration, fully meeting the stated constraints. Incorrect Options:
  • Replace the IAM policy attached to the task execution role with an inline policy that grants s3:* on all resources. Then use CloudTrail logs to identify the actions the application actually uses and progressively restrict the inline policy over time — Granting s3:* on all resources gives the ECS task execution role full S3 access across every bucket in the account, not just the target bucket. This is a serious security violation that directly contradicts the requirement not to decrease security. Even with the intent to tighten permissions later, granting overly broad permissions as a temporary troubleshooting measure introduces unacceptable risk in a production environment.
Replacing a managed policy with an inline policy also changes the permission model and makes the configuration harder to audit and manage at scale. The broad-access interim period, even if brief, is operationally and compliance-wise unacceptable.
  • Create an Amazon S3 VPC endpoint in the VPC where the ECS tasks run. Configure the endpoint policy to explicitly allow s3:GetObject, s3:GetObjectVersion, and s3:ListBucket actions for the task execution role on the target bucket ARN. Enable VPC Flow Logs and CloudTrail data event logging to verify that all S3 requests from the ECS tasks route successfully through the VPC endpoint — An S3 VPC endpoint routes traffic from the VPC to S3 through the AWS private network rather than through the internet, and the endpoint policy controls which requests through the endpoint are permitted. However, creating a VPC endpoint and configuring its policy does not resolve an AccessDenied error caused by a missing or misconfigured IAM policy on the task execution role. The IAM policy evaluation and the endpoint policy are additive controls; both must allow the action for the request to succeed.
    • If the task execution role's IAM policy does not grant the required S3 actions, the AccessDenied error persists regardless of the VPC endpoint configuration. This option adds observability through VPC Flow Logs and CloudTrail, but it also potentially expands the scope of S3 access granted through the endpoint policy, reducing security rather than preserving it.
  • Disable the S3 Block Public Access feature on the target S3 bucket. Review AWS CloudTrail logs to determine whether the task execution role is being assumed correctly by the application — Disabling S3 Block Public Access explicitly reduces the security posture of the S3 bucket, which the question prohibits. S3 Block Public Access prevents public ACLs and public bucket policies from making the bucket accessible to unauthenticated users. Disabling it for a bucket storing sensitive logistics data introduces unnecessary risk. The application uses IAM role-based authentication, so Block Public Access does not affect authenticated API calls and is not related to the AccessDenied error.
    • Reviewing CloudTrail logs is a valid diagnostic step, but it must not be combined with a reduction in bucket security. CloudTrail analysis should be performed without altering any bucket configuration settings. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/what-is-access-analyzer.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/troubleshoot_access-denied.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_execution_IAM_role.html

questions.question_text:
An IAM user receives an Access Denied error when trying to retrieve objects from an Amazon S3 bucket. The IAM user and the S3 bucket are in the same AWS account. The bucket uses server-side encryption with AWS KMS keys and encrypts all objects at rest by using a customer managed KMS key from the same account. The bucket does not have a bucket policy. The IAM user has an IAM policy that allows s3:List* and s3:Get* on the bucket and its objects. The same IAM policy also allows kms:Decrypt on the customer managed KMS key. A security engineer must determine a possible reason why the IAM user still cannot access the objects.
Which explanation is most likely correct?
options: The Amazon S3 bucket requires a bucket policy that explicitly allows the IAM user to access the objects, even when the user and the bucket are in the same AWS account
The customer managed KMS key policy was changed so that the AWS account no longer has permission to use IAM policies to control access to the key
The IAM policy must also allow the kms:DescribeKey permission for the customer managed KMS key before the user can retrieve SSE-KMS encrypted objects from Amazon S3
The IAM user also needs the kms:GenerateDataKey permission on the customer managed KMS key to retrieve existing SSE-KMS encrypted objects from Amazon S3
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • The customer managed KMS key policy was changed so that the AWS account no longer has permission to use IAM policies to control access to the key
For an IAM principal to use a customer managed KMS key, AWS KMS authorization depends on both the IAM policy and the KMS key policy. AWS documents that unless the key policy allows the account to use IAM policies for access control, permissions granted only through IAM policies are not sufficient to use the key. If the key policy was edited to remove the account-level permission that enables IAM policy delegation, an IAM user can have kms:Decrypt in IAM and still receive Access Denied. This fits the scenario precisely. The user already has s3:Get*, s3:List*, and kms:Decrypt in IAM, and there is no bucket policy denying access. A broken or restrictive KMS key policy is therefore a realistic reason the user still cannot access the SSE-KMS encrypted objects. Amazon S3 needs to use the KMS key on behalf of the requester during retrieval, and if the key policy does not allow that authorization path, the request fails. This is a common AWS exam trap. Many learners assume IAM permissions alone are enough for a customer managed KMS key, but KMS key policy evaluation is critical. In same-account scenarios, the default key policy usually enables IAM policies to control access. If that default behavior has been removed, access breaks even when the IAM policy appears correct. Incorrect Options:
  • The IAM policy must also allow the kms:DescribeKey permission for the customer managed KMS key before the user can retrieve SSE-KMS encrypted objects from Amazon S3 — kms:DescribeKey can be useful for viewing metadata about a KMS key, but it is not the core permission required to retrieve an existing SSE-KMS encrypted object from Amazon S3. The essential KMS permission for reading such an object is kms:Decrypt. Since the scenario already grants kms:Decrypt, adding kms:DescribeKey is not the most likely missing permission. A failure caused by key policy configuration is much more plausible than the absence of a metadata-read permission.
  • The IAM user also needs the kms:GenerateDataKey permission on the customer managed KMS key to retrieve existing SSE-KMS encrypted objects from Amazon S3 — kms:GenerateDataKey is typically needed when new data keys must be generated for encryption operations. Retrieving an existing SSE-KMS encrypted object from Amazon S3 does not normally require the IAM user to generate a new data key. The critical read path permission is kms:Decrypt, which the user already has in IAM. Therefore, the absence of kms:GenerateDataKey is not the most likely reason for the Access Denied error in this scenario.
  • The Amazon S3 bucket requires a bucket policy that explicitly allows the IAM user to access the objects, even when the user and the bucket are in the same AWS account — An S3 bucket policy is not required for same-account access when IAM identity-based policies already allow the relevant S3 actions and there is no explicit deny. In this scenario, the user already has s3:List* and s3:Get* permissions in IAM, so the absence of a bucket policy is not itself a problem. Bucket policies are often used for cross-account access or for additional restrictions, but they are not mandatory here. Since the bucket has no bucket policy, there is no bucket-policy-based deny to explain the failure.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policies.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/control-access.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingKMSEncryption.html

options: Add a second bucket policy statement with Effect Allow, Action s3:ListBucket, and Resource arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET, because the s3:GetObject permission requires a prior successful bucket-level listing call to enumerate object keys before the Lambda runtime can retrieve any individual objects from the bucket
Change the Resource element to arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET/* to target the objects within the bucket rather than the bucket itself
Remove the Condition element and change the Principal to specify the Lambda function's IAM execution role ARN instead of the Lambda service principal

Remove the ArnLike condition block and replace it with a StringEquals condition using the aws:SourceAccount key set to the 12-digit AWS account ID to broaden the permission scope beyond a single function ARN
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Change the Resource element to arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET/* to target the objects within the bucket rather than the bucket itself
In Amazon S3, bucket ARNs and object ARNs are distinct resources that require separate permission grants. The bucket ARN format arn:aws:s3:::BUCKET-NAME refers to the bucket resource itself, while the object ARN format arn:aws:s3:::BUCKET-NAME/* refers to all objects within that bucket. The s3:GetObject action applies to objects, not to the bucket. When the Resource element specifies only the bucket ARN without the trailing /*, the policy grants s3:GetObject on a resource type that the action does not support, which causes the permission evaluation to fail. Changing the Resource element to arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET/* corrects this mismatch by pointing the permission at the actual resource type that s3:GetObject requires. With this change, the policy allows the Lambda service principal to call s3:GetObject for any object within the bucket, subject to the ArnLike condition that restricts the caller to the DataProcessingFunction Lambda function ARN. The remaining elements of the policy are correctly configured. The Service principal lambda.amazonaws.com is the appropriate principal for a bucket policy that grants S3 access to a Lambda function. The ArnLike condition using aws:SourceArn scopes the permission to the specific function, which follows the principle of least privilege for cross-service access controls. Incorrect Options:
  • Remove the Condition element and change the Principal to specify the Lambda function's IAM execution role ARN instead of the Lambda service principal — Removing the Condition element and switching the Principal to the Lambda function's IAM execution role ARN is one valid approach to granting S3 access to a Lambda function, but it is not the minimal change required to fix the existing policy. The current policy uses the Lambda service principal with a source ARN condition, which is a supported pattern. The root cause of the failure is the Resource element not including the /*, not the principal configuration.
Using the IAM role ARN as the principal in a bucket policy is an alternative design, not a correction to the identified error. The least disruptive fix is to adjust the Resource element to match the resource type required by the s3:GetObject action.
  • Add a second bucket policy statement with Effect Allow, Action s3:ListBucket, and Resource arn:aws:s3:::ANALYSIS-DATA-BUCKET, because the s3:GetObject permission requires a prior successful bucket-level listing call to enumerate object keys before the Lambda runtime can retrieve any individual objects from the bucket — The s3:ListBucket permission is used for operations that list the objects in a bucket, such as the ListObjectsV2 API call. It is not a prerequisite for s3:GetObject when the application already knows the object key. The Lambda function calling s3:GetObject with a specific object key does not require a preceding ListBucket call. Adding a ListBucket statement does not fix the Resource element error that is causing the current failure.
    • Even if ListBucket were needed for the application's logic, granting it without fixing the Resource element for s3:GetObject would still leave the original error unresolved. The s3:GetObject grant must reference object ARNs with the trailing /* to function correctly.
  • Remove the ArnLike condition block and replace it with a StringEquals condition using the aws:SourceAccount key set to the 12-digit AWS account ID to broaden the permission scope beyond a single function ARN — Replacing the ArnLike condition with aws:SourceAccount broadens the permission scope to allow any Lambda function in the account to access the bucket, rather than restricting access to the specific DataProcessingFunction. While this technically allows the function to access the bucket, it grants more access than necessary and violates the principle of least privilege. The existing ArnLike condition with the specific function ARN is the more precise and preferred design.
    • Furthermore, changing the condition block does not address the root cause of the failure. The policy fails because the Resource element references the bucket ARN instead of the object ARN. Modifying the condition does not resolve this resource-type mismatch. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/example-bucket-policies.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/access-policy-language-overview.html

questions.question_text:
A company runs a data processing application on Amazon EC2 instances inside a private VPC. The application retrieves objects from an Amazon S3 bucket. The S3 bucket stores sensitive data that is encrypted by using an AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) customer managed key. The VPC does not have internet connectivity. The EC2 instances access Amazon S3 by using a gateway VPC endpoint. The application recently started failing when attempting to retrieve objects, and the logs indicate AccessDenied errors. A security engineer must determine the possible causes of this issue.
Which factors could be responsible for this behavior? (Select three)
options: The EC2 instances and the customer managed KMS key reside in different AWS Regions, and cross-Region decryption operations are not supported by default when S3 access is routed through a gateway VPC endpoint that lacks explicit cross-Region request routing configuration
The KMS key policy that protects the S3 objects does not grant the kms:Decrypt permission to the IAM role used by the EC2 instances
The security group associated with the EC2 instances does not allow outbound HTTPS traffic on port 443, which prevents the instances from reaching Amazon S3 through the gateway VPC endpoint
The KMS key policy that protects the S3 objects does not grant the kms:ListKeys permission to the IAM role used by the EC2 instances
The S3 bucket policy does not permit requests that originate from the gateway VPC endpoint that the EC2 instances use
The KMS key policy does not allow the kms:GenerateDataKey action to the IAM role, which prevents Amazon S3 from retrieving the encrypted object
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • The KMS key policy that protects the S3 objects does not grant the kms:Decrypt permission to the IAM role used by the EC2 instances
When Amazon S3 objects are encrypted with a customer managed AWS KMS key, the requesting principal must have permission to decrypt the object. The kms:Decrypt permission allows Amazon S3 to use the key on behalf of the caller during a GetObject operation. If the IAM role associated with the EC2 instances does not have kms:Decrypt permission, Amazon S3 cannot decrypt the object before returning it. This results in an AccessDenied error even when the role has the correct S3 permissions. This is a direct and common cause of access failures when working with KMS-encrypted S3 objects.
  • The S3 bucket policy does not permit requests that originate from the gateway VPC endpoint that the EC2 instances use
    • When a gateway VPC endpoint is used to access Amazon S3, both the endpoint policy and the S3 bucket policy can influence authorization decisions. If the bucket policy includes conditions such as aws:SourceVpce and does not explicitly allow the VPC endpoint ID, requests from the EC2 instances are denied. This is often used to enforce private access to S3. However, if the bucket policy is misconfigured or does not match the endpoint being used, valid requests from within the VPC can still fail with AccessDenied errors. Therefore, a restrictive or incorrect bucket policy tied to the VPC endpoint is a valid cause of the issue.
  • The security group associated with the EC2 instances does not allow outbound HTTPS traffic on port 443, which prevents the instances from reaching Amazon S3 through the gateway VPC endpoint
    • Even when using a gateway VPC endpoint, EC2 instances must initiate outbound HTTPS traffic to communicate with Amazon S3. Security groups control outbound traffic from the instance. If the security group does not allow outbound traffic on port 443, the instance cannot establish a connection to Amazon S3 through the endpoint. This results in failed requests that can appear as access-related errors at the application level. Proper outbound rules are required to ensure connectivity, making this a valid factor in the observed failure. Incorrect Options:
    • The KMS key policy does not allow the kms:GenerateDataKey action to the IAM role, which prevents Amazon S3 from retrieving the encrypted object — The kms:GenerateDataKey permission is required when generating new data encryption keys, typically during encryption operations. For retrieving existing objects, Amazon S3 requires only kms:Decrypt. The absence of kms:GenerateDataKey does not prevent decryption of existing data. Therefore, this option does not explain the issue.
    • The EC2 instances and the customer managed KMS key reside in different AWS Regions, and cross-Region decryption operations are not supported by default when S3 access is routed through a gateway VPC endpoint that lacks explicit cross-Region request routing configuration — AWS KMS keys are Regional resources, and S3 SSE-KMS encryption uses a key in the same Region as the S3 bucket. If the EC2 instances and the S3 bucket are in the same Region, the KMS key used for encryption is also in that Region, so there is no cross-Region KMS operation involved. Gateway VPC endpoints are Region-specific and forward requests to S3 endpoints within the same Region. Cross-Region scenarios are not relevant when the instances, bucket, and KMS key all reside in the same Region, as implied by the standard architecture described.
    • The KMS key policy that protects the S3 objects does not grant the kms:ListKeys permission to the IAM role used by the EC2 instances — The kms:ListKeys permission allows a principal to list KMS keys in an account. It does not grant permission to use a key for encryption or decryption operations. Retrieving encrypted objects requires kms:Decrypt. Even without kms:ListKeys, a principal can use a specific key if granted the appropriate permissions. Therefore, this option does not cause the issue.
    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingKMSEncryption.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policies.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/vpc-endpoints-s3.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/example-bucket-policies.html

questions.question_text:
A company reviews its password standards and discovers that no minimum password length is enforced for two identity systems used by its workforce and applications. The first identity system uses AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) federation with an on-premises Active Directory environment. The second identity system uses Amazon Cognito user pools as the user directory for a custom cloud application.
A security engineer must implement a required minimum password length for both identity systems while keeping password enforcement aligned with the system that actually manages the user credentials.
Which combination of actions should the security engineer take to meet these requirements? (Select two)
options: Enable AWS IAM Identity Center password requirements and use those settings to propagate a minimum password length to the federated Active Directory users and the Amazon Cognito user pools
Update the account password policy in AWS Identity and Access Management so that federated users and Amazon Cognito users must use longer passwords
Modify the password policy settings for the Amazon Cognito user pools so that the application users must meet the new minimum password length
Modify the password policy in the on-premises Active Directory environment so that the federated users must meet the new minimum password length
Configure an AWS Config rule to check for password policy compliance in IAM and Cognito, and rely on the rule to block users from setting passwords that are shorter than the required minimum
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Modify the password policy settings for the Amazon Cognito user pools so that the application users must meet the new minimum password length
Amazon Cognito user pools maintain their own password policy settings for the users stored in the user pool. Because the custom cloud application uses Cognito user pools as its user database, the minimum password length for those application users must be enforced directly in the Cognito configuration. Updating the Cognito password policy is the correct control point because Cognito is the system of record for those credentials. This allows the company to enforce minimum password length where the passwords are created, stored, and validated for the application.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/managing-users-passwords.html
  • Modify the password policy in the on-premises Active Directory environment so that the federated users must meet the new minimum password length
    • For users who authenticate through IAM federation with an on-premises Active Directory, the passwords are not managed in IAM. The external directory remains the authoritative identity source and performs the password validation. Because Active Directory manages those user credentials, the minimum password length must be enforced in the on-premises Active Directory password policy. This ensures the federated users are subject to the required standard before authentication assertions are sent to AWS. Incorrect Options:
    • Update the account password policy in AWS Identity and Access Management so that federated users and Amazon Cognito users must use longer passwords — The IAM account password policy applies only to IAM users that exist in the AWS account. It does not control password requirements for federated users who authenticate through an external directory, and it does not control Amazon Cognito user pool passwords. As a result, updating the IAM password policy would not solve the stated problem for either of the two identity systems in the scenario. It targets the wrong credential stores.
    • Configure an AWS Config rule to check for password policy compliance in IAM and Cognito, and rely on the rule to block users from setting passwords that are shorter than the required minimum — AWS Config can evaluate whether certain resource configurations comply with desired settings, and there are managed rules for IAM and Cognito password policy checks. However, Config is primarily a detective compliance service, not a preventative password enforcement mechanism. Using AWS Config can help the company identify that a password policy is too weak, but it does not itself block a user from setting a short password. The enforcement still must happen in Active Directory and Cognito.
    • Enable AWS IAM Identity Center password requirements and use those settings to propagate a minimum password length to the federated Active Directory users and the Amazon Cognito user pools — AWS IAM Identity Center has its own password requirements only when it is the identity source. In this scenario, the company uses on-premises Active Directory for federated users and Amazon Cognito user pools for the application users. Because IAM Identity Center is not the authoritative identity source for either set of users described in the scenario, its password settings would not propagate to those systems. This option applies controls in the wrong place.
    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/managing-users-passwords.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito-user-identity-pools/latest/APIReference/API_PasswordPolicyType.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_passwords_account-policy.html

questions.question_text:
A financial services firm must protect critical records stored in Amazon S3 from permanent deletion to meet regulatory requirements. The firm must also replicate the records from its primary AWS Region to a secondary Region to satisfy disaster recovery obligations. The solution must ensure that even users with administrator access cannot permanently delete the records in the secondary Region.
Which solution will meet these requirements?
options: Enable S3 Object Lock in governance mode on the primary S3 bucket, configure S3 Cross-Region Replication to the secondary bucket, and create an IAM permission boundary policy that explicitly denies the s3:DeleteObject and s3:DeleteObjectVersion actions for all users including those with administrator access

Enable S3 Object Lock on the source bucket and apply legal holds to objects in the primary Region so that the objects cannot be deleted, and configure cross-Region replication to copy the protected objects to the destination bucket
Enable S3 Object Lock in compliance mode on the primary S3 bucket and configure S3 Cross-Region Replication to copy the protected objects to a bucket in the secondary Region
Configure AWS Backup to perform cross-Region S3 backups to a vault in the secondary Region and enable AWS Backup Vault Lock in governance mode on that vault
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Enable S3 Object Lock in compliance mode on the primary S3 bucket and configure S3 Cross-Region Replication to copy the protected objects to a bucket in the secondary Region
S3 Object Lock in compliance mode provides the strongest immutability guarantee available in Amazon S3. In compliance mode, no user including the AWS account root user and administrators can delete or overwrite a protected object version until its configured retention period expires. This directly satisfies the requirement that administrator access cannot permanently delete the data, because the S3 service itself enforces the retention constraint independently of IAM policies. When S3 Object Lock is active on the source bucket and Cross-Region Replication is configured, the Object Lock settings are preserved on replicated objects in the destination bucket. The destination bucket must also have Object Lock enabled and the replication configuration must include replica modification and Object Lock replication settings. Once replication is established, objects in the secondary Region carry the same compliance mode retention that prevents administrator deletion. The combination of compliance mode and Cross-Region Replication satisfies both stated requirements simultaneously. Compliance mode addresses the immutability requirement, and Cross-Region Replication satisfies the disaster recovery requirement by maintaining a geographically separate copy of the data. No additional IAM policies or custom bucket deny statements are needed because compliance mode retention is enforced at the S3 service layer.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-lock.html
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-lock.html Incorrect Options:
  • Configure AWS Backup to perform cross-Region S3 backups to a vault in the secondary Region and enable AWS Backup Vault Lock in governance mode on that vault — AWS Backup Vault Lock in governance mode does not prevent administrators from permanently deleting data. In governance mode, users who hold the backup:DeleteBackupVault permission can still remove the vault and its contents. Only vault lock in compliance mode prevents deletion by any user. Using governance mode therefore does not satisfy the requirement that administrator access cannot permanently delete the records.
Additionally, AWS Backup creates backup copies rather than maintaining continuously synchronized replicas. Recovery latency and operational overhead differ significantly from S3 Cross-Region Replication, which keeps a live, near-real-time copy in the destination Region.
  • Enable S3 Object Lock in governance mode on the primary S3 bucket, configure S3 Cross-Region Replication to the secondary bucket, and create an IAM permission boundary policy that explicitly denies the s3:DeleteObject and s3:DeleteObjectVersion actions for all users including those with administrator access — S3 Object Lock in governance mode allows users who hold the s3:BypassGovernanceRetention permission to override the retention period and delete protected objects. Account administrators can grant themselves this permission, which means governance mode does not provide the same immutability guarantee as compliance mode. Since the requirement states that administrator access cannot permanently delete the data, governance mode explicitly fails to meet this requirement.
    • An IAM permission boundary that denies delete actions is subject to the same limitation: it can be modified by any IAM administrator who holds the permissions to update permission boundaries. Because the goal is to prevent permanent deletion even for administrators, only S3 Object Lock compliance mode provides a service-enforced control that cannot be undone through IAM policy changes.
  • Enable S3 Object Lock on the source bucket and apply legal holds to objects in the primary Region so that the objects cannot be deleted, and configure cross-Region replication to copy the protected objects to the destination bucket — Applying legal holds in the primary Region can prevent deletion of objects in that Region as long as the legal hold remains in place. Legal holds do not have a fixed retention period and can be removed by users with the appropriate permissions, which means they do not provide the same level of enforcement as compliance mode.
    • https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/object-lock.html https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/storage/protecting-data-with-amazon-s3-object-lock/

questions.question_text:
A security team must immediately isolate a potentially compromised EC2 instance to prevent lateral movement while maintaining the ability to investigate the instance later. The team applied a network ACL to the target instance's subnet that denies all inbound and outbound traffic. However, the instance still responds to SSH requests and the security team can ping the instance from other hosts on the network. The initial containment measures are insufficient and the team requires a more comprehensive isolation solution.
What should the security team do to isolate the instance?
options: Stop the target instance, create an Amazon Machine Image of the instance, and relaunch the instance in a quarantine subnet that allows access only from the forensics team
Execute an AWS Systems Manager document that deploys host-level firewall rules on the instance to block all network communication while preserving forensic data
Remove the security group rule that allows port 22 inbound and reconfigure the instance to use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager instead of SSH
Create a network ACL on the target instance's subnet that places deny-all-inbound rules above all other rules at the top priority and deny-all-outbound rules above all other rules at the top priority
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Create a network ACL on the target instance's subnet that places deny-all-inbound rules above all other rules at the top priority and deny-all-outbound rules above all other rules at the top priority
Network ACLs are stateless layer 3 and 4 filtering mechanisms that operate on the subnet. Network ACL rules are evaluated in order from lowest rule number to highest, with processing stopping at the first matching rule. By placing explicit deny-all-inbound and deny-all-outbound rules at the top priority (rule numbers 1-2), all subsequent rules become unreachable for matching traffic. This creates a complete traffic isolation that blocks SSH, ICMP (ping), and all other protocols. The scenario describes SSH still responding and ping still succeeding despite an existing network ACL, which indicates the original ACL did not have deny rules at the top priority. Perhaps the original deny rules were numbered lower (higher values) than allow rules, making them unreachable. By explicitly creating properly-ordered deny rules at the top, the team ensures no traffic of any kind can reach or leave the instance at the network layer. This approach provides comprehensive isolation at the infrastructure level that is independent of instance configuration, making it impossible for malware or an attacker to circumvent through host-level configuration changes. The instance remains intact and accessible for later forensic investigation once approved by incident response leadership. Incorrect Options:
  • Stop the target instance, create an Amazon Machine Image of the instance, and relaunch the instance in a quarantine subnet that allows access only from the forensics team — Stopping and relaunching the instance in a quarantine subnet is disruptive and can alter the system before the investigation is complete. It also takes more effort and more time than necessary for the immediate isolation requirement. This option may be used later as part of a containment or rebuild strategy, but it is not the cleanest first action for isolating active network traffic in the current test scenario. The scenario points to the need for a network control that immediately blocks the active session.
  • Remove the security group rule that allows port 22 inbound and reconfigure the instance to use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager instead of SSH — Removing a single security group rule for port 22 does not prevent the instance from responding to SSH requests if the rule still exists elsewhere or if there are overlapping rules. Moreover, reconfiguring the instance to use Session Manager requires the instance to still have outbound network access to Systems Manager endpoints and EC2 Instance Metadata Service, which conflicts with the isolation requirement. This approach requires modifying the instance configuration rather than enforcing isolation at the network layer.
  • Execute an AWS Systems Manager document that deploys host-level firewall rules on the instance to block all network communication while preserving forensic data — Host-level firewall rules are maintained on the instance itself and can potentially be disabled, reconfigured, or bypassed by an attacker with sufficient privileges on the compromised instance. The scenario indicates the instance is potentially compromised, so relying on controls running on the compromised system is not reliable containment. Network-layer controls (security groups, network ACLs) cannot be modified or bypassed from within the guest OS, making them more trustworthy for isolation.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-network-acls.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/security.html

questions.question_text:
A media streaming platform deploys microservices as Docker containers running on Amazon ECS. The security team requires that all data in transit between streaming clients and backend containers must remain encrypted from origin to destination. The platform expects highly variable traffic loads with sharp spikes during live broadcast events.
Which solution provides the MOST scalability and LOWEST latency while satisfying the encryption requirement?
options: Configure a Network Load Balancer to terminate the incoming TLS session and then establish a new encrypted connection to each backend container
Configure a Network Load Balancer with a TCP listener to pass TLS traffic directly through to the ECS containers without terminating the encryption at the load balancer

Deploy an AWS Global Accelerator standard accelerator in front of the ECS containers. Configure Global Accelerator to perform TLS offloading at the nearest AWS edge location, then re-establish a separate encrypted tunnel from the edge location to each backend container using a certificate managed by AWS Certificate Manager
Place an AWS WAF web ACL configured for TLS inspection in front of the ECS containers to inspect and re-encrypt all incoming TLS traffic before forwarding requests to the backend containers
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Configure a Network Load Balancer with a TCP listener to pass TLS traffic directly through to the ECS containers without terminating the encryption at the load balancer
A Network Load Balancer (NLB) operating with a TCP listener passes network packets to registered targets without inspecting or altering the payload. When TLS traffic arrives at an NLB TCP listener, the NLB forwards the encrypted packets as-is to the backend containers. The containers perform TLS termination themselves, which means the encryption session runs end-to-end from the client to the container. No intermediate component decrypts and re-encrypts the traffic, satisfying the requirement for unbroken encryption from origin to destination. Network Load Balancers are designed for extreme performance and operate at Layer 4 of the OSI model. They handle millions of requests per second at ultra-low latency because they process TCP flows rather than HTTP requests. The NLB uses a flow-hash algorithm to route packets consistently to the same target for the duration of a connection, ensuring session continuity. It scales automatically to handle sudden traffic spikes such as those generated by live broadcast events, without any pre-warming or manual capacity management. Combining the NLB TCP pass-through capability with ECS containers that perform their own TLS termination achieves both the encryption requirement and the performance requirement simultaneously. The NLB handles connection routing and elastic scaling while the containers maintain the private keys and TLS session state. This is the recommended AWS pattern for high-throughput workloads that require unbroken encryption between clients and containerized backends. Listeners for your Network Load Balancers:
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/network/load-balancer-listeners.html Incorrect Options:
  • Configure a Network Load Balancer to terminate the incoming TLS session and then establish a new encrypted connection to each backend container — When an NLB terminates TLS, it decrypts incoming traffic at the load balancer and then establishes a separate TLS session to each backend target.
