This TLS bridging pattern breaks the unbroken encryption requirement because the NLB gains access to the plaintext payload between termination and re-encryption. The security requirement explicitly prohibits any intermediate component from decrypting the traffic.
Additionally, TLS termination at the NLB requires the load balancer to perform cryptographic operations and manage certificates, which adds processing overhead and latency compared to a pure TCP pass-through that simply routes packets without inspection.
  • Place an AWS WAF web ACL configured for TLS inspection in front of the ECS containers to inspect and re-encrypt all incoming TLS traffic before forwarding requests to the backend containers — AWS WAF is a web application firewall that filters and monitors HTTP and HTTPS requests based on rules such as SQL injection patterns and rate limits. WAF operates at Layer 7 and requires access to decrypted HTTP payloads to evaluate those rules. TLS inspection in WAF requires TLS termination, which breaks the unbroken encryption chain required by the security team. WAF is fundamentally incompatible with the stated encryption requirement.
    • AWS WAF also cannot distribute traffic across multiple containers; it must be deployed in front of a CloudFront distribution, an ALB, or an API Gateway endpoint, all of which also terminate TLS. Using WAF here does not meet either the encryption requirement or the scalability and latency requirements.
  • Deploy an AWS Global Accelerator standard accelerator in front of the ECS containers. Configure Global Accelerator to perform TLS offloading at the nearest AWS edge location, then re-establish a separate encrypted tunnel from the edge location to each backend container using a certificate managed by AWS Certificate Manager — AWS Global Accelerator routes traffic through the AWS global network backbone and can reduce latency by directing traffic to the nearest AWS edge location. However, Global Accelerator does not perform TLS offloading itself; it forwards traffic to a registered endpoint such as an NLB or ALB where termination occurs. Describing Global Accelerator as performing TLS termination at edge locations is technically inaccurate.
    • Even if Global Accelerator were layered in front of an NLB with a TCP listener, TLS offloading at an edge location and re-encryption to the backend would still break the unbroken encryption chain required by the security team. This option introduces an additional network hop and an additional termination point without satisfying the core requirement. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/network/introduction.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/network/load-balancer-listeners.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/load-balancer-types.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/network/load-balancer-target-groups.html

questions.question_text:
A security engineer uses Amazon Macie to analyze a large Amazon S3 data estate for sensitive information. The environment contains many S3 buckets and a large number of objects. The engineer needs a low-overhead way to identify which buckets are likely to contain sensitive data and then perform deeper analysis only on those buckets.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend to meet these requirements with the least administrative effort?
options: Configure AWS Security Hub to receive and aggregate all Macie findings from the buckets, then use Security Hub's insight queries to identify buckets with sensitive data and automatically trigger Lambda-based remediation workflows

Enable Amazon Macie automated sensitive data discovery so Macie continuously samples objects across the buckets, and then run targeted sensitive data discovery jobs for the buckets where Macie finds sensitive data

Create an AWS Config custom rule that inventories all S3 buckets, tags the buckets that are likely to contain regulated data based on bucket names, and then run Amazon Macie jobs only for buckets whose names match the rule logic
Create recurring Amazon Macie sensitive data discovery jobs for all buckets, export the findings into an Amazon DynamoDB table, and query the table to identify the buckets that require more analysis
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Enable Amazon Macie automated sensitive data discovery so Macie continuously samples objects across the buckets, and then run targeted sensitive data discovery jobs for the buckets where Macie finds sensitive data
Amazon Macie automated sensitive data discovery is designed for broad, ongoing visibility into where sensitive data might exist across an S3 data estate. AWS documents that automated sensitive data discovery continually evaluates bucket inventory and uses sampling techniques to identify and analyze representative objects in buckets. This gives the security engineer a low-overhead way to identify which buckets are likely to contain sensitive data without launching full scans everywhere. After Macie identifies buckets or objects that appear to contain sensitive data, the engineer can create targeted sensitive data discovery jobs for those specific buckets. AWS documents discovery jobs as the mechanism for more directed and thorough analysis of S3 buckets and objects. This two-step approach matches the requirement to first identify relevant buckets and then perform additional scanning only where needed. This is also the option with the least administrative overhead. It uses Macie’s native automated discovery capability for continuous sampling and then uses focused jobs only for buckets that warrant deeper inspection, rather than forcing the team to build event-driven orchestration or scan every bucket repeatedly.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd.html
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd-how-it-works.html via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd-how-it-works.html Incorrect Options:
  • Create an AWS Config custom rule that inventories all S3 buckets, tags the buckets that are likely to contain regulated data based on bucket names, and then run Amazon Macie jobs only for buckets whose names match the rule logic — Using bucket names as a heuristic for regulated or sensitive content is unreliable and can easily miss buckets that contain sensitive data but do not follow a naming convention. It also creates a custom governance workflow where Macie already provides native data-aware classification capabilities.
This option therefore shifts the solution away from actual content analysis and toward guesswork based on naming patterns. Since the company already uses Macie, the least-overhead and more accurate approach is to let Macie identify buckets with sensitive data based on sampled object analysis.
  • Configure AWS Security Hub to receive and aggregate all Macie findings from the buckets, then use Security Hub's insight queries to identify buckets with sensitive data and automatically trigger Lambda-based remediation workflows — AWS Security Hub aggregates findings from Macie and other services, but it does not reduce the scanning workload. If Macie is running full scans on all buckets before Security Hub ingests the findings, the cost and processing overhead remain the same as running Macie standalone. Security Hub is a finding aggregation and compliance tool, not a sampling or prioritization mechanism.
  • Create recurring Amazon Macie sensitive data discovery jobs for all buckets, export the findings into an Amazon DynamoDB table, and query the table to identify the buckets that require more analysis — Running recurring discovery jobs for all buckets defeats the requirement to first identify which buckets contain sensitive data and then do additional scanning only for those buckets. It increases cost and administrative effort because the engineer must maintain comprehensive jobs for the entire S3 estate. Although DynamoDB could store derived results, Macie already provides findings and discovery results natively. Adding a separate table for basic identification adds overhead without solving the problem more efficiently than automated sensitive data discovery plus targeted jobs.
    • https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/discovery-asdd-how-it-works.html

questions.question_text:
A financial technology company runs trading platform microservices on Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS). The company needs a solution to detect Kubernetes security risks by monitoring Amazon EKS audit logs as well as operating system events, network activity, and file system changes on EKS nodes. When risks are identified, the solution must send email alerts to the security team's mailing list.
Which solution will meet these requirements?
options: Activate AWS Security Hub and enable the security standards that include Amazon EKS controls, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to forward relevant Security Hub findings to the SNS topic

Configure AWS CloudTrail to capture all Kubernetes API server calls on the EKS cluster, build a CloudWatch Logs Insights query to identify suspicious API activity patterns, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and deploy an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule that triggers an AWS Lambda function every hour to execute the query and publish identified findings to the SNS topic

Activate Amazon GuardDuty, enable EKS Protection and Runtime Monitoring for the EKS cluster, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to route relevant GuardDuty findings to the SNS topic for email delivery

Enable Amazon Inspector for container image scanning, configure Amazon Detective to correlate EKS security events, set up Amazon CloudWatch log groups to capture EKS audit logs, and deploy an AWS Lambda function to process log events and dispatch email alerts to the security team
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Activate Amazon GuardDuty, enable EKS Protection and Runtime Monitoring for the EKS cluster, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to route relevant GuardDuty findings to the SNS topic for email delivery
Amazon GuardDuty provides purpose-built threat detection for Amazon EKS through two distinct protection features. EKS Audit Log Monitoring analyzes Kubernetes audit log events to detect suspicious activity such as unauthorized API calls, privilege escalation attempts, and anomalous access patterns within the cluster control plane. EKS Runtime Monitoring deploys a GuardDuty security agent on EKS nodes to collect operating system-level telemetry including process execution, network connections, and file system activity. Together, these two features address the complete monitoring scope specified in the requirements. Amazon EventBridge integrates natively with Amazon GuardDuty and generates events whenever GuardDuty produces a new finding. A single EventBridge rule can filter GuardDuty findings by severity or finding type and route them to an Amazon SNS topic. Configuring the security team's mailing list as an SNS email subscriber ensures that relevant findings trigger automatic email notifications to the team without requiring any custom code or additional infrastructure. This architecture uses fully managed AWS services with native integration, minimal configuration, and no custom development. GuardDuty handles the detection logic automatically, EventBridge handles the event routing, and SNS handles the email delivery, which together provide the complete monitoring and alerting capability described in the requirements.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/runtime-monitoring.html Incorrect Options:
  • Activate AWS Security Hub and enable the security standards that include Amazon EKS controls, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and configure an Amazon EventBridge rule to forward relevant Security Hub findings to the SNS topic — AWS Security Hub aggregates findings from multiple AWS security services and provides compliance dashboards based on security standards. While Security Hub includes some EKS-related controls within its security standards, these controls perform configuration compliance checks rather than real-time threat detection against audit logs and runtime events. Security Hub does not natively monitor EKS audit logs or collect operating system, network, and file system events from EKS nodes.
The requirements specifically call for monitoring EKS audit logs in addition to OS, networking, and file events, which requires GuardDuty EKS Protection and Runtime Monitoring. Security Hub alone cannot satisfy this monitoring scope even when EKS security standards are enabled.
  • Enable Amazon Inspector for container image scanning, configure Amazon Detective to correlate EKS security events, set up Amazon CloudWatch log groups to capture EKS audit logs, and deploy an AWS Lambda function to process log events and dispatch email alerts to the security team — Amazon Inspector scans container images for software vulnerabilities and does not perform runtime threat detection against EKS audit logs or OS-level events. Amazon Detective provides investigative capabilities for analyzing findings from GuardDuty, but it does not independently detect threats or generate findings from EKS logs. CloudWatch log groups can receive EKS audit logs, but processing those logs through a Lambda function to detect threats requires custom threat detection logic that duplicates the functionality already built into GuardDuty.
    • This approach combines multiple services without providing the comprehensive, native threat detection that GuardDuty EKS Protection and Runtime Monitoring offer. The operational complexity of building and maintaining custom threat detection logic is significantly higher than enabling GuardDuty's purpose-built EKS features.
  • Configure AWS CloudTrail to capture all Kubernetes API server calls on the EKS cluster, build a CloudWatch Logs Insights query to identify suspicious API activity patterns, create an Amazon SNS topic subscribed to the security team's mailing list, and deploy an Amazon EventBridge scheduled rule that triggers an AWS Lambda function every hour to execute the query and publish identified findings to the SNS topic — CloudTrail captures Kubernetes API server calls and can provide valuable audit trail data for EKS. However, this approach has significant gaps. A CloudWatch Logs Insights query run on a scheduled hourly basis introduces up to one hour of detection delay between when a threat occurs and when the security team is notified. Real-time threat detection requires continuous analysis, not periodic batch queries. Additionally, building a custom Lambda function to identify security threats in raw audit log data requires developing and maintaining custom detection rules that approximate the managed threat intelligence already built into GuardDuty.
    • The complexity of operating a custom threat detection pipeline, combined with the inherent latency of hourly scheduled execution, makes this approach significantly inferior to GuardDuty EKS Protection, which provides continuous real-time analysis using AWS-managed threat intelligence. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/runtime-monitoring.html

questions.question_text:
A company runs workloads in an AWS account and has Amazon GuardDuty enabled for threat detection. A security engineer notices unusual GuardDuty findings and wants to investigate a particular IAM role in more depth. The engineer needs a report that includes anomalous activity details and any indicators of compromise associated with that role.
Which solution should the security engineer use to meet these requirements?
options: Use AWS Audit Manager to create an assessment for the IAM role and generate an assessment report for the suspicious activity
Use Amazon Detective to investigate the IAM role and generate an investigation report that includes anomalous behavior and indicators of compromise
Use Amazon Inspector to run an on-demand scan of the IAM role and review the resulting report for indicators of compromise

Use AWS Security Hub to create a custom insight for the IAM role, export the matching findings, and rely on the insight output as the investigation report for anomalous behavior and compromise indicators
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use Amazon Detective to investigate the IAM role and generate an investigation report that includes anomalous behavior and indicators of compromise
Amazon Detective is the AWS service designed to help security teams analyze, investigate, and identify the root cause of suspicious activity and GuardDuty findings. AWS documents that Detective Investigations can be used to investigate IAM users and IAM roles by using indicators of compromise, and that the investigation summary highlights anomalous indicators that require attention. This makes Detective the best fit for the requirement because the engineer specifically wants to investigate an IAM role and generate a report that contains details about anomalous behavior and indicators of compromise. Detective is purpose-built for this type of investigation workflow rather than for compliance assessment or vulnerability scanning. Using Detective also minimizes operational effort because it provides a native investigative experience tied to security findings, instead of requiring the company to build a separate reporting and correlation pipeline.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/detective/latest/userguide/detective-investigation-about.html Incorrect Options:
  • Use AWS Audit Manager to create an assessment for the IAM role and generate an assessment report for the suspicious activity — AWS Audit Manager is designed for compliance assessments and evidence collection against audit frameworks. It helps organizations map controls and gather evidence for standards such as PCI DSS or CIS, but it is not an incident investigation service for anomalous IAM role behavior. This option does not align with the requirement to analyze indicators of compromise and unusual GuardDuty-related activity. Audit Manager focuses on governance and compliance reporting, not on threat investigation of a specific IAM role.
  • Use AWS Security Hub to create a custom insight for the IAM role, export the matching findings, and rely on the insight output as the investigation report for anomalous behavior and compromise indicators — AWS Security Hub can aggregate and normalize findings from multiple security services, and custom insights can help filter findings related to a particular IAM role. That makes Security Hub useful for centralized visibility and prioritization. However, an insight is not the same as an investigation report with detailed anomalous behavior analysis and indicators of compromise. Security Hub helps surface findings, but Amazon Detective is the more appropriate service for the investigative report requested in this scenario.
  • Use Amazon Inspector to run an on-demand scan of the IAM role and review the resulting report for indicators of compromise — Amazon Inspector on-demand scans are associated with supported code security or workload scanning use cases, not with investigating IAM roles for GuardDuty-style suspicious behavior. Even though this option sounds plausible because it mentions a report, Inspector is not the right service for analyzing IAM role anomalies or compromise indicators. The requirement is investigative, not vulnerability-oriented.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/detective/latest/userguide/detective-investigation-about.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/what-is-guardduty.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/audit-manager/latest/userguide/what-is.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/inspector/latest/user/what-is-inspector.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/what-is-securityhub-v2.html

questions.question_text:
A financial services organization operates 18 AWS accounts distributed across AWS Organizations. These accounts expose multiple REST APIs through Amazon API Gateway to support mobile applications and partner integrations. The organization must defend these APIs against common web-based attack patterns including SQL injection and sophisticated bot activity. The solution should require minimal ongoing tuning and administrative effort after deployment.
Which approach best meets these requirements?
options: Deploy AWS Firewall Manager with a centralized AWS WAF policy that incorporates the AWS Bot Control and AWS Managed Rules for SQL databases, then assign the policy scope to API Gateway stages across the organization

Leverage AWS Security Hub for detecting exposed API Gateway resources and configuring Amazon EventBridge to route findings to Lambda, then program Lambda functions to instantiate AWS WAF rules on unprotected resources and monitor WAF logs for attack attempts

Establish an AWS WAF web ACL independently for each API, incorporate managed rules for SQL injection and bot detection, use AWS Config to identify unprotected resources, and set up automated remediation via Lambda to apply WAF policies to new resources

Consolidate all APIs into a single Amazon CloudFront distribution with multiple custom origins representing each API Gateway endpoint, attach a unified AWS WAF web ACL to CloudFront, and route all traffic through the distribution to centralize threat protection across all 18 accounts
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Deploy AWS Firewall Manager with a centralized AWS WAF policy that incorporates the AWS Bot Control and AWS Managed Rules for SQL databases, then assign the policy scope to API Gateway stages across the organization
AWS Firewall Manager enables the creation of a single centralized WAF policy that is automatically distributed and enforced across all member accounts in the AWS Organizations hierarchy. The policy can incorporate AWS Managed Rules, including the Bot Control rule group for bot mitigation and the SQL database rule group for SQL injection protection. By setting the resource scope to API Gateway stages, Firewall Manager ensures that every current and future API Gateway stage across all 18 accounts automatically receives the same WAF protection without requiring individual configuration per stage. The critical advantage is that once the policy is created in Firewall Manager, new accounts that join the organization are automatically enrolled in the policy. New API Gateway stages created in existing accounts also inherit the policy without additional administrative action. This eliminates the operational burden of discovering unprotected resources and provisioning WAF rules reactively; protection is proactive and inherited by default. Firewall Manager also provides centralized rule management. Any updates to the Bot Control managed rules or SQL injection rules are applied uniformly across all 18 accounts from a single policy definition. This operational model minimizes tuning and maintenance overhead compared to managing 18 separate WAF configurations or relying on reactive remediation.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-bot-control.html Incorrect Options:
  • Establish an AWS WAF web ACL independently for each API, incorporate managed rules for SQL injection and bot detection, use AWS Config to identify unprotected resources, and set up automated remediation via Lambda to apply WAF policies to new resources — Creating individual WAF web ACLs for each API introduces significant operational overhead because a large API Gateway deployment could involve dozens or hundreds of APIs across 18 accounts. Reactive discovery and remediation via AWS Config and Lambda adds latency between the creation of a new API and the application of WAF protection. This approach requires ongoing management effort to monitor Config findings and debug Lambda remediation failures, and it does not automatically protect new accounts that join the organization.
  • Consolidate all APIs into a single Amazon CloudFront distribution with multiple custom origins representing each API Gateway endpoint, attach a unified AWS WAF web ACL to CloudFront, and route all traffic through the distribution to centralize threat protection across all 18 accounts — Routing all 18 accounts' APIs through a single CloudFront distribution creates a single point of failure and complicates multi-account architecture by centralizing traffic through one edge location. CloudFront adds latency compared to direct API Gateway access, and a single CloudFront distribution cannot represent the best architectural practice of distributed API ownership. Additionally, CloudFront is designed for content delivery, not for API aggregation, and this pattern complicates API endpoint discovery, access controls, and DNS routing for partner integrations.
  • Leverage AWS Security Hub for detecting exposed API Gateway resources and configuring Amazon EventBridge to route findings to Lambda, then program Lambda functions to instantiate AWS WAF rules on unprotected resources and monitor WAF logs for attack attempts — AWS Security Hub aggregates findings but does not provide a deployment or enforcement mechanism for WAF rules. Routing findings to EventBridge and Lambda introduces a delay between detection of an unprotected resource and the application of WAF protection. Lambda functions must be authored and tested to instantiate WAF rules, which increases complexity. Additionally, this reactive pattern leaves a window during which new APIs are unprotected while awaiting Lambda execution.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/what-is-aws-waf.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/aws-managed-rule-groups.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-bot-control.html

options: Amazon WorkMail and Amazon SES receive delegated AWS KMS permissions from the key policy and can use the customer managed key independently of the MailProcessingRole principal in account 777788889999

The kms:CreateGrant and kms:ListGrants actions in this policy extend grant management permissions to all IAM principals in the 777788889999 account when requests route through WorkMail or SES endpoints in us-east-1
The key policy allows Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES to perform cryptographic operations on behalf of any IAM role in the account because the policy uses a wildcard resource value
The customer managed key can be used only when the principal is MailProcessingRole and when the AWS KMS request is made through Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES in the us-east-1 Region
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • The customer managed key can be used only when the principal is MailProcessingRole and when the AWS KMS request is made through Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES in the us-east-1 Region
The key policy grants permissions to a specific principal, which is the IAM role arn:aws:iam::777788889999:role/MailProcessingRole. Those allowed permissions apply only when the request satisfies the kms:ViaService condition. That condition restricts the use of the key to requests that flow through Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES in the us-east-1 Region. This means the role does not receive unrestricted direct access to call AWS KMS for those operations in arbitrary contexts. Instead, AWS KMS authorizes the request only when the named role is the principal and when the service path matches one of the allowed integrated services. The condition narrows the usage pattern to service-mediated requests through WorkMail or SES. The policy therefore limits the customer managed key to a very specific access model. MailProcessingRole can use the key for the listed cryptographic and grant-related operations only through Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES in us-east-1. That is the precise effect of combining a named principal with a kms:ViaService condition in a key policy statement. Incorrect Options:
  • Amazon WorkMail and Amazon SES receive delegated AWS KMS permissions from the key policy and can use the customer managed key independently of the MailProcessingRole principal in account 777788889999 — The policy does not make Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES the principals that receive permission. The principal in the statement is the IAM role MailProcessingRole. The services appear only inside the kms:ViaService condition, which restricts how the principal can use the key. Because the services are not the principals in the policy, they do not gain independent authority to use the key outside the context of requests made on behalf of the allowed role. The statement controls usage by MailProcessingRole, not standalone service access delegated directly to WorkMail or SES.
  • The kms:CreateGrant and kms:ListGrants actions in this policy extend grant management permissions to all IAM principals in the 777788889999 account when requests route through WorkMail or SES endpoints in us-east-1 — The kms:CreateGrant and kms:ListGrants actions listed in this policy are subject to the identical principal and condition restrictions as every other action in the same policy statement. Only MailProcessingRole can invoke these actions, and only when the request routes through the WorkMail or SES endpoints in us-east-1. The policy does not extend any permissions to other IAM principals in the account. A policy statement applies its Principal, Condition, and Resource fields uniformly to all actions listed within that statement. There is no mechanism in this single-statement policy that provides broader scope to grant management actions than to the other listed cryptographic actions.
  • The key policy allows Amazon WorkMail or Amazon SES to perform cryptographic operations on behalf of any IAM role in the account because the policy uses a wildcard resource value — The wildcard resource value in a KMS key policy statement refers to the key resource that the policy is attached to, not to all customer managed keys in the account. In AWS KMS key policies, "Resource": "*" is commonly used and does not expand the permission scope to every key in the account. The statement also does not authorize any IAM role in the account. It names one specific principal, MailProcessingRole. Because both the principal scope and the service path are restricted, this option overstates the effect of the policy and incorrectly interprets the wildcard resource element.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policies.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/conditions-kms.html#conditions-kms-via-service https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/policy-conditions.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workmail/latest/adminguide/what_is.html

questions.question_text:
An organization stores sensitive customer data in Amazon S3 buckets and requires automated detection and reporting of personally identifiable information (PII) such as credit card numbers, social security numbers, and email addresses. The security team needs to identify sensitive data with minimal configuration effort and automatically alert the compliance team via SNS notifications whenever sensitive data is discovered.
Which solution requires the least operational effort to implement?
options: Implement Amazon Inspector scanning of S3 bucket policies and configure CloudWatch alarms to detect sensitive patterns
Configure AWS Config custom rules with Lambda functions to monitor S3 bucket contents and publish notifications to SNS topics
Use GuardDuty for threat detection, export CloudTrail S3 data events, analyze findings in CloudWatch, and trigger SNS notifications
Enable Amazon Macie with managed data identifiers and configure EventBridge to route Macie findings to SNS topics
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Enable Amazon Macie with managed data identifiers and configure EventBridge to route Macie findings to SNS topics
Amazon Macie is a fully managed data security service specifically designed to discover and classify sensitive data in AWS environments. Macie comes pre-loaded with over 200 managed data identifiers that can detect common sensitive data types like credit card numbers, social security numbers, email addresses, and other PII patterns without requiring custom configuration or regex pattern development. When Macie discovers sensitive data in S3 buckets, it generates findings that can be automatically routed through Amazon EventBridge to target services including SNS topics. This integration requires minimal setup: enable Macie, define an EventBridge rule that filters Macie findings, and connect it to your SNS topic. The entire solution is managed by AWS and requires no custom code development. This approach represents the lowest operational effort because there is no need to build and maintain Lambda scanning functions, write custom detection patterns, manage code deployments, or handle scale-related performance issues that come with custom solutions. Incorrect Options:
  • Configure AWS Config custom rules with Lambda functions to monitor S3 bucket contents and publish notifications to SNS topics — AWS Config is designed to monitor the configuration state of resources, not to analyze the actual contents of S3 objects. While Config custom rules with Lambda could technically scan object contents, this requires writing and maintaining custom Python code to detect PII patterns, managing execution permissions, handling S3 object pagination, and implementing custom SNS integration logic. This approach requires significantly more development and operational overhead compared to using a purpose-built service like Macie that already has comprehensive data identifier libraries.
  • Implement Amazon Inspector scanning of S3 bucket policies and configure CloudWatch alarms to detect sensitive patterns — Amazon Inspector is a vulnerability assessment service that scans EC2 instances and container images for software vulnerabilities, not a tool for discovering sensitive data in S3. Inspector cannot analyze S3 bucket contents for PII or other sensitive information patterns. S3 bucket policy monitoring through Config would only detect access control changes, not the presence of sensitive data, making this fundamentally unsuitable for the stated requirement.
  • Use GuardDuty for threat detection, export CloudTrail S3 data events, analyze findings in CloudWatch, and trigger SNS notifications — GuardDuty is designed to detect malicious threats and unusual account behavior, not to identify sensitive data content within S3 objects. While GuardDuty can monitor CloudTrail API events, this approach only detects who accessed data and when, not what sensitive data exists in the buckets. This solution would require additional manual analysis of CloudTrail logs in CloudWatch and would not accomplish the primary objective of automatically discovering sensitive data types within S3 objects.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/what-is-macie.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/managed-data-identifiers.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/macie/latest/user/macie-terms.html

questions.question_text:
A company observes that one of its Amazon EC2 instances has suddenly begun consuming an unusually high amount of CPU. The security team cannot yet determine whether the behavior is the result of a compromise or whether the operating system is running legitimate background maintenance tasks. Before beginning the investigation, a security engineer must preserve evidence and reduce the risk of accidental loss of the potentially affected resource.
Which combination of actions should the security engineer take first? (Select three)
options: Collect the EC2 instance metadata and apply a quarantine tag to the EC2 instance
Detach the IAM instance profile from the EC2 instance and terminate the instance after exporting the system logs to Amazon CloudWatch Logs
Turn off termination protection for the EC2 instance if the instance currently has API termination protection enabled
Restart the EC2 instance immediately so that background cleanup tasks finish and the CPU usage pattern returns to normal before evidence collection begins
Create snapshots of the Amazon EBS data volumes that are attached to the EC2 instance
Turn on termination protection for the EC2 instance if API termination protection is not already enabled
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Turn on termination protection for the EC2 instance if API termination protection is not already enabled
Enabling termination protection is an important first step when an EC2 instance might be involved in a security incident. It reduces the risk that the instance is accidentally terminated during containment or investigation activities, which would destroy valuable evidence and make forensic analysis more difficult. AWS documents that termination protection helps prevent accidental termination through the EC2 API or console. In an incident-response workflow, preserving the affected resource is critical, especially before investigators determine whether the activity is malicious or benign. By enabling this control early, the security engineer preserves the option to investigate the running instance safely without introducing unnecessary operational risk.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/security-ir/latest/userguide/collect-relevant-artifacts.html
  • Create snapshots of the Amazon EBS data volumes that are attached to the EC2 instance
    • Creating snapshots of the attached Amazon EBS volumes preserves the disk state of the instance at the time of suspicion. This is a core evidence-preservation step because the volume contents might contain logs, malware artifacts, persistence mechanisms, or modified files that are necessary for analysis. AWS incident response guidance emphasizes preserving resources, snapshots, and other evidence before making disruptive changes. EBS snapshots are a native and efficient way to capture the state of persistent storage for later forensic review or recovery. This step supports both investigation and recovery. Even if the instance later needs to be isolated or replaced, the preserved volume state remains available for detailed examination.
    via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/security-ir/latest/userguide/introduction.html
    via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/security-ir/latest/userguide/introduction.html
  • Collect the EC2 instance metadata and apply a quarantine tag to the EC2 instance
    • Capturing EC2 instance metadata is an early investigative step because it records important contextual information such as instance configuration, attached roles, networking details, and other environment attributes. This metadata can help investigators understand the scope of the issue and reconstruct the instance state. Applying a quarantine tag is also a practical preparation step because it marks the resource clearly for incident handling workflows. Tags can be used operationally to signal containment state, prevent confusion during response, and trigger automated controls in mature incident-response environments. Together, metadata collection and quarantine tagging help preserve context and support an organized response without immediately destroying or altering critical evidence. How to automate incident response in the AWS Cloud for EC2 instances:
    via - https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/how-to-automate-incident-response-in-aws-cloud-for-ec2-instances/ Incorrect Options:
    • Turn off termination protection for the EC2 instance if the instance currently has API termination protection enabled — Disabling termination protection increases the risk that the instance is accidentally terminated during the investigation. That is the opposite of what a security engineer should do when evidence preservation is a priority. Before investigators understand whether the instance is compromised, they should protect it from accidental deletion rather than making it easier to terminate. This option weakens the response posture instead of strengthening it.
    • Restart the EC2 instance immediately so that background cleanup tasks finish and the CPU usage pattern returns to normal before evidence collection begins — Restarting the instance immediately can destroy volatile evidence and change the runtime state before investigators understand what is happening. High CPU usage might be caused by malicious activity, and a restart can erase important clues in memory or alter the timing of system artifacts. Incident response begins with preservation and controlled containment, not with disruptive remediation actions performed before evidence capture. This option therefore introduces unnecessary risk.
    • Detach the IAM instance profile from the EC2 instance and terminate the instance after exporting the system logs to Amazon CloudWatch Logs — Detaching the instance profile and terminating the instance is far too disruptive as an initial action. Termination can destroy the running resource and remove valuable forensic context before the investigation is complete. Although role detachment and stronger containment might be appropriate later in a confirmed compromise scenario, the question asks what should be done before investigating. Preserving the resource is the priority at that stage, not terminating it.
    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/security-ir/latest/userguide/collect-relevant-artifacts.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/security-ir/latest/userguide/introduction.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/Using_ChangingDisableAPITermination.html https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/how-to-automate-incident-response-in-aws-cloud-for-ec2-instances/

questions.question_text: A retail organization manages an online catalog system across multiple Amazon Aurora database clusters. During a recent compliance audit, auditors identified several Aurora clusters without proper encryption in transit. The security team needs to enforce encrypted connections to all Aurora database endpoints. What is the recommended approach to enforce encryption requirements for all connections?
options: Deploy AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) to issue certificates for Aurora clusters and attach them to the database endpoints for SSL/TLS negotiation
Modify the Aurora cluster parameter group and set the require_secure_transport database parameter to the value ON
Enable Amazon Inspector on the Aurora cluster to scan for insecure connections and automatically revoke non-TLS sessions through EventBridge integration
Use AWS Secrets Manager to store encrypted database passwords, then create an IAM policy that allows applications to connect only via HTTPS endpoints
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Modify the Aurora cluster parameter group and set the require_secure_transport database parameter to the value ON
The require_secure_transport parameter is an Aurora cluster-level setting that rejects any connection attempt that does not use an encrypted protocol. When set to ON, the cluster validates that every incoming connection is wrapped in TLS and terminates any connection presented without encryption. This check happens at the database engine level, before any SQL queries are processed, ensuring that no plaintext traffic can reach the cluster regardless of the client configuration. This parameter controls the security behavior of the Aurora cluster itself, not the client side, which means the enforcement is centralized and cannot be bypassed by misconfiguring a single application. The parameter takes effect immediately after the cluster parameter group is updated, and the change applies to all new connections while existing connections are gracefully allowed to complete. This approach directly addresses the compliance requirement with minimal operational overhead because it requires only a parameter change and does not mandate any changes to application code, proxy infrastructure, or external services. The enforcement is transparent to well-behaved clients that already use TLS and blocks only clients that attempt unencrypted connections.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/AuroraUserGuide/AuroraMySQL.Security.html Incorrect Options:
  • Deploy AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) to issue certificates for Aurora clusters and attach them to the database endpoints for SSL/TLS negotiation — AWS Certificate Manager issues certificates and ACM can integrate with some AWS services, but Aurora does not directly attach ACM certificates to database endpoints. Aurora manages its own certificate infrastructure separately. ACM is designed for HTTPS load balancers, CloudFront distributions, and API Gateway stages, not for Aurora database encryption. Attempting to use ACM for Aurora encryption misrepresents the certificate service model and introduces unnecessary infrastructure without solving the actual requirement to force encrypted connections.
  • Use AWS Secrets Manager to store encrypted database passwords, then create an IAM policy that allows applications to connect only via HTTPS endpoints — AWS Secrets Manager stores credentials securely, and HTTPS endpoints require encryption for API calls to Secrets Manager, but storing passwords in Secrets Manager does not enforce encryption for the database connections themselves. An application that retrieves a password from Secrets Manager can still connect to the Aurora cluster without TLS if the cluster allows it. Secrets Manager provides credential protection but does not control the cluster's requirement for encrypted connections.
  • Enable Amazon Inspector on the Aurora cluster to scan for insecure connections and automatically revoke non-TLS sessions through EventBridge integration — Amazon Inspector scans for vulnerabilities and compliance issues but does not enforce TLS connections. Inspector can detect that non-TLS connections are occurring, but detection is not the same as enforcement. An alert from Inspector indicates a problem after the fact, whereas the require_secure_transport parameter prevents non-TLS connections from being accepted in the first place. This option provides visibility but not the preventive control needed to fully comply with the encryption in transit requirement.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/AuroraUserGuide/AuroraMySQL.Security.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/UsingWithRDS.SSL.html

questions.question_text:
A company recently contained and eradicated a security incident in its production environment. As part of recovery, a security engineer must restore an Amazon RDS database cluster to the last known good state. The DB cluster is configured with automated backups and a retention period of 28 days. The investigation confirms that the initial malicious activity began 3 days ago at exactly 2:25 PM. The security engineer must recover the cluster to the most recent safe point before the attack started.
Which solution should the security engineer implement?
options: Review snapshots for all Amazon RDS databases in the account, choose the snapshot whose timestamp is closest to 3 days ago at 2:24 PM, and restore that snapshot
Identify the source DB cluster and perform a point-in-time restore for the cluster by choosing a restore time of 3 days ago at 2:24 PM
Identify the source DB cluster, review the available DB cluster snapshots, and restore the snapshot whose creation time is closest to 3 days ago at 2:24 PM
Restore the latest automated backup for the DB cluster, and then use database transaction logs inside the restored cluster to manually reverse changes that occurred after 3 days ago at 2:25 PM
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Identify the source DB cluster and perform a point-in-time restore for the cluster by choosing a restore time of 3 days ago at 2:24 PM
Amazon RDS DB clusters that have automated backups enabled can be restored to an arbitrary point in time within the backup retention window. Because the attack started 3 days ago at 2:25 PM and the retention period is 28 days, the cluster can be restored to a clean point immediately before the incident, such as 3 days ago at 2:24 PM. AWS documents that point-in-time restore is supported up to the configured retention period. This is the most precise recovery method because it restores to the last known good time rather than forcing the company to rely on the timing of a snapshot. Snapshot-based recovery can leave a larger data loss window if the nearest snapshot was taken well before the attack. Point-in-time restore directly matches the requirement to recover the cluster to the last known good version. Using the source DB cluster identifier for a restore to a specific timestamp is the native recovery path for an RDS or Aurora-style DB cluster in this situation. It minimizes unnecessary data loss and is exactly what automated backups are intended to support.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/USER_PIT.html Incorrect Options:
  • Identify the source DB cluster, review the available DB cluster snapshots, and restore the snapshot whose creation time is closest to 3 days ago at 2:24 PM — Restoring from the snapshot whose timestamp is closest to 3 days ago at 2:24 PM is less accurate than a point-in-time restore. Snapshots are discrete recovery points, while point-in-time restore can recover to a specific timestamp within the automated backup window. If the nearest snapshot is earlier than the desired time, the company loses more data than necessary. This option also ignores the fact that the cluster already has automated backups enabled specifically to support more granular recovery. Since the requirement is to restore to the last known good version just before the attack, PITR is the better and more precise choice.
  • Review snapshots for all Amazon RDS databases in the account, choose the snapshot whose timestamp is closest to 3 days ago at 2:24 PM, and restore that snapshot — Listing snapshots for all RDS databases in the company is unnecessarily broad and increases the chance of restoring the wrong database. The engineer already knows which DB cluster must be recovered, so recovery should target that specific cluster rather than searching across unrelated databases. It also still relies on snapshots instead of point-in-time restore. Even if the correct snapshot were selected, the result would usually be less precise than restoring the exact cluster to 2:24 PM, one minute before the attack began.
  • Restore the latest automated backup for the DB cluster, and then use database transaction logs inside the restored cluster to manually reverse changes that occurred after 3 days ago at 2:25 PM — This option is incorrect because Amazon RDS already provides point-in-time restore for a DB cluster that has automated backups enabled. The requirement is to recover the database to the last known good time, which is 3 days ago at 2:24 PM, not to restore some later recovery point and then manually reverse changes. A manual rollback approach is more error-prone and unnecessary when RDS can restore the cluster directly to the exact timestamp just before the attack began.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/UserGuide/USER_PIT.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/rds/restore-db-cluster-to-point-in-time.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/APIReference/API_RestoreDBClusterToPointInTime.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/AuroraUserGuide/Aurora.Managing.Backups.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/AuroraUserGuide/aurora-restore-snapshot.html

questions.question_text:
A security engineer is designing security controls for a fleet of 500 EC2 instances running a custom web application in a VPC. The organization requires the ability to continuously identify software vulnerabilities across the entire fleet and automatically remediate discovered issues to maintain compliance with industry standards.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend to meet these requirements?
options: Use Amazon GuardDuty Malware Protection for EC2 to scan the instances for software vulnerabilities, and use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager to deploy fixes
Install the Amazon CloudWatch agent on the EC2 instances, enable detailed logging, and use Amazon EventBridge rules to inspect software logs for suspicious patterns
Use Amazon Inspector to perform vulnerability discovery, and use AWS Systems Manager Run Command to execute ad hoc patching scripts on each EC2 instance instead of using a managed patching service
Use Amazon Inspector to identify software vulnerabilities on the EC2 instances, and use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager to deploy security patches and operating system updates
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use Amazon Inspector to identify software vulnerabilities on the EC2 instances, and use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager to deploy security patches and operating system updates
Amazon Inspector is the AWS service built for vulnerability management on Amazon EC2. AWS documents that Inspector continually scans EC2 instances for package vulnerabilities and unintended network exposure, and produces findings when it detects software vulnerabilities. That directly satisfies the detection part of the requirement. AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager is the native AWS service for patch remediation on managed nodes. AWS documents that Patch Manager automates the process of patching managed nodes with security-related and other updates. Using Inspector for discovery and Patch Manager for remediation creates the cleanest managed solution for detecting and mitigating software vulnerabilities on the EC2 fleet. This combination also has the lowest ongoing operational burden among the choices because it uses AWS-native managed services for both phases of the workflow. The engineer gets continuous vulnerability findings and an established patching mechanism instead of building custom detection or update logic. Incorrect Options:
  • Use Amazon Inspector to perform vulnerability discovery, and use AWS Systems Manager Run Command to execute ad hoc patching scripts on each EC2 instance instead of using a managed patching service — Amazon Inspector is the correct service for vulnerability detection, so this option is close. However, replacing Patch Manager with ad hoc Run Command patch scripts increases administrative effort and reduces consistency compared with using the managed patching workflow that Systems Manager Patch Manager already provides. The requirement is to implement a solution for both detection and mitigation. Patch Manager is the more appropriate remediation service because it is designed specifically for patch management at scale rather than one-off command execution.
  • Install the Amazon CloudWatch agent on the EC2 instances, enable detailed logging, and use Amazon EventBridge rules to inspect software logs for suspicious patterns — The CloudWatch agent and detailed logging can help with observability and anomaly detection, but they do not perform software vulnerability scanning. Reviewing logs for suspicious patterns is not the same as detecting known package vulnerabilities and exposure issues. EventBridge also is not a vulnerability analysis engine. This option focuses on monitoring behavior rather than identifying and remediating vulnerable software packages on EC2 instances.
  • Use Amazon GuardDuty Malware Protection for EC2 to scan the instances for software vulnerabilities, and use AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager to deploy fixes — GuardDuty Malware Protection for EC2 scans attached EBS volumes to detect the potential presence of malware. AWS documents it as malware detection, not as a service for identifying package vulnerabilities or unintended network exposure on EC2 instances. Patch Manager is valid for applying updates, but the detection half of this option is wrong for the stated requirement. Malware scanning and vulnerability scanning are different security functions, and Amazon Inspector is the service that covers software vulnerabilities.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/inspector/latest/user/scanning-ec2.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/inspector/latest/user/what-is-inspector.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/patch-manager.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/malware-protection.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/on-demand-malware-scan.html

Correct Option:
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Deny", "NotAction": [ ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringNotEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] } A service control policy that uses Effect: "Deny" together with NotAction for the allowed global services and StringNotEquals for aws:RequestedRegion correctly blocks actions outside the approved Region. This design denies every action except the listed global services whenever the request is made for a Region other than us-east-1. This pattern works well with the default FullAWSAccess SCP because SCPs establish a permissions boundary for accounts in the organization. By attaching this SCP to the OU, the company applies the regional restriction centrally to all existing accounts in that OU and to any accounts added later. The policy does not grant permissions. Instead, it filters what principals in the affected accounts can do. The use of NotAction is essential because some AWS services are global and do not operate within a single regional endpoint model. Excluding the approved global services from the deny statement preserves access to those services while preventing use of other services outside us-east-1. This is the standard SCP design for enforcing Region restrictions with exceptions for global services. Incorrect Options:
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Deny", "NotAction": [ ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] } - This policy uses Effect: "Deny" with NotAction, which is directionally close to the right structure. However, it uses StringEquals for aws:RequestedRegion and therefore denies requests when the requested Region is us-east-1. That behavior is the opposite of the requirement. The company wants to allow usage in us-east-1 and deny usage outside that Region except for designated global services. Because the condition logic is reversed, this policy blocks the approved Region rather than protecting it. { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] } - SCPs do not work like ordinary IAM identity policies for granting access. Even though Allow statements can exist in SCP evaluation, the common and correct way to enforce organizational guardrails with the default FullAWSAccess policy is to use explicit deny logic to block what should not be allowed. This policy also uses Action instead of NotAction, which means it only references the listed global services rather than exempting them from a broader deny. As written, it does not create the required regional boundary for all other AWS service usage. { { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "RestrictOutsideApprovedRegion", "Effect": "Deny", "Action": [ ], "Resource": "*", "Condition": { "StringNotEquals": { "aws:RequestedRegion": [ "us-east-1" ] } } } ] } - This policy is incorrect because it applies the deny only to the listed global services instead of restricting all other AWS services. The requirement is to allow global services everywhere while limiting all other service usage to us-east-1. By using Action instead of NotAction, the policy ends up denying only the specified global services outside us-east-1, while allowing all regional services such as EC2 and RDS to operate in any Region. This fails to enforce the intended Region restriction. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/organizations/latest/userguide/orgs_manage_policies_scps.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_condition-keys.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_elements_notaction.html

questions.question_text:
A company provides external compliance reviewers with cross-account IAM roles to conduct a security audit across multiple AWS accounts. Each reviewer's team was given specific role ARNs to assume in the target accounts. Several reviewers report that they cannot assume the designated roles in certain accounts, even though they believe access has been configured for them.
Which THREE of the following are the most likely causes of the access failures? (Select three)
options: The reviewer entered the wrong password when authenticating to the AWS Management Console of the source AWS account
The role ARN that the reviewer supplies in the sts:AssumeRole call contains an error such as a misspelled account ID, partition, or role name, or the role does not exist in the target account
The trust policy of the cross-account role in the target account specifies an external ID condition, but the reviewer's sts:AssumeRole call does not supply a matching external ID value

The reviewer's source AWS account does not hold AWS Organizations membership, and the trust policy of the target cross-account role includes an aws:PrincipalOrgID condition that restricts assumption to principals from within a specific organization
The trust policy of the cross-account role in the target account does not include an entry that grants the reviewer's IAM principal permission to perform the sts:AssumeRole action
The AWS secret access key stored in the reviewer's local credentials file contains a transcription error or has been deactivated in IAM
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • The trust policy of the cross-account role in the target account specifies an external ID condition, but the reviewer's sts:AssumeRole call does not supply a matching external ID value
AWS supports an external ID condition in role trust policies to address the confused deputy problem. When a trust policy includes a Condition block with sts:ExternalId, any caller that tries to assume the role must supply the exact same external ID value in the sts:AssumeRole request. If the reviewer does not supply the external ID or supplies an incorrect value, the sts:AssumeRole call fails with an AccessDenied error. This is a common oversight when cross-account roles include external ID conditions that were not communicated to the reviewing team.
  • The trust policy of the cross-account role in the target account does not include an entry that grants the reviewer's IAM principal permission to perform the sts:AssumeRole action
    • Cross-account role assumption requires the trust policy of the destination role to explicitly list the reviewer's principal as a trusted entity with the sts:AssumeRole action. If the trust policy is missing the reviewer's principal entirely, or lists the wrong account ID or role ARN for the reviewer, the sts:AssumeRole call fails. The destination account administrator must add the correct source principal to the trust policy.
  • The role ARN that the reviewer supplies in the sts:AssumeRole call contains an error such as a misspelled account ID, partition, or role name, or the role does not exist in the target account
    • The sts:AssumeRole API requires a precisely formatted role ARN in the format arn:aws:iam::AccountId:role/RoleName. A single character error in the account ID, role name, or partition results in a failure because the role cannot be found in the target account. Similarly, if the administrator created the role in a different account than intended, the ARN the reviewer uses points to a non-existent role. Verifying the full role ARN is an essential first step in troubleshooting AssumeRole failures. Incorrect Options:
    • The reviewer entered the wrong password when authenticating to the AWS Management Console of the source AWS account — Cross-account role assumption through the CLI or SDK uses access keys or an existing session token, not a console password. A password is only relevant for IAM user console login. If the reviewer calls sts:AssumeRole programmatically, a password is not involved in that API call at all. Even if the reviewer uses the console switch-role feature, a successful console session must already be established before a role switch can be attempted, so the password for the source account login is not the cause of the AssumeRole failure.
    A wrong password prevents initial console authentication to the source account but does not cause an AssumeRole failure once a session is active. These are two distinct authentication events.
  • The reviewer's source AWS account does not hold AWS Organizations membership, and the trust policy of the target cross-account role includes an aws:PrincipalOrgID condition that restricts assumption to principals from within a specific organization — This applies only when an administrator has explicitly added an aws:PrincipalOrgID condition to the trust policy. Cross-account role assumption does not require Organizations membership by default. If the trust policy does not include an organizational condition, any correctly identified principal from any AWS account can assume the role. This option describes an edge case that is only relevant when an administrator has deliberately restricted the role to organization members, not a common misconfiguration for externally shared audit roles.
  • The AWS secret access key stored in the reviewer's local credentials file contains a transcription error or has been deactivated in IAM — An invalid or inactive secret access key prevents the reviewer from making any authenticated AWS API call, not just sts:AssumeRole. The error would be an authentication failure such as InvalidClientTokenId or AuthFailure, not a role-assumption-specific error. A deactivated access key is a plausible cause of API call failure, but it would prevent all API calls uniformly, not just AssumeRole for specific accounts. The symptom described (inability to access certain accounts) is more consistent with role-specific configuration issues.
    • https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_use_permissions-to-switch.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/troubleshoot_roles.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/STS/latest/APIReference/API_AssumeRole.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_common-scenarios_third-party.html

questions.question_text:
A global enterprise runs all its cryptographic workloads through an AWS CloudHSM cluster that resides in a shared services AWS account managed by the central security team. A newly formed product team has provisioned a dedicated AWS account. The product team's applications must use the CloudHSM cluster in the shared services account for key management and cryptographic operations.
How should a security engineer enable the new dedicated account to use the CloudHSM cluster hosted in the shared services account?
options: Use AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) to share the CloudHSM cluster resource ID directly with the new dedicated account. After sharing is established, create a CloudHSM cluster client configuration in the new dedicated account using the shared cluster resource ID. Update the CloudHSM cluster security group to allow inbound CloudHSM client traffic on the management ports from the private IP addresses of all client instances that the new dedicated account will register with the cluster

Use AWS IAM Identity Center to generate an AWS STS token that authenticates from the new dedicated account to the shared services account. Configure the STS token's cross-account permissions to allow the new account to invoke operations on the CloudHSM cluster in the shared services account

Deploy an AWS PrivateLink endpoint service in the shared services account that exposes the CloudHSM cluster. Create an interface VPC endpoint in the new dedicated account's VPC and configure the CloudHSM client software to connect through the PrivateLink endpoint

Use AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) to share the VPC subnet ID that hosts the CloudHSM cluster in the shared services account with the new dedicated account. Update the CloudHSM cluster security group to permit inbound traffic from the private IP addresses of client EC2 instances running in the new dedicated account
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) to share the VPC subnet ID that hosts the CloudHSM cluster in the shared services account with the new dedicated account. Update the CloudHSM cluster security group to permit inbound traffic from the private IP addresses of client EC2 instances running in the new dedicated account
AWS CloudHSM is a network-attached hardware security module. The CloudHSM client software running on EC2 instances communicates with the HSM over a private network connection within a VPC using the CloudHSM proprietary protocol. To allow EC2 instances in a different AWS account to reach the HSM, those instances must be able to connect to the HSM's private IP address in the shared services account VPC. AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) enables resource sharing across accounts, and one of its supported shareable resource types is VPC subnets. By sharing the subnet where the CloudHSM cluster resides, the new dedicated account can launch EC2 instances directly into that shared subnet. Those instances occupy the same network segment as the HSM and can communicate with it over private IP addresses. The CloudHSM cluster security group must be updated to allow inbound connections from the private IP addresses of the client instances, enabling the CloudHSM client-to-HSM communication on the required management ports. This is the documented and recommended AWS approach for sharing a CloudHSM cluster across multiple accounts. The security group control restricts access to specific client instance IP addresses rather than broad IP ranges, maintaining network-level least privilege. The shared subnet model requires no additional gateway components, VPC peering, or AWS PrivateLink configurations, making it operationally straightforward and consistent with AWS architecture patterns.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ram/latest/userguide/shareable.html Incorrect Options:
  • Deploy an AWS PrivateLink endpoint service in the shared services account that exposes the CloudHSM cluster. Create an interface VPC endpoint in the new dedicated account's VPC and configure the CloudHSM client software to connect through the PrivateLink endpoint — AWS PrivateLink provides private connectivity between VPCs without using internet gateways or VPC peering. However, CloudHSM is not a service that supports being exposed through a PrivateLink endpoint service. CloudHSM clusters communicate using a proprietary protocol to the HSM's private IP address directly, and this communication path is not compatible with PrivateLink's model of service endpoints backed by Network Load Balancers. There is no supported mechanism to wrap a CloudHSM cluster in a PrivateLink endpoint service.
This distractor describes an architecture that does not exist for CloudHSM in practice. The correct cross-account mechanism is RAM subnet sharing, not PrivateLink.
  • Use AWS IAM Identity Center to generate an AWS STS token that authenticates from the new dedicated account to the shared services account. Configure the STS token's cross-account permissions to allow the new account to invoke operations on the CloudHSM cluster in the shared services account — IAM Identity Center and STS tokens govern access to the AWS management API plane, not to the CloudHSM hardware data plane. The CloudHSM client software establishes a direct network socket connection to the HSM using the CloudHSM proprietary protocol. This communication does not use IAM authentication, STS temporary credentials, or any AWS service API endpoint. STS tokens cannot provide or substitute for the network connectivity required for CloudHSM client-to-HSM communication.
    • This option conflates management plane authentication with data plane network connectivity, which are two independent layers that operate differently for CloudHSM.
  • Use AWS Resource Access Manager (AWS RAM) to share the CloudHSM cluster resource ID directly with the new dedicated account. After sharing is established, create a CloudHSM cluster client configuration in the new dedicated account using the shared cluster resource ID. Update the CloudHSM cluster security group to allow inbound CloudHSM client traffic on the management ports from the private IP addresses of all client instances that the new dedicated account will register with the cluster — AWS RAM does not support sharing CloudHSM cluster resources by cluster ID. The shareable resource types in AWS RAM for enabling cross-account CloudHSM access are VPC subnets, not CloudHSM cluster objects. Attempting to share a CloudHSM cluster resource ID through AWS RAM will fail because CloudHSM clusters are not in the list of resource types that AWS RAM supports for sharing. The cluster ID cannot be shared as a discrete resource through this mechanism.
    • This option correctly identifies the security group update requirement but incorrectly identifies what the RAM sharing mechanism operates on. Sharing the subnet (not the cluster) is what enables the new account's instances to join the same network and reach the HSM over private IP addresses. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cloudhsm/latest/userguide/configure-sg.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ram/latest/userguide/what-is.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ram/latest/userguide/shareable.html

questions.question_text:
A telecommunications provider has implemented a strict security policy requiring that all data exchanged between the company's on-premises data center and AWS KMS remain within the AWS backbone network at all times. Direct public internet connections between the data center and KMS endpoints are prohibited under the policy. The company uses VPC endpoints to establish private connectivity from its VPC to other AWS services.
Which two actions together BEST accomplish this security requirement? (Select two)
options: Add a condition to the KMS key policy that restricts decryption operations only to requests arriving through the company's designated VPC endpoint using the aws:sourceVpce resource restriction
Create a VPC endpoint for the AWS KMS service with private DNS enabled so that all DNS queries for the KMS endpoint resolve to the VPC endpoint interface rather than public KMS IP addresses
Create an interface VPC endpoint for AWS KMS, but leave private DNS disabled and update all applications to continue using the default public AWS KMS endpoint names
Modify the KMS key policy to restrict API calls based on source IP address using the condition aws:SourceIp with a CIDR block matching the on-premises network range
Use the AWS KMS import key feature to transport the KMS master key to on-premises through an encrypted VPN tunnel, storing a copy locally to avoid public service endpoint access
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Add a condition to the KMS key policy that restricts decryption operations only to requests arriving through the company's designated VPC endpoint using the aws:sourceVpce resource restriction
The aws:sourceVpce condition in the KMS key policy then creates a second layer of control by verifying that every decryption operation comes through the specific VPC endpoint ID that the company has designated. Even if an application or credential is leaked and used from outside the VPC, the KMS key policy rejects any decryption attempts that do not originate from the approved VPC endpoint. The combination of a private DNS endpoint plus a key policy condition ensures that both the network path and the policy layer enforce the requirement that communications stay within AWS backbone infrastructure.
  • Create a VPC endpoint for the AWS KMS service with private DNS enabled so that all DNS queries for the KMS endpoint resolve to the VPC endpoint interface rather than public KMS IP addresses
    • A VPC endpoint for AWS KMS with private DNS enabled creates a private network path that intercepts all DNS queries for the KMS service and redirects them to a private endpoint interface within the VPC instead of public KMS IP addresses on the internet. From the application's perspective, DNS resolution still points to the KMS service, but traffic flows through the private endpoint gateway instead of the public internet. This eliminates the requirement for applications to route through a NAT gateway or internet gateway to reach KMS. Together, these two controls satisfy the security policy requirement: private DNS redirects KMS traffic away from public endpoints, and the VPC endpoint condition in the key policy verifies the source before granting access. Incorrect Options:
    • Create an interface VPC endpoint for AWS KMS, but leave private DNS disabled and update all applications to continue using the default public AWS KMS endpoint names — Creating an interface VPC endpoint for KMS is directionally correct, but leaving private DNS disabled while continuing to use the default public KMS endpoint names defeats the purpose of the setup. Without private DNS, the standard service hostname does not automatically resolve to the interface endpoint inside the VPC.
    • Use the AWS KMS import key feature to transport the KMS master key to on-premises through an encrypted VPN tunnel, storing a copy locally to avoid public service endpoint access — AWS KMS customer managed keys cannot be exported, and the AWS KMS import key feature is used to import externally generated keys into KMS, not to export KMS-managed keys to on-premises. This option misrepresents KMS functionality and proposes a configuration that AWS does not support. Storing a local copy of a key on-premises would also eliminate the security benefit of centralized key management that AWS KMS provides.
    • Modify the KMS key policy to restrict API calls based on source IP address using the condition aws:SourceIp with a CIDR block matching the on-premises network range — Restricting the KMS key policy by source IP address does not ensure that traffic stays within AWS backbone infrastructure. An attacker with compromised credentials and network access to the corporate VPN can still place themselves behind the IP address range that the policy allows. IP-based restrictions are network-layer controls that do not prevent routing through public internet paths. The requirement explicitly states that communications must not use public service endpoints, which an IP address condition cannot enforce.
    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/overview.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policy-services.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/key-policies.html

questions.question_text:
A company receives an Amazon GuardDuty finding that indicates potential credential compromise on an Amazon EC2 instance hosting a critical web application. The finding type is UnauthorizedAccess:IAMUser/InstanceCredentialExfiltration.
