Further investigation confirms that API credentials associated with the EC2 instance profile were used from a geographic location where the company does not operate. A security engineer must take immediate action to block the malicious activity.
What should the security engineer do first to mitigate the threat?
options: Install the Amazon Inspector agent on the EC2 instance and run a vulnerability assessment using the CVE rules package
Install and configure the AWS Systems Manager Agent on the EC2 instance and generate an inventory report to assess the system state
Revoke all active IAM role sessions associated with the EC2 instance profile to immediately invalidate the compromised temporary credentials
Create an AWS WAF rule to block requests originating from the suspicious geographic location and associate the rule with the application endpoint
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Revoke all active IAM role sessions associated with the EC2 instance profile to immediately invalidate the compromised temporary credentials
Revoking active IAM role sessions is the fastest and most direct way to invalidate compromised temporary credentials. When instance profile credentials are exfiltrated, attackers can use the temporary access keys to perform API actions until the session expires. Revoking the session immediately cuts off the attacker’s ability to continue using those credentials. This action aligns with incident response best practices, where containment is the first priority. By invalidating the active sessions, the security engineer prevents further unauthorized API calls without requiring changes to infrastructure or application availability. This step is also minimally disruptive and can be executed quickly. After containment, the security engineer can proceed with deeper investigation, rotation of credentials, and hardening of the environment. Incorrect Options:
  • Create an AWS WAF rule to block requests originating from the suspicious geographic location and associate the rule with the application endpoint — Blocking traffic by geographic location using AWS WAF applies only to web traffic that flows through services integrated with WAF, such as Application Load Balancers or CloudFront. The attack involves API calls made using stolen credentials, which are not mitigated by WAF rules. Therefore, this option does not address the actual threat vector.
  • Install and configure the AWS Systems Manager Agent on the EC2 instance and generate an inventory report to assess the system state — Installing and using AWS Systems Manager to collect inventory data can help with post-incident investigation and system visibility. However, it does not provide immediate containment of the threat. The attacker can continue to use the compromised credentials while the inventory process runs, which makes this option inappropriate as the first step.
  • Install the Amazon Inspector agent on the EC2 instance and run a vulnerability assessment using the CVE rules package — Running Amazon Inspector to identify vulnerabilities is useful for improving the security posture of the instance over time. This option focuses on vulnerability assessment rather than incident containment. It does not stop the active misuse of stolen credentials and therefore does not meet the requirement.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_use_revoke-sessions.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_credentials_temp_control-access_disable-perms.html

questions.question_text:
A security operations team at a healthcare company continuously monitors AWS resource configurations to verify compliance with internal data protection policies. The team is concerned about scenarios where a regulated resource receives multiple configuration changes within a short time window. For each compliance evaluation, the team needs to assess only the configuration snapshot that represents the final state after all successive changes have been applied.
Which solution meets this requirement in the most operationally efficient way?
options: Use AWS Config to automatically detect resource configuration changes and record the most recent configuration snapshot for each resource, enabling compliance evaluations against the final cumulative state after successive changes

Enable AWS CloudTrail to capture all configuration change API calls for the resource, and write a custom script that processes the CloudTrail log stream and filters for the most recent API call to determine the cumulative resource configuration state

Deploy an AWS Lambda function triggered by Amazon EventBridge events for each resource configuration change API call, persist the latest configuration snapshot in an Amazon DynamoDB table keyed by resource ID, and query the table during compliance evaluations to retrieve the most current recorded state

Configure AWS Security Hub custom insights to detect and aggregate resource change events, use the Security Hub Findings API to retrieve the most recently generated finding for each resource, and map finding attributes to the current resource configuration for compliance evaluation
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use AWS Config to automatically detect resource configuration changes and record the most recent configuration snapshot for each resource, enabling compliance evaluations against the final cumulative state after successive changes
AWS Config is a managed service that continuously records the configuration state of AWS resources and stores a timestamped configuration item for each detected change. When multiple configuration changes occur in rapid succession, AWS Config captures each intermediate state and always retains the most recent configuration snapshot per resource. This enables compliance evaluations to assess the final cumulative configuration without requiring any custom filtering, log parsing, or event ordering logic. The Configuration History feature in AWS Config records every change for a resource and timestamps each configuration item in sequence. AWS Config managed rules and conformance packs evaluate compliance automatically against the latest configuration item, which reflects the net effect of all changes applied to the resource. This eliminates the need to build custom logic to determine which of several rapid changes represents the definitive current state. AWS Config requires no custom code, additional compute infrastructure, or streaming data pipeline. Enabling the AWS Config recorder for the relevant resource types and attaching the appropriate compliance rules provides immediate configuration tracking and evaluation capability as a fully managed service, making it the most operationally efficient solution. Incorrect Options:
  • Enable AWS CloudTrail to capture all configuration change API calls for the resource, and write a custom script that processes the CloudTrail log stream and filters for the most recent API call to determine the cumulative resource configuration state — AWS CloudTrail records API calls and their request parameters, but it does not maintain a structured configuration snapshot per resource. Parsing CloudTrail logs to reconstruct the current configuration requires custom logic to identify the most recent mutating API call for each configuration attribute, order events by timestamp, and apply changes cumulatively. This approach involves significant development work and ongoing maintenance. CloudTrail logs are also delivered to Amazon S3 with a typical delay of up to 15 minutes, making real-time compliance evaluation against current configuration impractical through this method.
The output of CloudTrail parsing would be a best-effort reconstruction rather than the authoritative configuration snapshot that AWS Config produces through direct resource polling and change notification integration.
  • Deploy an AWS Lambda function triggered by Amazon EventBridge events for each resource configuration change API call, persist the latest configuration snapshot in an Amazon DynamoDB table keyed by resource ID, and query the table during compliance evaluations to retrieve the most current recorded state — Building a custom Lambda-based configuration tracking pipeline using EventBridge, Lambda, and DynamoDB is technically feasible but adds substantial operational overhead compared to using AWS Config as a managed service. This approach requires writing and maintaining Lambda function code to capture resource configuration state from the relevant AWS APIs, designing a DynamoDB schema that accommodates all configuration attributes across resource types, managing Lambda execution errors and retries, and configuring EventBridge rules for each resource type and change event. AWS Config handles all of this automatically as a managed service with no custom code required.
    • The custom pipeline must also be updated whenever new resource types are added to the monitored environment or when AWS changes the API structure for existing resource types, introducing ongoing maintenance costs that do not exist with AWS Config.
  • Configure AWS Security Hub custom insights to detect and aggregate resource change events, use the Security Hub Findings API to retrieve the most recently generated finding for each resource, and map finding attributes to the current resource configuration for compliance evaluation — AWS Security Hub aggregates security findings generated by integrated services in response to specific security events or policy violations. Security Hub findings represent security-relevant assessments such as unencrypted storage, open security groups, or failed compliance checks. They do not contain comprehensive resource configuration snapshots covering all configuration attributes. Mapping Security Hub findings to the current configuration state of a resource is imprecise because findings are generated only when specific conditions are met, not after every configuration change.
    • Security Hub custom insights are designed for filtering and visualizing aggregated findings data, not for real-time configuration change tracking. This approach cannot guarantee that the latest configuration state is represented in Security Hub findings after every rapid successive change. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/how-does-config-work.html https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/mt/aws-config-best-practices/ https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/config-concepts.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/config/latest/developerguide/select-resources.html

questions.question_text:
A company is designing a web application that serves external users through multiple microservices hosted on Amazon Elastic Container Service (Amazon ECS). The architecture must authenticate users securely while issuing temporary credentials and minimizing the operational burden of managing user identities. The company wants a centralized authentication mechanism that avoids maintaining custom credential stores and ensures that all requests to backend microservices are validated before processing.
Select and arrange the necessary steps from the list below to build a secure authentication strategy. You may use each step one time or not at all:
1. Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication
2. Set up IAM roles for each microservice and assign permissions
3. Deploy an Amazon API Gateway HTTP API with Lambda authorizers to validate tokens before forwarding requests to microservices
4. Configure AWS IAM Identity Center to provide external users with access to the microservices
5. Create an Amazon DynamoDB table to store user credentials for each microservice
6. Configure an Amazon Cognito app client to enable the web application to interact with the user pool
Which sequence of steps should the security engineer implement to meet these requirements?
options: Step 1: Configure AWS IAM Identity Center to provide external users with access to the microservices -> Step 2: Set up IAM roles for each microservice and assign permissions -> Step 3: Deploy an Amazon API Gateway HTTP API with Lambda authorizers to validate tokens before forwarding requests to microservices

Step 1: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication -> Step 2: Set up IAM roles for each microservice and assign permissions -> Step 3: Configure AWS IAM Identity Center to provide external users with access to the microservices

Step 1: Create an Amazon DynamoDB table to store user credentials for each microservice -> Step 2: Configure an Amazon Cognito app client to enable the web application to interact with the user pool -> Step 3: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication

Step 1: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication -> Step 2: Configure an Amazon Cognito app client to enable the web application to interact with the user pool -> Step 3: Deploy an Amazon API Gateway HTTP API with Lambda authorizers to validate tokens before forwarding requests to microservices
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Step 1: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication -> Step 2: Configure an Amazon Cognito app client to enable the web application to interact with the user pool -> Step 3: Deploy an Amazon API Gateway HTTP API with Lambda authorizers to validate tokens before forwarding requests to microservices
Amazon Cognito user pools provide a fully managed authentication service that handles user sign-in, identity management, and token issuance. This removes the need to build and maintain a custom credential store and supports scalable authentication for external users. An example authentication session using Cognito User Pools:
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/authentication.html Configuring a Cognito application client allows the web application to securely interact with the user pool and obtain authentication tokens. These tokens are used to represent the user identity in subsequent requests to backend services. Using Amazon API Gateway with Lambda authorizers ensures that all incoming requests are validated before reaching the ECS microservices. This centralizes authentication, enforces consistent access control, and enables a scalable and secure architecture.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/apigateway-use-lambda-authorizer.html Incorrect Options:
  • Step 1: Configure AWS IAM Identity Center to provide external users with access to the microservices -> Step 2: Set up IAM roles for each microservice and assign permissions -> Step 3: Deploy an Amazon API Gateway HTTP API with Lambda authorizers to validate tokens before forwarding requests to microservices — AWS IAM Identity Center is designed for workforce identity use cases and is not intended for authenticating external application users. It does not integrate with web application authentication flows in the same way as Cognito.
Although API Gateway with Lambda authorizers is correctly included, the absence of Cognito user pools prevents proper token-based authentication. This makes the solution unsuitable for the given requirements.
  • Step 1: Create an Amazon DynamoDB table to store user credentials for each microservice -> Step 2: Configure an Amazon Cognito app client to enable the web application to interact with the user pool -> Step 3: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication — Storing user credentials in DynamoDB introduces unnecessary operational overhead and requires building a custom authentication system. This contradicts the requirement to minimize management effort.
    • Additionally, the sequence is incorrect because it attempts to configure an application client before establishing the user pool. The steps do not form a valid authentication workflow.
  • Step 1: Set up Amazon Cognito user pools to manage user sign-in and authentication -> Step 2: Set up IAM roles for each microservice and assign permissions -> Step 3: Configure AWS IAM Identity Center to provide external users with access to the microservices — While Cognito user pools are correctly included, this option replaces token-based authentication with IAM Identity Center for user access, which is not appropriate for external users.
    • IAM roles for microservices are useful for service-level permissions but do not address user authentication. This sequence does not establish a proper authentication and authorization flow for the application. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/authentication.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/cognito-user-pools.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/apigateway-use-lambda-authorizer.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/singlesignon/latest/userguide/what-is.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles.html

questions.question_text:
A technology startup operates a single AWS account and it is now expanding its AWS infrastructure across multiple geographies. The startup previously deployed AWS CloudTrail in a single Region with logs being delivered to an Amazon S3 bucket through the AWS CLI. Management has now authorized expansion to three additional Regions globally. After the expansion, the security team observes that CloudTrail logs from the newly provisioned Regions are not being delivered to the existing S3 bucket. The team needs to restore log delivery from all Regions while reducing manual reconfiguration.
What is the optimal solution?
options: Update the existing CloudTrail trail to enable multi-Region logging so all Regions deliver logs to the same bucket
Establish a new organization-wide CloudTrail trail in AWS Organizations that automatically covers all enabled Regions
Deploy AWS Config in each new Region to centralize CloudTrail log aggregation and route logs through an Amazon Kinesis Data Stream to the S3 bucket
Create separate CloudTrail trails for each of the three newly added Regions and manually configure each trail to point to the same centralized S3 bucket
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Update the existing CloudTrail trail to enable multi-Region logging so all Regions deliver logs to the same bucket
AWS CloudTrail supports a multi-Region mode that, when enabled on an existing trail, automatically captures API activity from all Regions and delivers those logs to a single S3 bucket. This setting eliminates the need to create and maintain separate trails for each Region. When the startup enables multi-Region mode on the existing trail, CloudTrail immediately begins collecting logs from the three newly added Regions and appending them to the existing delivery path. The multi-Region setting is a single toggle that applies organization-wide, requiring no per-Region configuration or additional trails. This approach leverages the trail already in place, which minimizes change risk and maintains the existing S3 bucket, log encryption, and retention policies without modification. The startup can enable multi-Region logging through the AWS Console or AWS CLI in minutes, and log delivery from the new Regions begins immediately without waiting for new trail creation or propagation delays. This is the least operationally burdensome solution because it changes only one attribute on one resource.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-create-and-update-a-trail-by-using-the-aws-cli-update-trail.html Incorrect Options:
  • Create separate CloudTrail trails for each of the three newly added Regions and manually configure each trail to point to the same centralized S3 bucket — Creating separate trails for each Region requires manually configuring each trail, specifying the destination S3 bucket for each, and managing authentication and encryption settings independently. This approach multiplies the configuration complexity and creates more resources to monitor and maintain. Separate region-specific trails also produce logs with different organizational structures in the S3 bucket, making consolidated analysis more difficult than a single multi-Region trail.
  • Update the existing CloudTrail trail to enable multi-Region logging so all Regions deliver logs to the same bucket — AWS Config is a resource compliance and configuration tracking service, not a log aggregation service. Config does not control CloudTrail log delivery. Adding Config does not solve the immediate problem and introduces unnecessary service dependencies and monitoring overhead. Kinesis Data Streams would require additional data processing pipeline code and ongoing infrastructure management, all of which are more complex than enabling an existing trail's multi-Region capability.
  • Establish a new organization-wide CloudTrail trail in AWS Organizations that automatically covers all enabled Regions — AWS Organizations CloudTrail trails are valuable for multi-account visibility, but the startup operates a single account and this option creates an entirely new trail. If the startup later onboards additional member accounts, an organization trail would be beneficial, but for a single-account scenario with multiple Regions, updating the existing trail to multi-Region mode is simpler than creating a new organization trail.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-user-guide.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-create-and-update-a-trail-by-using-the-aws-cli-update-trail.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide/cloudtrail-update-a-trail-console.html

options: "Action": "s3:*",
"Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive"
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:federated-user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } } An Amazon S3 bucket policy can explicitly deny access to a specific principal for actions against a specific bucket. In this case, the principal is a federated user, so the correct principal format uses the AWS STS federated-user ARN. By targeting arn:aws:sts::123456789012:federated-user/Alex and applying an explicit Deny to s3:* on the bucket, the policy prevents Alex from using Amazon S3 permissions against that bucket. This design affects only the permissions evaluated for that bucket because bucket policies are resource-based policies scoped to Amazon S3 resources. Alex can continue to retain any unrelated permissions he has for other AWS services or for other resources that are not covered by this bucket policy. That matches the requirement to preserve Alex's other permissions. The key point is that the policy must identify the correct type of principal. Because Alex is a federated user, the policy must reference the federated-user ARN in STS rather than an IAM user ARN or an assumed-role ARN. An explicit deny in a resource-based policy then overrides any allows that Alex might otherwise have for that bucket. Incorrect Options:
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "NotPrincipal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:federated-user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } } - NotPrincipal with an explicit deny reverses the intended logic. Instead of denying Alex, this statement denies everyone except Alex. That would create a broad and unintended access restriction on the bucket. The requirement is to affect only Alex's S3 permissions for this bucket while leaving all other principals unchanged. This policy does the opposite by making Alex the only principal excluded from the deny, so it clearly fails the requirement. { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/Alex" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } } - This policy uses an IAM user ARN, which does not match the identity type in the scenario. Alex is a federated user, so Amazon S3 evaluates his requests under the STS federated-user principal rather than an IAM user principal. Because the principal ARN is wrong, the deny statement does not reliably target the intended identity. The bucket policy therefore fails to meet the requirement to deny this specific federated user while preserving the rest of his permissions. { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": { "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:sts::123456789012:assumed-role/Alex/session-name" }, "Effect": "Deny", "Action": "s3:*", "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::finance-audit-archive" } } - An assumed-role ARN identifies a role session, not a federated user principal. The scenario states that Alex is a federated user, so using an assumed-role principal does not correctly represent the identity that must be denied. This mismatch matters because resource-based policies depend on the correct principal identity during authorization. If the principal does not match the actual caller type, the deny does not apply as intended, and the policy fails to provide the precise restriction required. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/security_iam_service-with-iam.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/example-bucket-policies.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_identifiers.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_elements_principal.html

questions.question_text:
A company is migrating a legacy application from an on-premises data center to AWS. The application tier will run on AWS, but the backend database must remain in the on-premises environment because of compliance requirements. The application exchanges latency-sensitive traffic with the on-premises database. The company also requires that all traffic between AWS and the on-premises data center be protected with IPsec encryption.
Which combination of AWS solutions should the security engineer recommend to meet these requirements? (Select two)
options: Use AWS Site-to-Site VPN to provide IPsec-encrypted connectivity between AWS and the on-premises data center
Use VPC peering to connect the application VPC in AWS directly to the on-premises data center network
Use AWS Client VPN to provide encrypted remote access from the application server in AWS to the on-premises database network over TLS
Use AWS Direct Connect to provide a dedicated, lower-latency network connection between AWS and the on-premises data center
Use AWS VPN CloudHub to connect the AWS workload and the on-premises database through a hub-and-spoke VPN topology
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use AWS Site-to-Site VPN to provide IPsec-encrypted connectivity between AWS and the on-premises data center
AWS Site-to-Site VPN is the AWS service that provides IPsec-encrypted connectivity between an on-premises environment and AWS. This directly satisfies the requirement that traffic between AWS and the data center must use IPsec encryption. AWS documents that Site-to-Site VPN creates encrypted tunnels between customer networks and AWS. By itself, Site-to-Site VPN provides encryption, but it is not always the best choice for minimizing latency because the traffic typically traverses the public internet. That is why it should be paired with a dedicated connectivity option when latency sensitivity is important. In this scenario, Site-to-Site VPN is the encryption component of the final design. It ensures compliance with the IPsec requirement while complementing a lower-latency transport path.
  • Use AWS Direct Connect to provide a dedicated, lower-latency network connection between AWS and the on-premises data center
    • AWS Direct Connect provides a dedicated network connection from the on-premises data center to AWS. This helps reduce latency and improve the consistency of network performance compared with internet-based connectivity, which makes it a strong fit for a latency-sensitive database dependency. However, Direct Connect by itself does not natively provide IPsec encryption. AWS documents that if encryption is required, customers commonly run Site-to-Site VPN over Direct Connect. This combines the dedicated low-latency connectivity of Direct Connect with the IPsec encryption of Site-to-Site VPN. Together, Direct Connect and Site-to-Site VPN meet both requirements. Direct Connect addresses the network latency concern, and Site-to-Site VPN satisfies the mandatory IPsec encryption requirement.
    via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/aws-vpc-connectivity-options/aws-direct-connect-site-to-site-vpn.html Incorrect Options:
    • Use AWS VPN CloudHub to connect the AWS workload and the on-premises database through a hub-and-spoke VPN topology — AWS VPN CloudHub is designed to provide secure communication between multiple remote sites by using AWS as a VPN hub. It is useful in hub-and-spoke connectivity patterns where multiple branch offices or customer sites need to communicate with each other. This scenario involves a single on-premises database connection requirement rather than a multi-site VPN hub design. VPN CloudHub also does not improve latency in the way a dedicated Direct Connect connection does, so it is not the best fit.
    • Use VPC peering to connect the application VPC in AWS directly to the on-premises data center network — VPC peering connects two VPCs. It cannot connect an AWS VPC directly to an on-premises data center network. Because the database remains on premises, VPC peering does not apply to this requirement. It also does not provide IPsec encryption for hybrid connectivity.
    • Use AWS Client VPN to provide encrypted remote access from the application server in AWS to the on-premises database network over TLS — AWS Client VPN provides secure remote-user access to AWS and on-premises resources. It is intended for end-user or device remote connectivity rather than for application-to-database hybrid network architecture. This does not match the requirement for a production application server connecting to an on-premises database with low latency. It is the wrong service model for this workload.
    https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpn/latest/s2svpn/VPC_VPN.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/directconnect/latest/UserGuide/Welcome.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/whitepapers/latest/aws-vpc-connectivity-options/aws-direct-connect-site-to-site-vpn.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpn/latest/s2svpn/VPN_CloudHub.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/peering/what-is-vpc-peering.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpn/latest/clientvpn-admin/what-is.html

questions.question_text:
A healthcare technology company organizes its AWS workloads across three separate accounts: development, QA, and production. All software engineers can access the development account, but only senior engineers with elevated privileges can access the QA and production accounts. The security operations team spends excessive time managing individual credentials for every engineer in every environment. A cloud security architect must implement a more scalable solution that allows engineers to access the appropriate resources across multiple accounts when their work assignments demand it. The solution must also reduce the overhead of managing individual credentials across environments.
Which solution will meet these requirements?
options: Create IAM roles in the QA account and the production account. Attach trust policies to those roles that permit the sts:AssumeRole action. Create corresponding IAM roles in the development account for senior engineers who require elevated access. Add the development account roles as trusted principals in the QA and production account role trust policies

Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to determine the minimum permissions that engineers need in each account. Configure IAM Access Analyzer to automatically provision cross-account access for each engineer based on its findings

Deploy AWS IAM Identity Center with an external Active Directory connector in the development account. Define permission sets that reflect each engineer's job function, including read-only sets for junior engineers and administrative sets for senior engineers. Propagate these permission sets to all three accounts through the IAM Identity Center console. Configure per-account session duration limits and automatic session revocation for elevated access

Create an Amazon Cognito identity pool that federates engineers from the development account. Configure resource-based policies in the QA and production accounts to allow Cognito-authenticated identities to access resources
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Create IAM roles in the QA account and the production account. Attach trust policies to those roles that permit the sts:AssumeRole action. Create corresponding IAM roles in the development account for senior engineers who require elevated access. Add the development account roles as trusted principals in the QA and production account role trust policies
AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) supports cross-account access through role assumption. When an IAM role in one account (the trusting account) includes a trust policy that references an IAM principal from another account (the trusted account), users or roles in the trusted account can call sts:AssumeRole to temporarily assume the role. This model eliminates the need for long-term credential management by replacing static access keys with short-lived temporary security credentials that AWS STS issues for each session. For this scenario, the architect creates an IAM role in each of the QA and production accounts with a trust policy that lists the development account IAM roles as trusted principals. Senior engineers assume the appropriate role through the AWS Management Console or programmatically through the CLI by calling sts:AssumeRole. This approach centralizes identity management in the development account while enforcing fine-grained access in the target accounts. No credentials need to be distributed because the assumed role provides temporary credentials valid only for the session duration. This design minimizes operational overhead because administrators only manage engineer identities in the development account and control which engineers can assume which cross-account roles. Changes to access are made by updating role trust policies in the target accounts without touching individual engineer credentials. This pattern scales across any number of accounts and is consistent with AWS security best practices for multi-account architectures.
via - https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/tutorial_cross-account-with-roles.html Incorrect Options:
  • Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer to determine the minimum permissions that engineers need in each account. Configure IAM Access Analyzer to automatically provision cross-account access for each engineer based on its findings — AWS IAM Access Analyzer helps identify resources shared with external entities and validates IAM policies against best practices. It can also generate IAM policies based on observed access activity logged in AWS CloudTrail. However, IAM Access Analyzer does not automatically provision cross-account access or create the IAM constructs needed to enable role switching between accounts. Administrators must create and configure those constructs themselves based on Access Analyzer recommendations.
This option conflates a policy analysis tool with an access provisioning system. Access Analyzer addresses policy validation and gap analysis, not the delegation mechanism required for engineers to switch between accounts without managing individual credentials.
  • Deploy AWS IAM Identity Center with an external Active Directory connector in the development account. Define permission sets that reflect each engineer's job function, including read-only sets for junior engineers and administrative sets for senior engineers. Propagate these permission sets to all three accounts through the IAM Identity Center console. Configure per-account session duration limits and automatic session revocation for elevated access — AWS IAM Identity Center is a valid and recommended solution for centralized multi-account access management. However, this option introduces an additional Active Directory connector dependency and implies managing permission sets for every engineer individually through a centralized portal. While IAM Identity Center does use temporary credentials internally, the option does not describe the explicit trust relationship mechanism between accounts that is at the core of the recommended solution. Managing granular permission sets per engineer across all accounts can also become operationally complex at scale.
    • This option is a distractor because IAM Identity Center does support multi-account access. However, it does not reflect the most direct and scalable answer to the stated requirement, which is the explicit cross-account trust relationship approach using IAM role assumption.
  • Create an Amazon Cognito identity pool that federates engineers from the development account. Configure resource-based policies in the QA and production accounts to allow Cognito-authenticated identities to access resources — Amazon Cognito is designed for application-level identity management, primarily for end users of web and mobile applications. It is not the recommended tool for granting developers or engineers cross-account access to the AWS management plane. Configuring Cognito for internal workforce access across AWS accounts introduces unnecessary architectural complexity compared to IAM role assumption with STS.
    • Resource-based policies in the target accounts referencing Cognito-authenticated identities are also difficult to manage at scale and do not align with standard AWS multi-account access patterns. This option introduces architectural complexity without providing a clear benefit over the IAM cross-account role trust policy approach. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/tutorial_cross-account-with-roles.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_roles_use.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/STS/latest/APIReference/API_AssumeRole.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies_access-advisor.html

questions.question_text:
A company runs a third-party software package on a fleet of Amazon EC2 instances and on-premises servers. The operations team occasionally needs shell access to these machines to perform maintenance. Outside of those maintenance windows, the servers must remain isolated from the network and must not accept general inbound administrative access. The company does not want to manage SSH key pairs. The company also wants to minimize cost and pay only for the compute time consumed during maintenance access sessions.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
options: Create a dedicated interface VPC endpoint for each server so administrators can connect privately to the machines only during approved maintenance windows
Deploy a bastion host, configure SSH port forwarding through the bastion host, and route administrators through the bastion host whenever they need shell access to the servers

Install a VPN client on every Amazon EC2 instance and on-premises server, establish on-demand site-to-site tunnels for maintenance windows, and authenticate administrators through temporary local operating system accounts instead of SSH keys
Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager to provide temporary shell access to the servers without managing SSH keys
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager to provide temporary shell access to the servers without managing SSH keys
AWS Systems Manager Session Manager provides interactive shell access to managed instances without requiring inbound SSH access or SSH key management. Administrators can connect through the Systems Manager service, which satisfies the requirement to avoid maintaining SSH key pairs and helps keep the machines isolated from the rest of the network when they are not being accessed. Session Manager also works for both Amazon EC2 instances and on-premises servers that are configured as managed nodes in AWS Systems Manager. This makes it a strong fit for a hybrid environment where the same administrative access model must support resources running in AWS and outside AWS. This approach provides the least operational effort among the available choices for secure temporary access. It removes the need for bastion host administration, avoids direct inbound SSH exposure, and supports controlled maintenance access through a managed service. Incorrect Options:
  • Deploy a bastion host, configure SSH port forwarding through the bastion host, and route administrators through the bastion host whenever they need shell access to the servers — A bastion host can centralize SSH access, but it still relies on SSH and typically requires key management or some other credential management approach. That directly conflicts with the requirement to avoid maintaining SSH keys. A bastion host also introduces additional infrastructure that must remain available, monitored, patched, and secured. The servers would still depend on a network path for SSH access rather than being accessed through a managed session service.
  • Install a VPN client on every Amazon EC2 instance and on-premises server, establish on-demand site-to-site tunnels for maintenance windows, and authenticate administrators through temporary local operating system accounts instead of SSH keys — A VPN-based design can provide private connectivity, but it does not eliminate the operational burden of managing machine-level access controls and temporary local accounts. It also fails the requirement to avoid maintaining SSH-related administrative complexity in practice. This approach is also heavier operationally than necessary. The company wants occasional maintenance access with minimal overhead, and Systems Manager Session Manager already provides that without requiring inbound SSH exposure or additional tunneling infrastructure.
  • Create a dedicated interface VPC endpoint for each server so administrators can connect privately to the machines only during approved maintenance windows — Interface VPC endpoints provide private connectivity from resources in a VPC to supported AWS services. They are not a per-machine remote access mechanism for administrators to connect to EC2 instances or on-premises servers. This option also does not solve the problem of SSH key management or temporary shell access. It focuses on service connectivity rather than administrative session access.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager-getting-started-instance-profile.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/systems-manager-hybrid-multicloud.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/privatelink/concepts.html

questions.question_text:
A company must store all objects that contain sensitive data in an Amazon S3 bucket. The company will use server-side encryption for the bucket. The operations team manages access to the company’s S3 buckets, and the security team manages access to encryption keys. The company wants a separation-of-duties design so that a configuration mistake by only one of these teams cannot result in unauthorized access to plaintext data.
Which solution should the security engineer recommend?
options: Require the operations team to configure default bucket encryption with a customer managed AWS KMS key and also give the operations team permission to administer the KMS key so the bucket and key remain aligned during troubleshooting

Require the operations team to configure default bucket encryption for the bucket by using server-side encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys, and require the security team to manage IAM permissions for the encryption keys

Require the security team to create a customer managed AWS KMS key and configure a key policy that allows any principal in the account to decrypt with the key, and require the operations team to leverage S3 default encryption without a bucket policy that enforces SSE-KMS on object uploads

Require the operations team to create a bucket policy that enforces uploads by using server-side encryption with customer managed AWS KMS keys, and require the security team to manage a key policy that controls access to the KMS keys
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Require the operations team to create a bucket policy that enforces uploads by using server-side encryption with customer managed AWS KMS keys, and require the security team to manage a key policy that controls access to the KMS keys
This design creates the intended separation of duties by splitting control between the S3 bucket policy and the AWS KMS key policy. The operations team controls the bucket policy and can require that object uploads use server-side encryption with customer managed AWS KMS keys. The security team separately controls the key policy that governs who can use the KMS key. This means a mistake by only one team is not enough to expose plaintext data. If the operations team misconfigures S3 access but the security team does not permit use of the KMS key, unauthorized users still cannot decrypt the objects. If the security team allows broad key use but the bucket policy correctly restricts uploads and access patterns, the S3 side still enforces its control boundary. Both layers must align for successful plaintext access. This is the strongest answer because it uses SSE-KMS with a customer managed key, which gives the security team independent control over key usage. It directly satisfies the requirement that a configuration error by only one team must not compromise the data. AWS managed S3 encryption keys do not provide that same independent security-team-controlled policy layer. Incorrect Options:
  • Require the operations team to configure default bucket encryption for the bucket by using server-side encryption with Amazon S3 managed keys, and require the security team to manage IAM permissions for the encryption keys — SSE-S3 uses Amazon S3 managed keys, which means there is no separate customer-controlled key policy for the security team to manage. The option says the security team will create an IAM policy that controls access to the encryption keys, but there are no customer-managed KMS keys in this design for them to control. Because the encryption layer is not independently governed by the security team, this option fails the separation-of-duties requirement. A mistake on the S3 side could be enough to expose access because the key-control plane is not separately owned in the way the question requires.
  • Require the operations team to configure default bucket encryption with a customer managed AWS KMS key and also give the operations team permission to administer the KMS key so the bucket and key remain aligned during troubleshooting — This option uses a customer managed KMS key, which is directionally better than SSE-S3. However, it gives the operations team permission to administer the KMS key as well as the bucket configuration, which breaks the separation-of-duties requirement. If the same team can control both the S3 enforcement layer and the encryption key permissions, then a mistake by that one team could result in unauthorized plaintext access. The question specifically wants to prevent that outcome, so combining those permissions in the operations team is not acceptable.
  • Require the security team to create a customer managed AWS KMS key and configure a key policy that allows any principal in the account to decrypt with the key, and require the operations team to leverage S3 default encryption without a bucket policy that enforces SSE-KMS on object uploads — Creating a customer managed KMS key is part of a good design, but allowing any principal in the account to decrypt with the key defeats the purpose of having the security team control access tightly. Broad decrypt permissions make the key policy far too permissive. This option also relies only on default encryption and does not include a bucket policy that enforces SSE-KMS on object uploads. Default encryption helps, but it is not the same as an explicit enforcement policy combined with a tightly controlled KMS key policy. The design is therefore weaker than the required split-control model.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/concepts.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingKMSEncryption.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingServerSideEncryption.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/bucket-policies.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/default-bucket-encryption.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/control-access.html

questions.question_text:
A company operates a legacy internet-facing application that runs on Amazon EC2 instances. The application distributes traffic by using Amazon Route 53 weighted routing across two instances. The backend connects to an Amazon RDS cluster. The application no longer receives vendor updates. A recent security assessment identified that the application is vulnerable to SQL injection attacks. The security team must implement a mitigation within 24 hours. The solution must require minimal changes to the application and must not disrupt normal operations during deployment.
What should the security engineer do to meet these requirements?
options: Modify the security groups associated with the EC2 instances to block inbound database-related traffic from the internet, create an AWS WAF web ACL with SQL injection protection rules, attempt to associate the web ACL directly with the EC2 instances, and then restore the original security group configuration after testing

Deploy AWS Network Firewall to inspect inbound HTTP traffic for SQL injection patterns, integrate the firewall with the VPC routing configuration, and apply rule groups to filter malicious payloads before they reach the EC2 instances

Deploy an Application Load Balancer in front of the existing EC2 instances, register the instances as targets, associate an AWS WAF web ACL with SQL injection protection rules to the load balancer, validate the mitigation, update Route 53 records to route traffic to the load balancer, and restrict direct internet access to the EC2 instances
Configure Amazon RDS database parameter groups to enforce strict query validation and enable database-level protections against SQL injection attacks without modifying the application layer
questions.explanation:
Correct Option:
  • Deploy an Application Load Balancer in front of the existing EC2 instances, register the instances as targets, associate an AWS WAF web ACL with SQL injection protection rules to the load balancer, validate the mitigation, update Route 53 records to route traffic to the load balancer, and restrict direct internet access to the EC2 instances
Deploying an Application Load Balancer with AWS WAF provides an immediate and effective mitigation against SQL injection attacks without requiring changes to the application code. AWS WAF offers managed rule groups and custom rules that can detect and block common SQL injection patterns at the edge of the application. This approach meets the requirement for minimal effort and rapid deployment. The ALB can be introduced in front of the existing EC2 instances with minimal disruption, and traffic can be gradually shifted by updating Route 53 records after validation. Restricting direct access to the EC2 instances ensures that all incoming traffic is inspected by AWS WAF. This solution also aligns with AWS best practices for protecting legacy applications. It introduces a protective layer that can be deployed quickly while maintaining normal operations and minimizing risk during implementation. Incorrect Options:
  • Configure Amazon RDS database parameter groups to enforce strict query validation and enable database-level protections against SQL injection attacks without modifying the application layer — Amazon RDS parameter groups control database engine configuration but do not provide application-layer protection against SQL injection attacks. SQL injection vulnerabilities must be mitigated either in the application logic or by filtering malicious input before it reaches the application. Database-level configurations alone cannot prevent such attacks.
  • Deploy AWS Network Firewall to inspect inbound HTTP traffic for SQL injection patterns, integrate the firewall with the VPC routing configuration, and apply rule groups to filter malicious payloads before they reach the EC2 instances — AWS Network Firewall operates at the network layer and is not optimized for inspecting application-layer payloads such as HTTP requests for SQL injection patterns. Implementing Network Firewall also requires changes to VPC routing and rule configuration, which increases operational complexity. This does not meet the requirement for least effort and rapid deployment.
  • Modify the security groups associated with the EC2 instances to block inbound database-related traffic from the internet, create an AWS WAF web ACL with SQL injection protection rules, attempt to associate the web ACL directly with the EC2 instances, and then restore the original security group configuration after testing — AWS WAF cannot be directly associated with EC2 instances. It integrates with services such as Application Load Balancer, Amazon CloudFront, and API Gateway. Modifying security groups to block database ports does not prevent SQL injection attacks because these attacks occur at the application layer over HTTP or HTTPS. This option misunderstands both the threat model and the capabilities of AWS WAF.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-rule-statement-type-sqli-match.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf/latest/developerguide/waf-chapter.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/Route53/latest/DeveloperGuide/routing-policy.html https://docs.aws.amazon.com/network-firewall/latest/developerguide/what-is-aws-network-firewall.html
